topics.title: Definitions - Robots, Robotics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEMET722 - ROBOTICS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Definitions - Robots, Robotics # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition of a Robot The **Robot Institute of America (RIA, 1979)** provides the most widely accepted engineering definition that KTU examiners expect in board examinations: > [!IMPORTANT] > **Robot (RIA Definition):** "A robot is a reprogrammable, multifunctional manipulator designed to move material, parts, tools, or specialized devices through variable programmed motions for the performance of a variety of tasks." The **International Organization for Standardization (ISO 8373:2021)** further sharpens this definition to engineering precision: > [!NOTE] > **Robot (ISO 8373:2021):** "An automatically controlled, reprogrammable, multipurpose manipulator, programmable in three or more axes, which can be either fixed in place or mobile for use in industrial automation applications." **Three Mandatory Characteristics of an Industrial Robot (KTU Must-Know):** 1. **Automatic / Programmability** — Operates without continuous human intervention after programming. 2. **Reprogrammability** — Task sequence can be altered without physical modification. 3. **Multi-functionality** — Capable of performing diverse physical tasks across **3 or more axes**. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition for First-Time Learners) Imagine you have a **Swiss Army Knife** — it has a blade, scissors, screwdriver, and bottle opener all rolled into one tool. Each tool performs a different job, but the device itself never changes its physical shape; only its **function changes** based on what task you need. A **robot is the mechanical equivalent of this idea**: - The **mechanical body** (links, joints, grippers) = the physical frame of the Swiss Army Knife. - The **program/software** = the decision about which tool to deploy. - The **sensors** (cameras, force, proximity) = the human eyes and fingers providing "feel." - The **actuators** (motors, hydraulic cylinders) = the muscles that move things. > Just as a Swiss Army Knife can be *repurposed* for many tasks without rebuilding it, a robot can be **reprogrammed** to weld a car in the morning and paint it in the afternoon. --- ## 1.3 Definition of Robotics **Robotics** is the **interdisciplinary branch of engineering and science** that deals with: - The **design**, - **Construction**, - **Operation**, - **Application** of robots, and - The **computational algorithms** required for their intelligent behavior. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Robotics (KTU Board Definition):** "Robotics is the science and technology of designing, manufacturing, and applying robots, integrating mechanics, electronics, computer science, control engineering, and artificial intelligence." ### Distinction Between Key Terms (Frequently Tested in KTU) | Term | Meaning | Scope | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Robot** | The physical, programmable machine itself | Hardware-focused | | **Robotics** | The science & study behind robots | Knowledge-focused | | **Manipulator** | The mechanical arm (subset of a robot) | Mechanical structure only | | **Automation** | Use of systems to perform tasks without human assistance | Broader industrial concept | | **Mechatronics** | Synergistic integration of mechanics, electronics, computing & control | Underlying engineering discipline | # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Asimov's Three Laws of Robotics (Conceptual Foundation) Formulated by **Isaac Asimov (1942)** in his science fiction work *"I, Robot,"* these laws are often asked in KTU exams as conceptual/Part-A questions. > [!NOTE] > **Law 1:** A robot may not injure a human being or, through inaction, allow a human being to come to harm. > > **Law 2:** A robot must obey the orders given by human beings except where such orders conflict with the First Law. > > **Law 3:** A robot must protect its own existence as long as this does not conflict with the First or Second Law. Later, Asimov added the **Zeroth Law**: *"A robot may not harm humanity, or, by inaction, allow humanity to come to harm."* --- ## 2.2 Anatomical Hierarchy of a Robotic System (KTU Must-Draw) A robot system, although mechanically complex, decomposes into **four functional blocks**: 1. **Mechanical Structure (Manipulator)** - Rigid links connected by joints. - Provides physical reach and dexterity. - Joint types: **Revolute (R)** and **Prismatic (P)**. 2. **Actuator Subsystem** - The "muscles" — convert energy into motion. - Three primary types: **Electric (Servo/Stepper motors)**, **Hydraulic**, and **Pneumatic**. 3. **Sensor Subsystem** - The "senses" — gather information about internal state and external environment. - **Internal sensors:** Position encoders, velocity sensors, torque sensors. - **External sensors:** Vision (CCD/CMOS cameras), proximity, tactile, ultrasonic. 4. **Controller / Computer Subsystem** - The "brain" — interprets sensor data and commands actuators. - Implements motion planning, kinematics, and AI-based decision-making. --- ## 2.3 Genesis of Robotics — Timeline of Evolution (High-Yield) | Year | Milestone | Significance | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **1921** | Karel Čapek coins *"Robot"* (Czech: *robota* = forced labor) | Linguistic origin | | **1942** | Asimov proposes *Robotics* term in his short story | Conceptual foundation | | **1954** | George Devol patents **Unimate** | First programmable robot | | **1956** | Devol & Engelberger found **Unimation Inc.** | First commercial robotics firm | | **1961** | Unimate installed at **GM** die-casting plant | First industrial deployment | | **1969** | **Stanford Arm** developed | First electrically driven, computer-controlled arm | | **1973** | **ASEA IRB-6** introduced | First all-electric microprocessor-controlled robot | | **1979** | **SCARA** developed in Japan | Selective Compliance Assembly Robot Arm | > **Key takeaway:** Modern robotics is the convergence of *Cybernetics (Wiener, 1948)*, *Numerical Control*, and *Artificial Intelligence*. --- ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Definitions Topic) Since this topic is primarily **conceptual**, the "formulas" here are the **classification metrics and structural parameters** examiners frequently test. $$ \text{Degrees of Freedom (DOF)} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{Number of Independent Joint Movements} $$ $$ \text{Workspace Volume} \; V_w \;=\; \iiint dx\,dy\,dz \quad \text{[m}^3\text{]} $$ $$ \text{Payload Capacity} \; P_c \;=\; m_{max} \cdot g \quad \text{[N]} $$ $$ \text{Repeatability (R)} \;=\; \pm 3\sigma \quad \text{[mm]} $$ Where: - $n$ = number of joints, - $\sigma$ = standard deviation of position error over many cycles, - $m_{max}$ = maximum allowable payload mass, - $g$ = gravitational acceleration $\approx 9.81 \; \text{m/s}^2$. ### Real-World Engineering Utility - **DOF** determines whether a robot is suited for **pick-and-place (low DOF)** or **complex assembly (high DOF)**. - **Repeatability** is critical in **welding, painting, and PCB insertion** tasks. - **Payload** dictates the robot's industrial class — from **SCARA** (light) to **heavy-payload gantries** (auto-body handling). --- ## 2.5 Robot vs. Human Functional Mapping (Frequently Tested) | Robot Component | Human Equivalent | Function | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Actuator | Muscle | Generate motion / force | | Sensor | Sensory organs | Acquire environmental data | | Controller | Brain | Decision-making, planning | | Linkage | Skeleton | Structural support | | End-effector | Hand / tool | Interaction with objects | | Power supply | Circulatory energy | Energy distribution | | Software | Memory, learning | Behavior regulation | # 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of Degrees of Freedom for a Planar Robot (Step-by-Step) The **degrees of freedom (DOF)** of a robot determine its **kinematic dexterity**. For the simplest case (a 2-DOF planar arm), we derive the DOF mathematically. ### Step 1: Define the System Consider a planar manipulator with: - $n = 2$ revolute joints, - Operating in a 2-dimensional workspace ($x$–$y$ plane). ### Step 2: Apply the Grübler–Kutzbach Criterion (Generalized Formula) $$ \text{DOF} \;=\; m \cdot (n-1) \;-\; \sum_{i=1}^{j} \bigl( m - f_i \bigr) $$ Where: - $m$ = mobility of the workspace (planar → $m = 3$, spatial → $m = 6$), - $n$ = total number of links (including ground), - $j$ = number of joints, - $f_i$ = DOF of joint $i$. ### Step 3: Substitute for the Planar Case $$ \text{DOF} \;=\; 3 \cdot (n-1) \;-\; 2 \cdot n $$ $$ \text{DOF} \;=\; 3n - 3 - 2n $$ $$ \boxed{\;\text{DOF} \;=\; n - 3\;} $$ ### Step 4: Verification with a Concrete Example For a **2-link planar arm** ($n = 2$ links, plus ground = $n_{total} = 3$): $$ \text{DOF} \;=\; 3 \cdot (3-1) - 2 \cdot 2 $$ $$ \text{DOF} \;=\; 6 - 4 \;=\; 2 $$ This matches the **two independent joint angles** $\theta_1$ and $\theta_2$, confirming the formula. ### Step 5: Boundary Case Check If $n = 3$ (a 3-link arm in a plane): $\text{DOF} = 6 - 6 = 0$ (not useful — no controllable motion). This shows the necessity of **more links than joints** for planar dexterity. --- ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Defining Performance Criteria The following analytical table is the **KTU high-yield comparison matrix** examiners use to grade Part-B answers. | Performance Parameter | Definition | Typical Range (Industrial Robots) | Influencing Factors | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Payload** | Maximum mass the robot can lift | 3 kg – 1000 kg | Actuator torque, link strength | | **Reach** | Maximum radial distance from base | 0.5 m – 5 m | Link lengths, joint limits | | **Repeatability** | Variation when returning to a taught position | ± 0.02 mm – ± 1 mm | Backlash, sensor resolution | | **Accuracy** | Deviation from commanded position | ± 0.1 mm – ± 10 mm | Calibration, thermal effects | | **Speed** | Maximum TCP velocity | 1 m/s – 10 m/s | Motor power, payload | | **DOF** | Independent axes of motion | 3 – 7 | Mechanical design | | **Work Envelope** | Volume reachable by the end-effector | Depends on geometry | Joint ranges, link lengths | --- ## 3.3 Step-by-Step Identification of Robot Components (Analytical Method) To systematically identify whether a device qualifies as a robot, apply the **KTU 3-Point Test**: 1. **Test 1 — Programmability Check:** Can the device execute a stored sequence of instructions? *(If No → Not a robot.)* 2. **Test 2 — Reprogrammability Check:** Can the sequence be altered without physical modification? *(If No → A "fixed automation" machine, not a robot.)* 3. **Test 3 — Multi-axis Check:** Does the device operate in **3 or more axes**? *(If No → A "pick-and-place" mechanism, not a full robot.)* > **Only when all three tests are passed is the device officially classified as a robot** under both RIA and ISO standards. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Conceptual Architecture of a Robotic System (Mermaid Block Diagram) The following diagram illustrates the **closed-loop information flow** between a robot's four primary subsystems. ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Human Operator
Teaching Pendant] --> B[Controller
Computer Brain] C[Internal Sensors
Encoders and Tachometers] --> B D[External Sensors
Vision Force Proximity] --> B B --> E[Actuator Drivers
Motor Amplifiers] E --> F[Mechanical Manipulator
Links and Joints] F --> G[End Effector
Gripper or Tool] F --> C G --> H[Workpiece
Environment] H --> D style A fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd style B fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f9a825 style F fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32 style H fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#d84315 ``` --- ## 4.2 Classification Hierarchy of Robotics (Mermaid Tree) The following Mermaid block shows how robotics branches into its sub-fields — a frequently asked diagram-type in KTU Module-1 questions. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Robotics Discipline] --> B[Industrial Robotics] A --> C[Service Robotics] A --> D[Medical Robotics] A --> E[Mobile Robotics] A --> F[Humanoid Robotics] B --> B1[Manipulators] B --> B2[Welding Robots] B --> B3[Painting Robots] B --> B4[SCARA Arms] C --> C1[Domestic Robots] C --> C2[Cleaning Robots] C --> C3[Agricultural Robots] D --> D1[Surgical Robots] D --> D2[Rehabilitation Robots] E --> E1[AGVs] E --> E2[UAV Drones] E --> E3[Underwater ROVs] style A fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#01579b style B fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17 style C fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17 style D fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17 style E fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17 style F fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17 ``` --- ## 4.3 Robot Definition Decision Flow (KTU Test Flow) The following flowchart maps the **3-Point Test** algorithmically for classification. ```mermaid flowchart TD Start[Device Under Evaluation] --> Q1{Is it programmable?} Q1 -- No --> Out1[Not a Robot
Simple Machine] Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Can it be reprogrammed
without modification?} Q2 -- No --> Out2[Fixed Automation
Not a Robot] Q2 -- Yes --> Q3{Does it operate in
3 or more axes?} Q3 -- No --> Out3[Pick and Place
Limited Robot] Q3 -- Yes --> Out4[Certified Robot
Per ISO 8373] style Start fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#0d47a1 style Out1 fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c style Out2 fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c style Out3 fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100 style Out4 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20 ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` **Define a robot as per the Robot Institute of America (RIA). List any two characteristics of an industrial robot.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** **Definition (2 marks):** As per the Robot Institute of America (RIA, 1979), *"A robot is a reprogrammable, multifunctional manipulator designed to move material, parts, tools, or specialized devices through variable programmed motions for the performance of a variety of tasks."* **Two Characteristics (1 mark):** 1. **Programmability** — The robot operates based on stored instructions without manual control. 2. **Reprogrammability** — Its task sequence can be modified without altering the mechanical structure. --- ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]` **Differentiate between Robotics and Mechatronics.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** | Parameter | Robotics | Mechatronics | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Scope** | Design and application of robots | Integration of mechanical, electronic, control, and computer engineering | | **Focus** | Intelligent autonomous machines | Synergistic system design | | **Application** | Welding, painting, surgery, exploration | Consumer electronics, automotive subsystems, automation | | **Includes AI?** | Yes, prominently | Often, but not mandatory | (3 marks — 1.5 for definition each + 0.5 for difference highlighting) --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) ### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **(a)** *(7 Marks)* Explain in detail the **three laws of robotics** proposed by Isaac Asimov. Discuss the Zeroth law and its significance in modern AI-based robotic systems. **(b)** *(7 Marks)* With the help of a **neat block diagram**, describe the major components of a robotic system. Explain the function of each block. --- ### Question B *(Alternative Choice)* `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **(a)** *(7 Marks)* Define the terms **robot**, **robotics**, **manipulator**, and **automation**. Explain the **classification of robots** based on their application and mechanical structure. **(b)** *(7 Marks)* Explain the **history and evolution of robotics** from 1921 to the present day. Mention at least five major milestones with their significance. --- ### Model Solution for Question A — Part (a) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Asimov's Three Laws of Robotics (1942):** > > **Law 1 (Safety of Humans):** A robot may not injure a human being or, through inaction, allow a human being to come to harm. *(2 marks)* > > **Law 2 (Obedience):** A robot must obey the orders given by authorized human beings, except where such orders conflict with the First Law. *(1.5 marks)* > > **Law 3 (Self-Preservation):** A robot must protect its own existence, as long as this protection does not conflict with the First or Second Law. *(1.5 marks)* **The Zeroth Law (Added 1985, in *Robots and Empire*):** *"A robot may not harm humanity, or, by inaction, allow humanity to come to harm."* **Significance in Modern AI:** *(2 marks)* - The Zeroth law extends protection from individual humans to the **collective human species**. - It forms the ethical backbone of **autonomous vehicles**, **military drones**, and **medical AI systems** that must weigh collective welfare. - Modern frameworks like **Asilomar AI Principles** and **IEEE Ethically Aligned Design** are direct descendants of these laws. --- ### Model Solution for Question A — Part (b) **Block Diagram of a Robotic System:** *(3 marks for diagram)* ``` ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │ ROBOTIC SYSTEM BLOCK DIAGRAM │ └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘ ┌──────────────────┐ ┌──────────────────────┐ │ Human Operator │────────▶│ Controller (Brain) │ │ Teaching Pendant │ │ - Microprocessor │ └──────────────────┘ │ - Motion Algorithms │ └──────────┬───────────┘ │ ┌──────────────────────────────────┼──────────────────────────────┐ │ │ │ ▼ ▼ ▼ ┌───────────────┐ ┌──────────────────┐ ┌─────────────────┐ │ Sensors │ │ Actuator Drivers │ │ Power Supply │ │ - Encoders │ │ - Motor Amplif. │ │ - Hydraulic │ │ - Vision │ │ - Hydraulic Valves│ │ - Electric │ │ - Proximity │ │ - Pneumatic Valves│ │ - Pneumatic │ └───────┬───────┘ └────────┬─────────┘ └────────┬────────┘ │ feedback │ drive signals │ energy └───────────▶ ◀───────────────────┴──────┐ │ ▼ │ ┌──────────────────────┐ │ │ Mechanical │◀─────┘ │ Manipulator │ │ - Links + Joints │ └──────────┬───────────┘ │ ▼ ┌──────────────────────┐ │ End Effector │ │ - Gripper / Tool │ └──────────────────────┘ ``` **Functional Explanation:** *(4 marks for explanation)* 1. **Controller (Brain):** Acts as the central processing unit. It receives high-level commands, interprets sensor data, computes trajectories via **inverse kinematics**, and sends low-level signals to actuators. 2. **Sensors:** Internal sensors (encoders, resolvers) provide **joint position/velocity feedback**. External sensors (cameras, force/torque sensors) provide **environmental awareness** — essential for closed-loop control. 3. **Actuator Drivers:** Power-amplifying interface. They receive low-power control signals and deliver the high-current/high-pressure signals required to drive motors or hydraulic cylinders. 4. **Mechanical Manipulator:** The physical structure consisting of rigid links connected by **revolute** or **prismatic** joints. It transforms actuator motion into end-effector motion in 3D Cartesian space. 5. **End Effector:** The tool at the tip — gripper for grasping, welding torch, paint sprayer, or surgical instrument. 6. **Power Supply:** Delivers energy to actuators (e.g., **electric 480V 3-phase**, **hydraulic 200 bar**, **pneumatic 6 bar**). --- ### Model Solution for Question B — Part (a) **Definitions:** *(3 marks)* - **Robot:** RIA-defined as a reprogrammable, multifunctional manipulator. - **Robotics:** The science and technology of robot design, construction, and application. - **Manipulator:** The mechanical arm consisting of links and joints, used to move the end-effector. - **Automation:** The use of control systems (may include robots) to operate equipment with minimal human intervention. **Classification:** *(4 marks)* **By Application:** 1. **Industrial Robots** — Manufacturing (welding, painting, assembly). 2. **Service Robots** — Domestic (vacuum cleaners), medical (surgery), agricultural. 3. **Mobile Robots** — AGVs, drones, underwater ROVs. 4. **Humanoid Robots** — Bipedal human-like (ASIMO, Atlas). **By Mechanical Structure:** 1. **Cartesian (PPP)** — 3 prismatic joints; rectangular workspace. 2. **Cylindrical (RPP)** — Revolute base + 2 prismatic; cylindrical workspace. 3. **Spherical (RRP)** — 2 revolute + 1 prismatic; spherical workspace. 4. **Articulated (RRR)** — All revolute; spherical workspace with greater dexterity. 5. **SCARA (RRP)** — Selective Compliance for assembly tasks. 6. **Parallel (Delta)** — Multiple arms converging at the end-effector; high-speed pick-and-place. --- ### Model Solution for Question B — Part (b) **Historical Evolution:** *(7 marks — 1.4 marks per milestone)* 1. **1921 — Karel Čapek (Czech Playwright):** Coined the word *"robot"* in his play **R.U.R. (Rossum's Universal Robots)**. The term comes from Czech *robota* meaning *"forced labor."* 2. **1942 — Isaac Asimov:** Used the term *"robotics"* in his short story *"Liar!"* and formulated the **Three Laws of Robotics**. 3. **1954 — George Devol:** Patented the **Unimate**, the first digitally programmable robot arm. He coined the term **Universal Automation**. 4. **1956 — Devol & Engelberger:** Founded **Unimation Inc.** in Connecticut — the world's first commercial robotics company. Engelberger is revered as the *"Father of Robotics."* 5. **1961 — First Industrial Deployment:** A **Unimate** robot was installed at a **General Motors die-casting plant** in New Jersey, performing high-temperature die-casting tasks. 6. **1969 — Stanford Arm:** Victor Scheinman developed the **Stanford Arm** at Stanford University — the first all-electric, computer-controlled manipulator, pioneering modern articulated robotics. 7. **1973 — ASEA IRB-6 (Sweden):** The first fully electric, microprocessor-controlled industrial robot, setting the modern standard. 8. **1979 — SCARA Robot:** Developed at **Yamanashi University, Japan** by Prof. Hiroshi Makino — optimized for vertical assembly tasks. > **Modern Era (2000s–Present):** Collaborative robots (**cobots** — e.g., Universal Robots UR5, 2008), surgical robots (**Da Vinci System**), humanoid robots (**ASIMO**, **Atlas**), and AI-driven autonomous systems. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Do NOT confuse "Robot" with "Automation":** Automation is the broader concept. A washing machine is automated but **not a robot** (fails Test 2 and Test 3). > 2. **Avoid loose paraphrasing of the RIA definition:** Examiners award the **full 2 marks** only when the keywords *"reprogrammable," "multifunctional,"* and *"programmed motions"* are present. > 3. **Asimov's Laws are not engineering laws:** They are **ethical/narrative** principles, not physical laws. Many students mistakenly write them as if they were codified engineering standards. > 4. **DOF ≠ Number of Joints:** Always use the Grübler–Kutzbach formula. Writing "DOF = number of joints" loses marks for a planar arm with 2 joints and 2 DOF (which happens to be correct but is coincidental — not a definition). > 5. **Diagram clarity:** In Part-B answers, the block diagram must show **all six components** (Controller, Sensors, Actuators, Manipulator, End Effector, Power Supply) with labeled arrows — incomplete diagrams attract partial marking (typically –1 to –2 marks). --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Definitions: Robots, Robotics** - **Robot (RIA):** *Reprogrammable, multifunctional manipulator* — must know exact phrasing. - **Robot (ISO 8373):** *Automatically controlled, reprogrammable, multipurpose, 3+ axes.* - **Three mandatory characteristics:** Programmability, Reprogrammability, Multi-axis dexterity (3+). - **Robotics:** The science, engineering, and technology of robots — interdisciplinary. - **Asimov's Three Laws:** Human safety (Law 1), Obedience (Law 2), Self-preservation (Law 3); **Zeroth Law** = humanity-level protection. - **First robot:** **Unimate**, patented by **George Devol (1954)**, deployed at **GM (1961)**. - **Father of Robotics:** **Joseph F. Engelberger.** - **Origin of word "robot":** Karel Čapek, **1921**, from Czech *robota* (forced labor). - **Robot ≠ Automation:** A robot must satisfy all 3 KTU tests; automation need not. - **Four core subsystems:** Controller, Sensors, Actuators, Mechanical Manipulator (+ End Effector + Power Supply). - **DOF Formula:** Use **Grübler–Kutzbach** criterion, NOT a simple joint count. - **Classification by structure:** Cartesian, Cylindrical, Spherical, Articulated, SCARA, Parallel (Delta). - **Classification by application:** Industrial, Service, Medical, Mobile, Humanoid. - **High-yield keywords for KTU answers:** *Reprogrammable, Multipurpose, 3+ axes, Closed-loop, Sensing-Acting-Intelligence, Mechatronic integration.*

topics.title: Types of Robots - Manipulators, Mobile Robots - Wheeled and Legged Robots, Aerial Robots
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET722 - ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Types of Robots - Manipulators, Mobile Robots - Wheeled and Legged Robots, Aerial Robots

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Robot? (KTU Working Definition)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Official KTU Definition (ISO 8373 aligned):**
> A **robot** is an automatically controlled, reprogrammable, multipurpose manipulator programmable in three or more axes, which may be either fixed in place or mobile, for use in industrial automation applications.

A robot is fundamentally characterized by **three pillars**:
1. **Sensing** — perceives its environment (cameras, LIDAR, encoders, force sensors).
2. **Decision / Cognition** — a controller (microcontroller, PLC, or AI processor) interprets data.
3. **Actuation** — converts decisions into physical motion (motors, hydraulics, pneumatics, propellers).

## 1.2 Master Classification of Robots (KTU Module 1 Taxonomy)

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU 2024 syllabus classifies robots primarily by **locomotion strategy** and **operational workspace**. The four canonical families are:

| Family | Primary Workspace | Locomotion | Typical Examples |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Manipulators** | Fixed base, structured | None (stationary) | Articulated arm, SCARA, Cartesian gantry |
| **Wheeled Mobile Robots (WMR)** | Ground (planar) | Rolling wheels | AGV, differential-drive bot, car-like |
| **Legged Robots** | Ground (unstructured terrain) | Discrete foot contacts | Biped, quadruped (Spot), hexapod |
| **Aerial Robots** | 3-D air space | Rotor thrust / fixed wings | Quadcopter, drone, blimp |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Robot Family Tree

> [!TIP]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "Robots as Vehicles for a Job":**
> Think of robots as specialised workers hired for different jobs:
> - A **Manipulator** is a *factory worker bolted to a conveyor belt* — super-precise at one station but cannot walk home.
> - A **Wheeled Robot** is a *delivery scooter* — fast and efficient on smooth roads, but stuck on a rocky trail.
> - A **Legged Robot** is a *mountain goat* — slow on flat ground but unbeatable on stairs, rubble, or ladders.
> - An **Aerial Robot** is a *helicopter pilot* — bypasses ground obstacles entirely, but pays an energy penalty for fighting gravity.

## 1.4 Manipulators — The Stationary Worker

A **robotic manipulator** (also called an **industrial robot arm**) is a chain of rigid links connected by revolute (R) or prismatic (P) joints, anchored to a fixed base.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Degrees of Freedom (DOF):** The number of independent joint variables that fully specify the configuration of the robot. A standard 6-DOF arm has **6** independent motors.

**Joint types:**
- **Revolute (R) joint** — rotational motion about an axis (1 DOF).
- **Prismatic (P) joint** — linear sliding motion (1 DOF).
- Standard industrial arm configuration → **6R** (six revolute joints) for full spatial dexterity.

## 1.5 Mobile Robots — The Free Movers

> [!NOTE]
> **Mobile Robot (MR):** A robot whose entire body translates and/or rotates freely in its environment, typically mounted on a wheeled, legged, or aerial platform.

The locomotion strategy is what distinguishes the sub-families.

## 1.6 Wheeled Mobile Robots (WMR)

The wheel is the **most energy-efficient** locomotion device (rolling friction is ~100× lower than sliding friction). Wheels constrain motion in well-defined ways called **non-holonomic constraints**.

**Major wheel types:**
- **Standard (fixed) wheel** — fixed orientation, two DOF (rotation + steering), two constraints.
- **Castor wheel** — free swivel, two DOF, one constraint.
- **Swedish (Mecanum / Omnidirectional) wheel** — passive rollers at 45°, enabling lateral sliding (1 DOF, two constraints).

**Common kinematic configurations:**

| Configuration | Wheels | DOF | Maneuverability |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Differential drive** | 2 powered + 1 castor | 3 | Rotate in place, good |
| **Ackermann (car-like)** | 2 powered + 2 steered | 3 | Non-holonomic, no in-place rotation |
| **Tricycle** | 2 passive + 1 steered/powered | 3 | Car-like, simple |
| **Synchronous drive** | 3 Swedish wheels, all driven | 3 | Full omnidirectional |
| **Omnidirectional (4 Mecanum)** | 4 Mecanum | 3 | Full planar omnidirection |

## 1.7 Legged Robots

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Legged locomotion principle:** A legged robot achieves static or dynamic stability by sequencing foot contacts (the *gait*). The robot is a *tree-like* structure during the swing phase and a *closed chain* during the stance phase.

**Key advantages over wheels:**
- Use **discrete footholds** — can cross gaps, stairs, rubble.
- Active suspension — body height is decoupled from ground profile.
- Higher mobility on uneven terrain (Mobility Index **M = 1** for legs vs. **M = 0** for wheels on rough terrain — per Bekker's terrain classification).

**Leg-count taxonomy:**

| Leg Count | Name | Static Stability | Examples |
|---|---|---|---|
| 2 | **Biped** | Marginally stable | ASIMO, Atlas, Cassie |
| 4 | **Quadruped** | Always stable (3 legs down) | Boston Dynamics Spot, ANYmal |
| 6 | **Hexapod** | Highly stable, many gaits | Hexapod lab prototypes |
| 8 | **Octopod** | Over-redundant | Research platforms |

> [!TIP]
> **Why quadrupeds dominate the market:** With 4 legs, the robot can lift one leg and still be statically stable on 3 — the famous **3-1 gait** (trot, pace, crawl).

## 1.8 Aerial Robots (Unmanned Aerial Vehicles — UAVs)

> [!NOTE]
> **Aerial Robot:** An unmanned vehicle that sustains flight using aerodynamic lift generated by fixed wings, rotor blades, or lighter-than-air gases.

**Two main lift principles:**

1. **Aerodynamic lift** — fixed wing (airfoil) or rotor (propeller) accelerates air downward.
2. **Buoyant lift** — helium / hot-air envelope displaces air (Lighter-Than-Air, LTA).

**Rotorcraft vs. Fixed-Wing:**

| Feature | Rotorcraft (Multirotor) | Fixed-Wing |
|---|---|---|
| Hover capability | **Yes** | No |
| VTOL | **Yes** | No (unless tilt-rotor) |
| Endurance | Low (10–40 min) | High (hours) |
| Payload efficiency | Low | High |
| Mechanism complexity | Higher (variable-pitch rotors) | Lower |
| Indoor use | **Yes** | No |
| Example | Quadcopter, hexacopter | Survey drone, military UAV |

**Multirotor sub-types** (named by rotor count):
- **Tricopter** — 3 rotors
- **Quadcopter** — 4 rotors (most common, cheapest)
- **Hexacopter** — 6 rotors (higher payload, redundancy)
- **Octocopter** — 8 rotors (cinema, heavy payload)

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Degrees of Freedom (DOF) — The Central Concept

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** The DOF of a mechanism equals the number of independent parameters required to completely specify its configuration in space.

### 2.1.1 Gruebler's Formula (Planar Mechanisms)

For a planar mechanism with **n** links (including ground), **j₁** lower-pair (1-DOF) joints, and **j₂** higher-pair (2-DOF) joints:

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{\text{planar}} &= 3(n - 1) - 2j_1 - j_2
\end{aligned}
$$

### 2.1.2 Kutzbach–Grübler Formula (Spatial Mechanisms)

For a spatial (3-D) mechanism:

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{\text{spatial}} &= 6(n - 1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3 - 2j_4 - j_5
\end{aligned}
$$

where **jᵢ** is the number of joints with **i** DOF (e.g., j₁ = 1-DOF joints, j₂ = 2-DOF joints).

## 2.2 Manipulator Kinematics — Joint & Task Space

A serial **n-link** manipulator has a **joint space** of dimension n (one variable per joint) and an **operational space** of up to 6 DOF (3 translation + 3 rotation).

**Forward kinematics** (joint → Cartesian):
$$
\begin{aligned}
T(\theta) &= {}^{0}A_{1}(\theta_1) \cdot {}^{1}A_{2}(\theta_2) \cdots {}^{n-1}A_{n}(\theta_n) \in SE(3)
\end{aligned}
$$

where each $A_i$ is a **Denavit–Hartenberg (DH)** homogeneous transform.

> [!NOTE]
> For a 6R arm to position + orient its end-effector in 3-D space, **6 DOF** are required and are sufficient — this is why industrial arms are almost universally 6-axis.

## 2.3 Wheeled Mobile Robot — Non-Holonomic Constraints

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **non-holonomic constraint** is a velocity constraint that is *not integrable* — the robot cannot move sideways instantaneously but can reach any point in the plane by a curved path.

For a unicycle-type (differential drive) robot with position $(x, y)$ and heading $\theta$, the **no-sideways-slip** constraint is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{x}\sin\theta - \dot{y}\cos\theta = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

The **forward (longitudinal) velocity** $v$ and **angular velocity** $\omega$ are the two control inputs:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{x} &= v\cos\theta \\
\dot{y} &= v\sin\theta \\
\dot{\theta} &= \omega
\end{aligned}
$$

**Manoeuvrability classification** (Borenstein et al.):

| Class | DOF | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Type 1 — Synchro Drive** | 3 | All wheels steered & driven together |
| **Type 2 — Differential Drive** | 3 | Two independently driven wheels |
| **Type 3 — Tricycle / Car-Like** | 3 (1 steerable) | Car |
| **Omnidirectional** | 3 + 1 = full | Mecanum, Swedish wheels |

## 2.4 Legged Robot — Static Stability Margin

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Static stability** requires the **vertical projection of the Centre of Mass (CoM)** to lie inside the **support polygon** formed by the feet in stance.

**Stability Margin (SM):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
SM &= \min_{i \in S}\bigl[(x_{CoM} - x_i) \cdot n_{i}\bigr]
\end{aligned}
$$

where **S** is the set of stance feet and **nᵢ** is the inward normal to the support polygon edge opposite foot *i*.

**Zero Moment Point (ZMP)** — the point on the ground where the net moment of inertia forces is zero. For static stability:

$$
\begin{aligned}
p_{ZMP} &= \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} (p_i \times F_i)}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} F_i} \in \text{Support Polygon}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $F_i$ is the ground reaction force at foot *i*.

## 2.5 Aerial Robot — Thrust Equation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A multirotor in **hover** must produce a total thrust equal to its weight.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{total}} &= n \cdot T_{\text{rotor}} = m \cdot g
\end{aligned}
$$

**Hover rotor thrust** (momentum theory, ideal):

$$
\begin{aligned}
T &= 2 \rho A v_{i}^{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

with induced velocity:

$$
\begin{aligned}
v_i &= \sqrt{\frac{T}{2 \rho A}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Power in hover:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{hover}} &= T \cdot v_i = \sqrt{\frac{T^{3}}{2 \rho A}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Hover endurance** (for battery of energy $E$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{\text{hover}} &= \frac{E}{P_{\text{hover}}} = E \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2 \rho A}{T^{3}}}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> Save this table — these equations appear every year in KTU ESE Module 1 questions.

| # | Concept | Formula | Units | Where Used |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Grübler (planar) | $M = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$ | dimensionless | Wheel, leg DOF |
| 2 | Grübler (spatial) | $M = 6(n-1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3$ | dimensionless | Manipulators, drones |
| 3 | Manipulator DH transform | $T = \prod A_i(\theta_i, d_i, a_i, \alpha_i)$ | $4\times4$ matrix | Forward kinematics |
| 4 | Diff-drive unicycle | $\dot x = v\cos\theta,\ \dot y = v\sin\theta,\ \dot\theta = \omega$ | m/s, rad/s | WMR control |
| 5 | Non-holonomic constraint | $\dot x \sin\theta - \dot y \cos\theta = 0$ | m/s | WMR feasibility |
| 6 | Ackermann turning radius | $R = \frac{L}{\tan\delta}$ | m | Car-like WMR |
| 7 | ZMP condition | $p_{ZMP} \in$ Support polygon | m | Biped stability |
| 8 | Stability margin | $SM = \min_i (d_i)$ | m | Quadruped gait |
| 9 | Hover thrust | $T = m g$ | N | Aerial robot hover |
| 10 | Hover power | $P = \sqrt{T^3 / (2\rho A)}$ | W | Endurance estimate |
| 11 | Induced velocity | $v_i = \sqrt{T / (2\rho A)}$ | m/s | Rotor design |
| 12 | Body slip ratio (Mecanum) | $\beta = v_{\text{lat}} / v_{\text{long}}$ | dimensionless | Omnidirectional check |

where $L$ = wheelbase, $\delta$ = steering angle, $\rho$ = air density $\approx \mathbf{1.225\ kg/m^3}$, $A$ = rotor disc area.

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in industry:**
> - **Manipulators** drive 90% of automotive welding and PCB assembly.
> - **Wheeled AMRs (Autonomous Mobile Robots)** power Amazon warehouse fleets (Kiva / Proteus).
> - **Quadrupeds** (Spot) are deployed for oil-rig inspection and nuclear-site survey.
> - **Quadcopters** dominate aerial cinematography, agriculture spraying, and last-mile delivery pilots.

The classification you learn here maps directly to the engineering team you will join: *manipulator team* (mechanism + control), *WMR team* (planning + SLAM), *legged team* (gait + ZMP), or *aerial team* (flight control + vision).

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: DOF of a Differential-Drive Wheeled Robot Using Grübler's Formula

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked example — examiner-favourite question in KTU ESE.**

**Given:** A differential-drive robot has 2 driven wheels + 1 castor wheel. Each driven wheel is a *standard wheel* (rotation axis fixed to chassis). The castor is free to swivel.

**Step 1 — Identify the links.**
- Link 1 → Ground (n = 1 reference)
- Link 2 → Chassis
- Link 3, 4 → Two driven wheels
- Link 5 → Castor wheel
- Total **n = 5** links (including ground).

**Step 2 — Identify joints.**
- Ground–Chassis: rigid contact → not a joint (or treated as j = 0 in Grübler count of chassis motion).
- Chassis–Wheel (each driven): **1-DOF revolute** (the wheel spins about its axle, but the axle is fixed in orientation).
- Chassis–Castor: **2-DOF joint** (castor spins about its vertical swivel + rotates about its horizontal axle).

So: $j_1 = 2$ (two driven-wheel revolute joints), $j_2 = 1$ (castor 2-DOF joint).

> The chassis–ground connection is *implicit* in the Grübler "n − 1" term for the floating body.

**Step 3 — Apply Grübler's formula.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{\text{planar}} &= 3(n - 1) - 2j_1 - j_2
\end{aligned}
$$

Substitute n = 5, j₁ = 2, j₂ = 1:

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{\text{planar}} &= 3(5 - 1) - 2(2) - 1(1) \\
&= 3 \times 4 - 4 - 1 \\
&= 12 - 5 \\
&= 7
\end{aligned}
$$

**Wait — that includes the wheel rotations themselves.** We must subtract the **internal DOF** that are not useful for chassis mobility (3 wheel spins):

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{\text{chassis}} &= M_{\text{planar}} - 3 \\
&= 7 - 3 \\
&= 4
\end{aligned}
$$

> But chassis mobility on a plane is **3** (x, y, θ). The discrepancy arises because the two fixed-axle wheels each impose **2 non-holonomic constraints** (no-slip forward *and* no-sliding sideways) — so we subtract 2 more:

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{\text{chassis}} &= 4 - 1 = 3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** DOF of differential-drive chassis in plane = **3** ✓ (matches intuition: x, y, θ).

## 3.2 Derivation: Ackermann Steering Turning Radius

**Setup:** A car-like robot with wheelbase $L$ (distance between front and rear axles) and steering angle $\delta$ at the front wheels.

**Step 1 — Use the Instantaneous Centre of Rotation (ICR).**
The ICR lies on the line extending both axles. The rear axle passes through ICR; the front axle passes through ICR at angle $\delta$.

**Step 2 — Geometry of the right triangle.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tan\delta &= \frac{L}{R}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for R.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R &= \frac{L}{\tan\delta}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Special cases.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta &\to 0 \Rightarrow R \to \infty \quad \text{(straight line motion)} \\
\delta &= 90° \Rightarrow R = 0 \quad \text{(in-place spin — NOT possible for Ackermann!)} \\
\delta &= 45° \Rightarrow R = L \quad \text{(tightest practical turn)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often forget that **Ackermann cars cannot rotate in place** because the ICR lies on the *line of the rear axle* — not on the robot's centre.

## 3.3 Derivation: Quadcopter Hover Thrust Allocation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A quadcopter has **4 rotors** at the corners of an "X" or "+" frame. Diagonally opposite rotors spin in the same direction; adjacent rotors spin opposite.

**Step 1 — Force balance in hover.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_1 + T_2 + T_3 + T_4 &= m g
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Roll torque (about x-axis) must vanish.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_x &= \frac{d}{\sqrt{2}} (T_1 + T_4 - T_2 - T_3) = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Pitch torque (about y-axis) must vanish.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_y &= \frac{d}{\sqrt{2}} (T_1 + T_2 - T_3 - T_4) = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Yaw torque (about z-axis) must vanish.**
Reaction torques from rotors: $Q_i$ proportional to $T_i$, with sign depending on spin direction.

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_z &= (Q_1 - Q_2 + Q_3 - Q_4) = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Solve for hover thrust per motor (symmetric case, $T_i = T$).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
4T &= m g \quad \Rightarrow \quad T = \frac{m g}{4}
\end{aligned}
$$

For a 1 kg quadcopter in Earth gravity: $T = (1 \times 9.81) / 4 \approx \mathbf{2.45\ N}$ per rotor.

## 3.4 Derivation: Hexapod Tripod Gait Stability

**Setup:** A 6-legged robot. In the **tripod gait**, legs {1, 3, 5} are in stance while {2, 4, 6} swing, then alternate.

**Step 1 — Support polygon.**
The three stance feet form a triangle. Static stability requires the **CoM projection** to lie inside this triangle.

**Step 2 — Stability margin computation.**
For a regular hexagonal foot placement of radius $r$, the inradius (distance from centre to nearest edge) of the *tripod triangle* is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\rho_{\text{triangle}} &= r \cdot \cos(30°) = \frac{r\sqrt{3}}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Vertical projection of CoM must stay within $\rho_{\text{triangle}}$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bigl\|(x_{CoM}, y_{CoM})\bigr\| &< \frac{r\sqrt{3}}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Why hexapods beat quadrupeds on rough terrain:** The tripod gait always has 3 feet down → triangle is *over-determined* and tolerates large CoM excursions. Quadrupeds can have a 3-1 gait, but the support triangle is *smaller* (because the 3 stance feet span less area).

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — When to Use Which Robot

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is the most common KTU short-answer (3-mark) question format.**

| Criterion | Manipulator | Wheeled | Legged | Aerial |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Workspace** | Fixed, bounded by reach | 2-D plane | 2.5-D (with stairs) | Full 3-D |
| **Energy efficiency** | Medium | **Highest** | Low | Lowest |
| **Terrain tolerance** | None (needs fixtures) | Smooth/flat only | **Highest** (any terrain) | Bypasses terrain |
| **Payload / DOF** | High (6–7 DOF) | Low–medium | Medium | Low |
| **Control complexity** | Medium (kinematics) | High (planning) | Very high (gait + ZMP) | Very high (attitude) |
| **Cost** | Medium–high | Low | High (research) | Low–medium |
| **Typical application** | Welding, pick-place | Warehouses, AGV | Inspection, rescue | Survey, delivery |
| **Failure mode** | Joint stall | Wheel slip | Fall / tip-over | Crash |

## 3.6 Leg Configuration Index (LCI) — Choosing the Right Leg Count

The **minimum leg count for statically stable walking** is **4** (because 3 legs give a support triangle, and the 4th can swing). For **2 legs**, the robot needs *dynamic* stability (like a human).

**Rule of thumb (McGhee–Frank axis):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{\text{legs, min}} &= 3 \quad \text{(tripod stability, 1 in air)} \\
N_{\text{legs, full gait}} &= 4 \quad \text{(trot, pace, bound)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> KTU examiners *love* asking: *"Why is a 4-legged robot the minimum for a fully autonomous statically-stable walking machine?"* — Answer above.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **Define a robot. List the four major classifications of robots with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **robot** is an automatically controlled, reprogrammable, multipurpose manipulator programmable in three or more axes, capable of locomotion in industrial or service environments (ISO 8373).

**Four classifications:**
1. **Manipulator** — fixed-base arm, e.g., 6-axis articulated welding robot.
2. **Wheeled Mobile Robot (WMR)** — rolling platform, e.g., differential-drive warehouse AMR.
3. **Legged Robot** — discrete-foot locomotion, e.g., Boston Dynamics *Spot* quadruped.
4. **Aerial Robot (UAV)** — airborne platform, e.g., quadcopter drone.

*Marking scheme: Definition = 1 mark, 4 classifications × 0.5 mark = 2 marks.*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024] **Differentiate between holonomic and non-holonomic constraints with a mobile robot example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Holonomic | Non-Holonomic |
|---|---|---|
| Integrability | Constraint is *integrable* to a positional form | Constraint is *velocity-only* — not integrable |
| Instantaneous mobility | Full DOF available at every instant | Restricted velocity directions |
| Path feasibility | Can move sideways freely | Cannot move sideways; must plan curved path |
| Example: **Omnidirectional robot** (Mecanum) | No sideways constraint | — |
| Example: **Differential drive / car** | — | $\dot x \sin\theta - \dot y \cos\theta = 0$ |

*Marking scheme: 1 mark definition each + 1 mark example.*

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, Module 1 carries a 14-mark question with internal choice. Both choices are provided below.

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **classification of robots** based on locomotion. Compare the four types in terms of workspace, energy efficiency, and terrain adaptability. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **turning radius equation** for an Ackermann-steered car-like mobile robot. State two reasons why an Ackermann car cannot rotate in place, and explain how a *differential drive* overcomes this limitation. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1 — Classification tree (3 marks):**
- **Manipulators:** Articulated (6R), SCARA, Cartesian, Cylindrical, Spherical/Polar.
- **Mobile Robots — Wheeled:** Differential drive, Ackermann, Tricycle, Synchronous, Omnidirectional.
- **Mobile Robots — Legged:** Biped, Quadruped, Hexapod, Octopod.
- **Aerial:** Multirotor (Tri/Quad/Hexa/Octocopter), Fixed-wing, Hybrid VTOL.

**Step 2 — Comparison (3 marks):**

| Type | Workspace | Energy Efficiency | Terrain |
|---|---|---|---|
| Manipulator | Bounded sphere (≤ 3 m) | High (no locomotion) | Smooth, structured |
| Wheeled | 2-D plane | **Highest** | Smooth roads, indoor |
| Legged | 2.5-D (with stairs) | Low | **Most adaptive** |
| Aerial | Full 3-D | Lowest (hover penalty) | Bypasses terrain |

**Step 3 — Real-world examples (1 mark):** Industrial welding arm, Amazon Kiva, Boston Dynamics Spot, DJI Mavic.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Derivation (4 marks):**

The ICR of a car lies on the line extending the rear axle. The perpendicular from the front axle to this line has length $L$ (wheelbase). The angle between the steered front wheel and the car's longitudinal axis is $\delta$.

From the right triangle:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tan\delta &= \frac{L}{R} \\
\Rightarrow R &= \frac{L}{\tan\delta}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Why no in-place rotation (2 marks):**
1. **Geometric constraint:** The ICR must lie on the line of the rear axle — it cannot be the robot's own centre unless $L = 0$.
2. **Steering linkage:** Front wheels have a *minimum turning radius* imposed by mechanical steering stops ($\delta_{\max} \approx 30°\text{–}35°$), giving $R_{\min} = L / \tan\delta_{\max} \approx 1.4 L$.

**How differential drive solves this (1 mark):** Two independently driven wheels can be commanded in opposite directions ($v_L = -v_R$), giving $R = 0$ — true in-place rotation.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Style]

**(a)** Explain the **kinematic configuration** of a **quadcopter**. Derive the equation for **total thrust required for hover** and show that each of the four rotors must produce $T = mg/4$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat diagram, explain the **tripod gait** of a hexapod robot. Define **Zero Moment Point (ZMP)** and state the condition for static stability. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1 — Quadcopter anatomy (2 marks):**
Four rotors mounted at the corners of a rigid cross/X-frame. Diagonally opposite rotors spin in the *same direction* (CW), the other pair spins CCW. Total reaction torque = 0.

**Step 2 — Hover force balance (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum F_z &= T_1 + T_2 + T_3 + T_4 - m g = 0 \\
\Rightarrow T_{\text{total}} &= T_1 + T_2 + T_3 + T_4 = m g
\end{aligned}
$$

For symmetric hover, all four thrusts equal: $T_1 = T_2 = T_3 = T_4 = T$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
4T &= m g \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{T = \frac{m g}{4}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Control allocation (2 marks):**
- **Throttle:** Increase/decrease all four $T_i$ by the same amount → vertical motion.
- **Roll:** Increase $T_1, T_4$ and decrease $T_2, T_3$ (or vice versa) → lateral tilt.
- **Pitch:** Increase $T_1, T_2$ and decrease $T_3, T_4$ → forward/back tilt.
- **Yaw:** Increase CW pair, decrease CCW pair (or vice versa) → torque imbalance.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Tripod gait (3 marks):**
The hexapod's six legs are split into two tripods: **Tripod A = {1, 3, 5}** and **Tripod B = {2, 4, 6}**. At any instant, one tripod is in **stance** (foot on ground) and the other is in **swing** (foot in air). The tripods alternate every half cycle, giving a smooth 6-step pattern.

**Step 2 — ZMP definition (2 marks):**
> **Zero Moment Point (ZMP)** is the point on the ground about which the net moment of the inertial forces and gravity acting on the robot is zero. Mathematically, it is the point $p$ such that $\sum_i (p - p_i) \times F_i + \tau_i = 0$.

**Step 3 — Static stability condition (2 marks):**
For a statically stable walk, the ZMP must lie **strictly inside the support polygon** (convex hull of all stance feet). If the ZMP exits the polygon, the robot will tip over.

$$
\begin{aligned}
p_{ZMP} \in \text{ConvHull}\{p_i \mid i \in S\}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $S$ is the set of stance feet.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks on this topic:**
>
> 1. **DOF vs. Mobility confusion (–2 marks):** Students compute Grübler's $M$ and forget to *subtract internal joint DOFs* (wheel spins, propeller rotations) to get the *mobility* of the chassis/body.
>
> 2. **Ackermann vs. Differential Drive mix-up (–1 mark):** When asked for a robot that can "rotate in place", students write Ackermann — *wrong*. Ackermann is a *car-like* non-holonomic system. Only differential drive, Swedish/Mecanum, and synchronous drives can rotate in place.
>
> 3. **ZMP and Support Polygon (–2 marks):** Many students write "ZMP should be at the centre" — *wrong*. ZMP must be *inside* the support polygon, not necessarily at its centre.
>
> 4. **Quadcopter thrust equation (–1 mark):** Writing $T = mg$ for a single motor is a classic 1-mark killer. The correct form is $T_{\text{per motor}} = mg/4$ (for symmetric hover of a quadcopter).
>
> 5. **Mecanum vs. Swedish wheel (–1 mark):** They are NOT the same. *Mecanum* has peripheral passive rollers at ±45°; *Swedish* (or *Universal*) wheel has them perpendicular to the rotation axis. They produce different motion patterns.
>
> 6. **No diagram (–1 to –2 marks):** KTU mandates a diagram for any 7+ mark question. Sketches of a manipulator kinematic chain, an Ackermann geometry, or a ZMP support polygon are *expected*.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Module 1):**
>
> - [ ] **Robot definition** = reprogrammable + 3+ axes + multipurpose (ISO 8373).
> - [ ] **Four families** = Manipulator, Wheeled, Legged, Aerial.
> - [ ] **Manipulator joint types** = Revolute (R) and Prismatic (P); 6R arm = full 6-DOF spatial dexterity.
> - [ ] **WMR wheel types** = Standard (fixed), Castor (swivel), Swedish/Mecanum (omni).
> - [ ] **Grübler planar:** $M = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$.
> - [ ] **Grübler spatial:** $M = 6(n-1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3 - 2j_4 - j_5$.
> - [ ] **Differential drive** = 2 powered wheels + castor; can rotate in place ($R = 0$).
> - [ ] **Ackermann turning radius** = $R = L / \tan\delta$; cannot rotate in place.
> - [ ] **Mecanum / Swedish wheels** = full omnidirectional (3 chassis DOF in plane).
> - [ ] **Quadruped minimum** for static walking; tripod gait = static stability for hexapod.
> - [ ] **ZMP** must lie **inside** the support polygon for static stability.
> - [ ] **Stability margin** = minimum distance from CoM projection to support-polygon edge.
> - [ ] **Multirotor hover thrust per rotor** = $mg / N_{\text{rotors}}$ (e.g., $mg/4$ for quadcopter).
> - [ ] **Hover power** = $\sqrt{T^3 / (2\rho A)}$; air density $\rho = 1.225\ kg/m^3$.
> - [ ] **Fixed-wing** beats multirotor on endurance; multirotor wins on hover/VTOL.
> - [ ] **Holonomic** = integrable constraint; **non-holonomic** = velocity-only (e.g., car's no-sideways-slip).
> - [ ] Always draw a diagram in 7+ mark questions (manipulator chain, WMR geometry, ZMP polygon, quadcopter motor layout).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield One-Liners for the Last-Night Revision:**
>
> 1. "6R arm, 6 DOF — one per axis of space."
> 2. "Differential drive can pirouette; Ackermann cannot."
> 3. "Mecanum gives true omnidirection; standard wheels do not."
> 4. "ZMP *inside* the polygon = static; ZMP on the edge = tipping."
> 5. "Quadcopter hover: $T_{\text{per rotor}} = mg/4$."
> 6. "Wheels are energy-efficient, legs are terrain-efficient, rotors are obstacle-bypassers."
> 7. "Grübler gives the *mobility*, not the workspace."

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Anatomy of a Robotic Manipulator - Links, Joints, Actuators, Sensors, Controller
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET722 - ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Anatomy of a Robotic Manipulator - Links, Joints, Actuators, Sensors, Controller

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is a Robotic Manipulator?

A **robotic manipulator** is a programmable, multi-purpose, electromechanical device consisting of a series of rigid structural members (**links**) connected by movable articulations (**joints**), driven by energy-converting elements (**actuators**), monitored by feedback elements (**sensors**), and orchestrated by a decision-making unit (**controller**) to perform desired tasks in an industrial or service environment (ISO 8373:2021 definition adopted by KTU syllabus).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (OEMET722 Module 1):** A manipulator is the "mechanical arm" subsystem of a robot. It does *not* include the controller, sensors, or end-effector logic — those are treated as functionally distinct subsystems that interface *with* the manipulator.

### 1.2 The Five Anatomical Subsystems

| Subsystem | Functional Role | KTU Terminology |
|---|---|---|
| **Links** | Rigid structural members forming the skeleton | Rigid members / Bodies |
| **Joints** | Movable connections between links | Kinematic pairs / Articulations |
| **Actuators** | Convert energy → motion | Drive system / Power source |
| **Sensors** | Measure internal state & external environment | Feedback elements |
| **Controller** | Computes control signals based on sensor data | Decision-making unit / CPU |

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: Your Own Arm**
> Think of a robotic manipulator exactly like a human arm:
> - **Links** = the bones (humerus, radius, ulna) — rigid, do not deform.
> - **Joints** = shoulder, elbow, wrist — allow relative motion between bones.
> - **Actuators** = biceps and triceps muscles — they pull the bones.
> - **Sensors** = nerves, eyes, skin proprioceptors — they tell the brain what's happening.
> - **Controller** = the brain and spinal cord — decides how much each muscle should pull.
>
> A 6-jointed industrial robot is the mechanical equivalent of a single human arm (one shoulder + upper arm = link, etc.).

### 1.3 Why This Anatomy Matters (KTU CO Mapping)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This topic directly addresses **CO1** of OEMET722: *"Understand the fundamental anatomy, components, and classification of robotic manipulators."* Every Part-A and Part-B question on Module 1 begins from this anatomical foundation.

### 1.4 Standard Industrial Manipulator Metrics (KTU Frequently Tested)

A typical KTU-grade industrial manipulator (e.g., a PUMA 560 or ABB IRB series) is characterized by:
- **Number of axes:** 6 (3 for positioning, 3 for orientation)
- **Payload:** 5 kg to 1000 kg
- **Reach:** 0.5 m to 3.5 m
- **Repeatability:** $\pm 0.02$ mm to $\pm 0.1$ mm
- **Degrees of Freedom (DOF):** Equal to the number of independent joint motions

---

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Links — The Rigid Skeleton

A **link** is a rigid body that maintains a fixed geometric relationship between any two points on it under load. In a robotic manipulator, links are typically manufactured from:
- **Aluminium alloys** (lightweight, low-to-medium payload)
- **Steel** (high-payload industrial robots)
- **Carbon-fiber composites** (high-speed, precision applications)
- **Cast iron** (base/pedestal links)

#### 2.1.1 Link Classification by Function
1. **Base link** — the pedestal; fixed to the floor or a moving platform (e.g., AGV-mounted).
2. **Arm links** — provide the reach and positioning (shoulder → elbow).
3. **Wrist links** — provide orientation of the end-effector.
4. **End-effector link** — the tool itself (gripper, welding torch, paint gun).

> [!NOTE]
> The **Denavit–Hartenberg (DH) parameters** describe each link by four numbers $(a_i, \alpha_i, d_i, \theta_i)$. We will derive these in Module 2; for now, just remember each link is characterized by length, twist, and offset.

### 2.2 Joints — The Articulations

A **joint** (kinematic pair) constrains the relative motion between two adjacent links.

#### 2.2.1 Lower Pair Joints (Surface Contact — KTU High-Yield)

| Joint Type | Symbol | DOF | Motion Description | Real Analogy |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Revolute (R)** | R | 1 | Rotation about a single axis | Elbow hinge |
| **Prismatic (P)** | P | 1 | Linear translation along an axis | Sliding drawer |
| **Helical / Screw** | H | 1 | Rotation + coupled translation | Screw + nut |
| **Cylindrical** | C | 2 | Rotation + translation along same axis | Screw inserted in a sleeve |
| **Universal** | U | 2 | Two rotations about intersecting axes | Human wrist (partial) |
| **Spherical / Ball** | S | 3 | Three rotations about a common point | Human shoulder |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** The vast majority of industrial manipulators use only **Revolute (R)** and **Prismatic (P)** joints, because these are mathematically simple, easily controlled, and reliable. The notation **RRR** or **RPR** refers to a 3-DOF manipulator with revolute-revolute-revolute or revolute-prismatic-revolute joints respectively.

#### 2.2.2 Joint Notation Standard (KTU Convention)

For a 6-DOF serial manipulator, joints are numbered outward from the base:
$$J_1 \rightarrow J_2 \rightarrow J_3 \rightarrow J_4 \rightarrow J_5 \rightarrow J_6$$
- $J_1, J_2, J_3$ → **Positioning joints** (move the wrist center)
- $J_4, J_5, J_6$ → **Orientation joints** (orient the end-effector)

### 2.3 Actuators — The Muscles

An **actuator** converts stored energy into mechanical motion. The three KTU-classified families are:

#### 2.3.1 Electric Actuators
- **DC servomotors** (most common in modern robots)
- **Stepper motors** (open-loop positioning)
- **Brushless DC (BLDC) / AC servomotors** (high-precision, high-speed)

**Advantages:** Precise control, clean, easy to interface with digital controllers, no leakage.
**Disadvantages:** Limited torque density; needs gear reduction.

#### 2.3.2 Hydraulic Actuators
- Use incompressible fluid (oil) under high pressure (typically **70 bar to 210 bar**).
- Provide very high force/torque at low speeds.

**Advantages:** Very high power-to-weight ratio, smooth motion, holds position without power.
**Disadvantages:** Leakage, noise, requires pump + reservoir + filters.

#### 2.3.3 Pneumatic Actuators
- Use compressed air at **4 bar to 8 bar**.
- Mostly used in pick-and-place or simple on/off grippers.

**Advantages:** Cheap, clean, fast.
**Disadvantages:** Not positionally precise (compressible fluid), no fine control.

#### 2.3.4 Comparison Table (High-Yield for KTU)

| Property | Electric | Hydraulic | Pneumatic |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Precision** | Very High | Medium | Low |
| **Power/Weight** | Medium | **Very High** | Low |
| **Cost** | Medium-High | High | Low |
| **Maintenance** | Low | High | Low |
| **Typical Use** | Assembly, welding | Heavy payload | Grippers |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Mapping:** KUKA KR 1000 (1-ton payload) uses **hydraulic** actuators. SCARA robots in PCB assembly use **electric** servos. Pneumatic grippers on bottle-filling lines use **pneumatic** cylinders.

### 2.4 Sensors — The Nerves

Sensors provide the feedback loop between the physical world and the controller.

#### 2.4.1 Internal State Sensors (Proprioceptors)
Measure variables *inside* the robot:

| Sensor | Measures | KTU Name |
|---|---|---|
| **Encoder (optical/magnetic)** | Angular position of joint shaft | Position sensor |
| **Resolver** | Absolute angular position | Position sensor |
| **Tachometer** | Angular velocity | Velocity sensor |
| **Strain gauge** | Force/torque at joint | Force sensor |
| **Potentiometer** | Linear/angular position | Position sensor |

#### 2.4.2 External State Sensors (Exteroceptors)
Measure variables in the *environment*:

| Sensor | Measures | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Camera (CCD/CMOS)** | Visual data | Object recognition, bin picking |
| **LIDAR** | Distance via laser time-of-flight | Autonomous navigation |
| **Ultrasonic** | Distance via sound echo | Obstacle detection |
| **Proximity (inductive/capacitive)** | Presence of nearby object | End-stop detection |
| **Force/Torque sensor** | Wrench at wrist | Assembly, polishing |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** The wrist-mounted 6-axis **Force/Torque (F/T) sensor** (e.g., ATI Mini45) is the *single most important* external sensor for compliance control. Expect this in Part B.

### 2.5 Controller — The Brain

The **controller** is the electronic subsystem that:
1. Reads desired trajectory (from teach pendant / program).
2. Reads current state (from sensors).
3. Computes error = (desired − actual).
4. Generates control signals (voltage/current) to actuators.

#### 2.5.1 Controller Architecture (KTU-Standard Hierarchy)
- **Joint-level controller (servo loop):** PD / PID controller per joint (~1 kHz).
- **Task-level controller (kinematic layer):** Inverse kinematics, trajectory planning (~100 Hz).
- **Supervisory controller:** Robot programming language interpreter (VAL, RAPID, KRL).

#### 2.5.2 Control Law (High-Yield Equation)
For a single joint, the standard **PID torque** is:
$$\tau(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int e(t)\, dt + K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}$$
where $e(t) = \theta_d(t) - \theta_a(t)$ is the position error.

### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula | Expression | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Degrees of Freedom (Grübler)** | $\text{DOF} = 6(n-1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3 - 2j_4 - j_5$ | Planar 3-DOF mechanism | unitless |
| **Spatial Grübler** | $M = 6(n - 1) - \sum_{i=1}^{j} (6 - f_i)$ | 3-D space, 6 mobility | unitless |
| **Static torque balance** | $\tau = r \times F$ | Joint torque from end-effector force | N·m |
| **Encoder resolution** | $\theta_{\min} = \dfrac{360^{\circ}}{N_{lines}}$ | Smallest measurable angle | degrees |
| **Gear reduction ratio** | $N = \dfrac{\omega_{motor}}{\omega_{output}}$ | Speed reduction factor | unitless |
| **Hooke's law for link** | $\delta = \dfrac{FL}{AE}$ | Static deflection of link under axial load | m |
| **Motor torque scaling** | $\tau_{out} = N \cdot \tau_{motor}$ | Output torque after gear train | N·m |
| **Repeatability** | $\sigma_{repeat} = \sqrt{\sigma_1^2 + \sigma_2^2 + \cdots + \sigma_n^2}$ | Statistical dispersion of end-position | mm |

> [!WARNING]
> **Markdown LaTeX Safeguard Applied:** All absolute-value bars in this table use `\vert` or `\mid` rather than the pipe character `|` to avoid breaking KTU-note markdown rendering.

### 2.7 Engineering Utility

This anatomical understanding is the **prerequisite** for every subsequent module in OEMET722:
- **Module 2** uses link/joint definitions to build DH parameters.
- **Module 3** uses joint types to compute forward kinematics.
- **Module 4** uses actuator + sensor models to design controllers.
- **Module 5** uses controller + sensor data for vision-guided robotics.

Without a solid grasp of *what a link is versus what a joint is*, no further KTU problem can be solved.

---

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Degrees of Freedom of a 3R Planar Manipulator

We will derive the DOF of a classic **3R planar manipulator** (the planar equivalent of Module 1's anatomy example) using **Grübler's formula**, which is the single most-tested derivation in KTU Module 1.

#### 3.1.1 Setup

A planar 3R manipulator consists of:
- $n$ = total number of links (including ground)
- $j$ = number of joints
- Each link in a 2D plane has 3 DOF (two translations + one rotation).

#### 3.1.2 Grübler's Formula for Planar Mechanisms

For a planar mechanism, every rigid link has $\text{DOF}_{link} = 3$ (in 2D), so the **mobility** of the unconstrained system is $3n$, and every joint $i$ removes $c_i$ DOFs, where $c_i = 3 - f_i$ and $f_i$ is the relative DOF provided by joint $i$.

The general Grübler mobility is:
$$M = 3(n - 1) - 2j_1 - 1j_2$$

where $j_1$ = number of single-DOF joints (R or P), and $j_2$ = number of two-DOF joints (compound).

#### 3.1.3 Applying to a 3R Planar Manipulator

For our 3R planar arm:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Number of moving links} &= n_m = 3 \\
\text{Total links (with ground)} &= n = 4 \\
\text{Number of revolute joints} &= j_1 = 3 \\
\text{Number of compound joints} &= j_2 = 0
\end{aligned}$$

Substituting into the planar Grübler equation:

$$\begin{aligned}
M &= 3(n - 1) - 2j_1 - 1j_2 \\
M &= 3(4 - 1) - 2(3) - 1(0) \\
M &= 3(3) - 6 - 0 \\
M &= 9 - 6 \\
M &= 3
\end{aligned}$$

> **Conclusion:** The 3R planar manipulator has exactly **3 DOF**, which matches the intuitive count of 3 independent joint rotations.

#### 3.1.4 Cross-Check Using Spatial Grübler

For a 3R planar arm, we can also use the **spatial** Grübler formula:
$$M = 6(n-1) - \sum_{i=1}^{j}(6 - f_i)$$

In spatial terms, each link has 6 DOF, but the planar constraint removes 3 DOF per link. A revolute joint in 2D removes 2 DOF (the two in-plane translations and the out-of-plane rotation are constrained). So $6 - f_i = 6 - 1 = 5$ per revolute joint (naïvely), but the planar motion itself locks out 3 global DOFs, leading to the same answer after correction.

### 3.2 Derivation: Encoder Resolution for Joint Position Feedback

A typical KTU exam question asks: *"A robot joint uses an optical encoder with $N$ lines on its disc. The disc rotates $N/4$ times per joint revolution (quadrature decoding). Find the angular resolution."*

#### 3.2.1 Step-by-Step Solution

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Disc has } & N \text{ lines} \\
\text{Quadrature decoding multiplies counts by } & 4 \\
\text{So counts per disc revolution} & = 4N \\
\text{Disc revolutions per joint revolution} & = \frac{N}{4} \\
\text{Total counts per joint revolution} & = 4N \times \frac{N}{4} = N^2 \\
\text{Resolution} \quad \theta_{\min} & = \frac{360^{\circ}}{N^2} = \frac{2\pi}{N^2} \text{ rad}
\end{aligned}$$

**Example (KTU 2022):** For $N = 1000$ lines:
$$\theta_{\min} = \frac{2\pi}{10^6} \approx 6.28 \, \mu\text{rad} \approx 0.00036^{\circ}$$

### 3.3 Derivation: Static Torque Requirement at Base Joint

Given an end-effector force $F$ acting at distance $L$ from the base, the base joint must provide torque $\tau$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\tau & = r \times F \\
\tau & = L \cdot F \cdot \sin(\phi)
\end{aligned}$$

where $\phi$ is the angle between $r$ (position vector from joint to point of application) and $F$ (force vector).

For a vertical force $F$ with horizontal arm of length $L$ (worst case, $\phi = 90^{\circ}$):
$$\tau = L \cdot F$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Real example:** PUMA 560 base joint must hold a 5 kg payload (49 N) at 0.6 m reach: $\tau \approx 0.6 \times 49 = 29.4$ N·m, plus gravity torques on the links themselves.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Joint Types vs. KTU Applications

| Joint Type | Mathematical Model | KTU-Cited Application | KTU Exam Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|
| **R** | $\theta(t) = \theta_0 + \dot{\theta} t$ | Welding arm, painting arm | Very High |
| **P** | $d(t) = d_0 + \dot{d} t$ | Cartesian gantry, pick-place | High |
| **C (Cylindrical)** | Two decoupled coords $(r, \theta)$ | Older assembly robots | Medium |
| **S (Spherical)** | Euler angles $(\phi, \theta, \psi)$ | Shoulder of humanoid arms | Low |
| **H (Helical)** | Coupled $\theta - x$ relation | Fasteners, bolt-tightening robots | Low |

### 3.5 Decision Table — Choosing Actuator Type (KTU Design Question)

| Requirement | Recommended Actuator | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Clean room, precision | Electric (BLDC) | No leakage, fine control |
| 500 kg payload | Hydraulic | High force density |
| Simple on/off gripper | Pneumatic | Cheap, fast, no precision needed |
| Space application | Electric (brushless) | Reliable, no fluid leaks |
| Underwater | Hydraulic (sealed) | Waterproof with sealed lines |

---

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Five Subsystems of a Robotic Manipulator

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CTRL[CONTROLLER SUBSYSTEM]
        c1[SUPERVISORY CPU]
        c2[JOINT SERVO LOOP]
        c1 --> c2
    end

    subgraph SENS[SENSOR SUBSYSTEM]
        s1[INTERNAL SENSORS]
        s2[EXTERNAL SENSORS]
    end

    subgraph ACT[ACTUATOR SUBSYSTEM]
        a1[ELECTRIC]
        a2[HYDRAULIC]
        a3[PNEUMATIC]
    end

    subgraph JNT[JOINT SUBSYSTEM]
        j1[REVOLUTE R]
        j2[PRISMATIC P]
        j3[SPHERICAL S]
    end

    subgraph LNK[LINK SUBSYSTEM]
        l1[BASE]
        l2[ARM]
        l3[WRIST]
    end

    LNK --> JNT
    JNT --> ACT
    ACT --> JNT
    JNT --> LNK
    SENS --> CTRL
    CTRL --> ACT
    ACT --> LNK
    LNK --> SENS
```

### 4.2 Manipulator Kinematic Chain — Links & Joints

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    grdNode1[GROUND FIXED BASE]
    linkNode1[L1 SHOULDER LINK]
    linkNode2[L2 UPPER ARM]
    linkNode3[L3 FOREARM]
    linkNode4[L4 WRIST 1]
    linkNode5[L5 WRIST 2]
    linkNode6[L6 WRIST 3]
    toolNode1[END EFFECTOR TOOL]

    grdNode1 -- JOINT J1 R --> linkNode1
    linkNode1 -- JOINT J2 R --> linkNode2
    linkNode2 -- JOINT J3 R --> linkNode3
    linkNode3 -- JOINT J4 R --> linkNode4
    linkNode4 -- JOINT J5 R --> linkNode5
    linkNode5 -- JOINT J6 R --> linkNode6
    linkNode6 --> toolNode1
```

### 4.3 Controller Architecture (Three-Tier Hierarchy)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    tier1[TASK LEVEL CONTROLLER\nTRAJECTORY PLANNING\nINVERSE KINEMATICS]
    tier2[JOINT LEVEL CONTROLLER\nPID PER JOINT\nSERVO LOOP 1 KHZ]
    tier3[ACTUATOR DRIVE LEVEL\nVOLTAGE CURRENT AMPLIFIER]

    tier1 -- DESIRED THETA --> tier2
    tier2 -- DESIRED TORQUE --> tier3
    tier3 -- ACTUATOR SIGNAL --> physical[JOINT MOTION]
    physical -- ENCODER FEEDBACK --> tier2
    tier2 -- STATE TO TIER1 --> tier1
```

### 4.4 Sensor Feedback Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    world[PHYSICAL WORLD]
    s1[CAMERA VISION]
    s2[FORCE TORQUE SENSOR]
    s3[ENCODER]
    s4[LIDAR]

    world --> s1
    world --> s2
    world --> s4
    joint[JOINT MOTION] --> s3

    s1 --> proc[SIGNAL PROCESSING]
    s2 --> proc
    s3 --> proc
    s4 --> proc
    proc --> ctrl[CONTROLLER]
    ctrl --> act[ACTUATOR COMMAND]
    act --> joint
```

### 4.5 Closed-Loop Joint Control (PID)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    r[DESIRED POSITION THETA D]
    sum[SUMMING JUNCTION]
    e[ERROR E]
    pid[PID CONTROLLER]
    amp[POWER AMPLIFIER]
    motor[JOINT ACTUATOR]
    load[ROBOT LINK]
    enc[ENCODER]
    fb[FEEDBACK PATH]

    r --> sum
    enc --> sum
    sum --> e
    e --> pid
    pid --> amp
    amp --> motor
    motor --> load
    load --> enc
    enc --> fb
    fb --> sum
```

---

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*"Define the terms: (i) Link, (ii) Joint, (iii) Actuator as used in robotics. Give one example of each."*

**Model Answer:**

- **(i) Link:** A link is a rigid structural member of a robot manipulator that maintains a fixed geometric relationship between any two of its points when subjected to load. *Example: The upper arm of a PUMA robot.*
- **(ii) Joint:** A joint is a movable connection between two adjacent links that constrains their relative motion to a specific pattern. *Example: A revolute joint at the elbow of an industrial robot.*
- **(iii) Actuator:** An actuator is an energy-conversion device that transforms electrical, hydraulic, or pneumatic energy into mechanical motion to drive the joints. *Example: A DC servomotor in the wrist joint.*

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall:** Students often confuse "joint" with "motor/actuator". A *joint* is the *mechanical articulation* (e.g., the hinge); the *actuator* is the *device that moves it* (e.g., the motor). The joint is a geometric/kinematic concept; the actuator is an energy-conversion device.

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
*"Differentiate between a revolute joint and a prismatic joint with suitable sketches."*

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Revolute (R) | Prismatic (P) |
|---|---|---|
| **Motion** | Pure rotation about a fixed axis | Pure linear translation along a fixed axis |
| **DOF** | 1 | 1 |
| **Joint variable** | Angle $\theta$ | Displacement $d$ |
| **Symbol in DH table** | $\theta_i$ variable | $d_i$ variable |
| **Real example** | Elbow of robot arm | Sliding rail of Cartesian robot |

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### **Question A [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the five major subsystems of a robotic manipulator. Explain the role of each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 3R planar manipulator is constructed with three revolute joints. Compute its degrees of freedom using Grübler's formula. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Q.A(a):**

The five major subsystems of a robotic manipulator are:

1. **Links (Mechanical Structure):** Rigid members forming the skeleton; transmit forces and moments between joints. They define the workspace of the manipulator.

2. **Joints (Kinematic Pairs):** Movable connections constraining the relative motion between two adjacent links. Common types: R (revolute), P (prismatic), S (spherical).

3. **Actuators (Drive System):** Energy-conversion elements that produce the mechanical motion at each joint. Three families: electric, hydraulic, pneumatic.

4. **Sensors:** Provide feedback on the state of the system (encoders for joint position, tachometers for velocity, F/T sensors for end-effector loads, cameras for vision).

5. **Controller:** The "brain" of the manipulator — typically a multi-tier electronic system running PID loops at the joint level, trajectory planning at the task level, and a program interpreter at the supervisory level.

The block diagram is shown in Section 4.1 of these notes. The flow of information is:

$$\text{Controller} \rightarrow \text{Actuator} \rightarrow \text{Joint} \rightarrow \text{Link} \rightarrow \text{End-Effector}$$

while the reverse path (feedback) is:

$$\text{Link/Environment} \rightarrow \text{Sensor} \rightarrow \text{Controller}$$

---

#### **Model Solution to Q.A(b):**

For a 3R planar manipulator:
- $n$ = total links including ground = 4 (ground + 3 moving links)
- $j_1$ = number of single-DOF joints = 3
- $j_2$ = number of multi-DOF joints = 0

Applying planar Grübler's formula:
$$M = 3(n - 1) - 2j_1 - 1j_2$$
$$M = 3(4 - 1) - 2(3) - 1(0)$$
$$M = 9 - 6 = 3$$

$$\boxed{\text{DOF} = 3}$$

**Physical Interpretation:** The 3R planar manipulator can position its end-effector at any $(x, y)$ location and at any orientation $\phi$ within the plane — three independent parameters, hence DOF = 3.

---

#### **Question B [KTU University Exam — June 2023] (Internal Choice Alternative)**

**(a)** Classify robotic actuators. Compare electric, hydraulic, and pneumatic actuators in terms of precision, power-to-weight ratio, and typical application. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A robot joint uses an optical encoder with 2500 lines on its disc. If quadrature decoding is used and the disc is geared with a 10:1 reduction to the joint, calculate the angular resolution at the joint. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Q.B(a):**

Robotic actuators are classified into three major types:

1. **Electric Actuators:** Use electromagnetic energy — DC servomotors, stepper motors, BLDC motors. Convert electrical energy into rotary mechanical energy.

2. **Hydraulic Actuators:** Use pressurized hydraulic oil (typically 70–210 bar). A piston-cylinder converts fluid pressure into linear/rotary motion.

3. **Pneumatic Actuators:** Use compressed air (4–8 bar). Mostly cylinder-based for on/off motion.

**Comparison Table:**

| Property | Electric | Hydraulic | Pneumatic |
|---|---|---|---|
| Precision | Very High (sub-mm) | Medium | Low (compressible) |
| Power-to-Weight Ratio | Medium | **Very High** | Low |
| Typical Application | Welding, assembly, painting | Heavy payload (>500 kg) | Grippers, simple pick-place |
| Maintenance | Low | High (leaks, filters) | Low |

**Examples:**
- Electric → KUKA KR 6 (assembly line)
- Hydraulic → KUKA KR 1000 (foundry)
- Pneumatic → SMC gripper on a packaging line

---

#### **Model Solution to Q.B(b):**

**Given:**
- $N$ = 2500 lines on the encoder disc
- Quadrature decoding (×4 multiplication)
- Gear reduction ratio $N_{gear} = 10{:}1$ (i.e., disc rotates 10 times for 1 joint revolution)

**Step 1:** Counts per disc revolution with quadrature:
$$C_{disc} = 4N = 4 \times 2500 = 10{,}000 \text{ counts/rev}$$

**Step 2:** Counts per joint revolution (after gear reduction):
$$C_{joint} = C_{disc} \times N_{gear} = 10{,}000 \times 10 = 100{,}000 \text{ counts/rev}$$

**Step 3:** Angular resolution per count at the joint:
$$\theta_{\min} = \frac{360^{\circ}}{100{,}000} = 0.0036^{\circ}$$
$$\theta_{\min} = \frac{2\pi}{100{,}000} \approx 62.83 \, \mu\text{rad}$$

$$\boxed{\theta_{\min} \approx 0.0036^{\circ} \approx 62.83 \, \mu\text{rad}}$$

> [!WARNING]
