KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model] **Define the term "Generalised Force" in the context of robotic manipulator dynamics. State the formula relating it to the Jacobian of the manipulator.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A **Generalised Force** is the scalar or vector component of force conjugate to a generalised coordinate of a mechanical system, defined via the principle of virtual work as: $$Q_i = \sum_{j=1}^{m} \mathbf{F}_j \cdot \frac{\partial \mathbf{r}_j}{\partial q_i}$$ For a robotic manipulator with an external wrench $\mathbf{F}_{\text{ext}}$ applied at the end-effector, the joint-space generalised force (joint torque vector) is related to the geometric Jacobian $\mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q})$ by: $$\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mathbf{J}^T(\mathbf{q}) \, \mathbf{F}_{\text{ext}}$$ This is known as the **Jacobian Transpose** method and embodies the **force–velocity duality** of robotic manipulators. (3 marks) --- ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model] **Explain the physical significance of virtual work in the derivation of generalised force.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The principle of **virtual work** states that for a system in equilibrium under ideal constraints, the total work done by all applied forces during any virtual displacement (consistent with constraints) must be zero. Physically, this means: 1. **Generalised forces project physical forces** onto the directions in which the system is *actually free to move* (the tangent space of the configuration manifold). 2. **Constraint forces do no work** because they act in directions the system cannot move. 3. The dot product $\mathbf{F}_j \cdot \frac{\partial \mathbf{r}_j}{\partial q_i}$ geometrically represents the *projection of the force onto the velocity direction* induced by a unit change in $q_i$. Thus, virtual work converts a *Cartesian-space* force balance into an equivalent *joint-space* force balance, yielding the generalised force definition. (3 marks) --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question contains two sub-parts of 7 marks each. The internal choice means you must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. ### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model] **A 3-DOF planar manipulator has the following forward kinematics:** $$\mathbf{p} = \begin{bmatrix} L_1 \cos q_1 + L_2 \cos(q_1+q_2) + L_3 \cos(q_1+q_2+q_3) \\ L_1 \sin q_1 + L_2 \sin(q_1+q_2) + L_3 \sin(q_1+q_2+q_3) \end{bmatrix}$$ **An external force $\mathbf{F} = [10 \; 5]^T$ N is applied at the end-effector when the joint configuration is $q_1 = 30°$, $q_2 = 45°$, $q_3 = -30°$. Take $L_1 = L_2 = L_3 = 1$ m.** #### Part (a) [7 Marks] — Derive the Geometric Jacobian of the Manipulator **Step 1:** Differentiate $\mathbf{p}$ with respect to $q_1$: $$\frac{\partial \mathbf{p}}{\partial q_1} = \begin{bmatrix} -L_1 \sin q_1 - L_2 \sin(q_1+q_2) - L_3 \sin(q_1+q_2+q_3) \\ L_1 \cos q_1 + L_2 \cos(q_1+q_2) + L_3 \cos(q_1+q_2+q_3) \end{bmatrix}$$ **Step 2:** Differentiate $\mathbf{p}$ with respect to $q_2$: $$\frac{\partial \mathbf{p}}{\partial q_2} = \begin{bmatrix} -L_2 \sin(q_1+q_2) - L_3 \sin(q_1+q_2+q_3) \\ L_2 \cos(q_1+q_2) + L_3 \cos(q_1+q_2+q_3) \end{bmatrix}$$ **Step 3:** Differentiate $\mathbf{p}$ with respect to $q_3$: $$\frac{\partial \mathbf{p}}{\partial q_3} = \begin{bmatrix} -L_3 \sin(q_1+q_2+q_3) \\ L_3 \cos(q_1+q_2+q_3) \end{bmatrix}$$ **Step 4:** Stack the column vectors to form the Jacobian (a $2 \times 3$ matrix, indicating redundancy): $$\mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q}) = \begin{bmatrix} -L_1 \sin q_1 - L_2 \sin(q_1+q_2) - L_3 \sin(q_1+q_2+q_3) & -L_2 \sin(q_1+q_2) - L_3 \sin(q_1+q_2+q_3) & -L_3 \sin(q_1+q_2+q_3) \\ L_1 \cos q_1 + L_2 \cos(q_1+q_2) + L_3 \cos(q_1+q_2+q_3) & L_2 \cos(q_1+q_2) + L_3 \cos(q_1+q_2+q_3) & L_3 \cos(q_1+q_2+q_3) \end{bmatrix}$$ #### Part (b) [7 Marks] — Compute the Generalised Joint Forces (Torques) Using $\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mathbf{J}^T \mathbf{F}$ **Step 1:** Evaluate the trigonometric values at the given configuration: $$\sin 30° = 0.5, \quad \cos 30° \approx 0.866, \quad \sin 45° \approx 0.707, \quad \cos 45° \approx 0.707$$ Intermediate angles: $q_1+q_2 = 75°$, $q_1+q_2+q_3 = 45°$ $$\sin 75° \approx 0.966, \quad \cos 75° \approx 0.259, \quad \sin 45° \approx 0.707, \quad \cos 45° \approx 0.707$$ **Step 2:** Substitute $L_1 = L_2 = L_3 = 1$ m into the Jacobian: $$\mathbf{J} = \begin{bmatrix} -(0.5) - (0.966) - (0.707) & -(0.966) - (0.707) & -(0.707) \\ (0.866) + (0.259) + (0.707) & (0.259) + (0.707) & (0.707) \end{bmatrix}$$ $$\mathbf{J} = \begin{bmatrix} -2.173 & -1.673 & -0.707 \\ 1.832 & 0.966 & 0.707 \end{bmatrix}$$ **Step 3:** Compute the transpose $\mathbf{J}^T$: $$\mathbf{J}^T = \begin{bmatrix} -2.173 & 1.832 \\ -1.673 & 0.966 \\ -0.707 & 0.707 \end{bmatrix}$$ **Step 4:** Compute $\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mathbf{J}^T \mathbf{F}$ with $\mathbf{F} = [10; 5]$: $$\tau_1 = (-2.173)(10) + (1.832)(5) = -21.73 + 9.16 = -12.57 \text{ N·m}$$ $$\tau_2 = (-1.673)(10) + (0.966)(5) = -16.73 + 4.83 = -11.90 \text{ N·m}$$ $$\tau_3 = (-0.707)(10) + (0.707)(5) = -7.07 + 3.535 = -3.535 \text{ N·m}$$ **Final Answer:** $$\boldsymbol{\tau} = \begin{bmatrix} -12.57 \\ -11.90 \\ -3.535 \end{bmatrix} \text{ N·m}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students often **forget to transpose the Jacobian** and write $\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mathbf{J} \mathbf{F}$, which is dimensionally *and* conceptually wrong. The Jacobian is $2 \times 3$; only its $3 \times 2$ transpose can multiply a $2 \times 1$ force to give a $3 \times 1$ torque. Marks lost: **2 out of 7**. Also, students frequently fail to convert degrees to radians before using trigonometric functions in derivations — but since these are *unitless* ratios, degree-mode calculators give correct numerical values. The error arises if derivatives are taken in radians but evaluated in degrees. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Alternative] **A 2-DOF manipulator has a single point mass $m = 2$ kg located at the end-effector. The end-effector position is $\mathbf{r} = [L \cos q_1; \; L \sin q_1] + [0.5 L \cos(q_1+q_2); \; 0.5 L \sin(q_1+q_2)]$ where $L = 1$ m. Gravity acts as $\mathbf{g} = [0; -9.81]^T$ m/s².** #### Part (a) [7 Marks] — Compute the Gravitational Potential Energy and the Generalised Gravitational Forces **Step 1:** Substitute the position into the gravitational potential formula $U_g = m \, \mathbf{g}^T \mathbf{r}$: $$U_g = m \cdot (-9.81) \cdot \left[ L \sin q_1 + 0.5 L \sin(q_1+q_2) \right]$$ **Step 2:** With $m = 2$ kg and $L = 1$ m: $$U_g = -19.62 \left[ \sin q_1 + 0.5 \sin(q_1+q_2) \right] \text{ Joules}$$ **Step 3:** Compute the partial derivative with respect to $q_1$: $$\frac{\partial U_g}{\partial q_1} = -19.62 \left[ \cos q_1 + 0.5 \cos(q_1+q_2) \right]$$ **Step 4:** Compute the partial derivative with respect to $q_2$: $$\frac{\partial U_g}{\partial q_2} = -19.62 \left[ 0.5 \cos(q_1+q_2) \right] = -9.81 \cos(q_1+q_2)$$ **Step 5:** Apply $Q_i = -\frac{\partial U_g}{\partial q_i}$ to obtain the generalised gravitational forces: $$G_1(\mathbf{q}) = 19.62 \left[ \cos q_1 + 0.5 \cos(q_1+q_2) \right] \text{ N·m}$$ $$G_2(\mathbf{q}) = 9.81 \cos(q_1+q_2) \text{ N·m}$$ #### Part (b) [7 Marks] — Verify the Result Using the Jacobian Transpose Method $\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mathbf{J}^T (m\mathbf{g})$ **Step 1:** Compute the Jacobian columns: $$\frac{\partial \mathbf{r}}{\partial q_1} = \begin{bmatrix} -L \sin q_1 - 0.5 L \sin(q_1+q_2) \\ L \cos q_1 + 0.5 L \cos(q_1+q_2) \end{bmatrix}$$ $$\frac{\partial \mathbf{r}}{\partial q_2} = \begin{bmatrix} -0.5 L \sin(q_1+q_2) \\ 0.5 L \cos(q_1+q_2) \end{bmatrix}$$ **Step 2:** Form the Jacobian and its transpose (with $L = 1$): $$\mathbf{J}^T = \begin{bmatrix} -\sin q_1 - 0.5 \sin(q_1+q_2) & \cos q_1 + 0.5 \cos(q_1+q_2) \\ -0.5 \sin(q_1+q_2) & 0.5 \cos(q_1+q_2) \end{bmatrix}$$ **Step 3:** Compute $m\mathbf{g} = 2 \cdot [0; -9.81] = [0; -19.62]$ N. **Step 4:** Compute $\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mathbf{J}^T (m\mathbf{g})$: $$\tau_1 = \left[ \cos q_1 + 0.5 \cos(q_1+q_2) \right] \cdot (-19.62) \cdot (-1) = 19.62 \left[ \cos q_1 + 0.5 \cos(q_1+q_2) \right]$$ $$\tau_2 = \left[ 0.5 \cos(q_1+q_2) \right] \cdot (-19.62) \cdot (-1) = 9.81 \cos(q_1+q_2)$$ **Step 5:** Observe that the two methods yield *identical* results, confirming the consistency of the generalised force definition: $$\boxed{G_1 = 19.62 \left[ \cos q_1 + 0.5 \cos(q_1+q_2) \right] \text{ N·m}, \quad G_2 = 9.81 \cos(q_1+q_2) \text{ N·m}}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** In Part (b), students often forget the **negative sign** of gravity (i.e., write $\mathbf{g} = [0; +9.81]$). This flips the sign of all answers and is a guaranteed **3-mark deduction** since it changes the physical meaning (anti-gravity vs gravity). Also, the factor of $m$ in $m\mathbf{g}$ must be included — students who use $\mathbf{g}$ alone without multiplying by mass will lose 1 mark. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Read Before Exam** - ✅ **Definition:** Generalised force is defined via the **virtual work principle**, NOT as a simple vector projection. Memorise: $Q_i = \sum_j \mathbf{F}_j \cdot \frac{\partial \mathbf{r}_j}{\partial q_i}$. - ✅ **Units:** Coordinate-dependent. **N** for translational coordinates; **N·m** for rotational coordinates. Always state the units explicitly. - ✅ **Jacobian Transpose Identity:** $\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mathbf{J}^T \mathbf{F}_{\text{ext}}$ is the master formula for static force transformation. Remember: it is the **transpose**, not the Jacobian itself. - ✅ **Force–Velocity Duality:** $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{J} \dot{\mathbf{q}}$ (kinematics) and $\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mathbf{J}^T \mathbf{F}$ (statics) are **dual relations** — they arise from the same Jacobian. - ✅ **Power Invariance:** $\mathbf{F}^T \mathbf{v} = \boldsymbol{\tau}^T \dot{\mathbf{q}}$. This is the conceptual proof of the Jacobian transpose method. - ✅ **Conservative Force Shortcut:** If force derives from potential $U$, then $Q_i = -\partial U / \partial q_i$. Use this for **gravity** and **spring forces**. - ✅ **Sign Convention:** Gravity points **downward** ($\mathbf{g} = [0; -9.81; 0]^T$). Watch for sign errors in exam computations. - ✅ **Rotational Generalised Forces** (joint torques) are conjugate to **angular coordinates in radians**, not degrees. Use radians throughout derivations. - ✅ **Equation of Motion Connection:** The generalised force $\boldsymbol{\tau}$ is the right-hand side of $M\ddot{q} + C\dot{q} + G = \boldsymbol{\tau}$. The left side is the generalised **inertial + Coriolis + gravitational** force. - ✅ **D'Alembert's Principle** is the formal bridge: include the inertial force $-m\ddot{\mathbf{r}}$ in the virtual work sum to get the full Lagrangian dynamics. - ✅ **Independence of $\delta q_i$** is the key assumption that allows decoupling the virtual work into $n$ separate scalar equations. - ✅ **Numerical Tip:** For 2-DOF or 3-DOF problems in KTU exams, expect Jacobian entries to be trigonometric functions of $q_1, q_2, q_1+q_2, \ldots$ — simplify using $\sin(\alpha+\beta)$ and $\cos(\alpha+\beta)$ expansions only when required. - ✅ **Common Mistake:** Confusing *generalised force* with *constraint force*. Generalised forces **do** virtual work; ideal constraint forces **do not**. - ✅ **Industrial Relevance:** Mention the Jacobian transpose method in any answer about applications — it is the most frequently asked application question in KTU ESE. - ✅ **Diagrammatic Importance:** Always draw the manipulator schematic with labelled joints, link lengths, and the force vector at the end-effector before starting derivations — KTU examiners award up to **1 mark** for a clear free-body diagram. > [!NOTE] > **Final Exam Advice:** The Jacobian transpose formula $\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mathbf{J}^T \mathbf{F}$ and the gravitational force formula $G_i = -\partial U / \partial q_i$ together account for **~70% of all marks** in this topic. Master them. Draw diagrams. State units. Show all intermediate steps. Good luck!

topics.title: Euler-Lagrange Equation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET722 - ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Euler-Lagrange Equation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Euler-Lagrange Equation (Canonical Form):**
> The Euler-Lagrange Equation is a second-order partial differential equation whose solutions are the *stationary functions* (extremals) of a given **functional**. In the context of Lagrangian mechanics and robotics, it is the mathematical engine that converts a scalar energy-based description of a system — the **Lagrangian $\mathcal{L}(q, \dot{q}, t)$** — into the explicit ordinary differential equations governing the time evolution of the generalized coordinates $q(t)$.

In the 2024 KTU Robotics syllabus, the Euler-Lagrange Equation is the formal bridge between **Hamilton's Principle of Stationary Action** and the dynamical equations of motion of a robotic manipulator. For a single degree-of-freedom (DOF) system, it is written as:

$$\frac{d}{dt}\left(\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \dot{q}}\right) - \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial q} = 0$$

For an $n$-DOF serial manipulator with generalized coordinates $q = [q_1, q_2, \dots, q_n]^T$, this compactly generalizes to a vector of $n$ scalar equations:

$$\frac{d}{dt}\left(\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \dot{q}_i}\right) - \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial q_i} = 0, \quad i = 1, 2, \dots, n$$

where the Lagrangian is defined as the difference between **kinetic energy $\mathcal{K}(q, \dot{q})$** and **potential energy $\mathcal{P}(q)$**:

$$\mathcal{L}(q, \dot{q}, t) = \mathcal{K}(q, \dot{q}) - \mathcal{P}(q)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:**
> The Euler-Lagrange formulation is preferred over Newtonian mechanics for multi-link robotic manipulators because (i) it requires no internal constraint force analysis, (ii) it works in any generalized coordinate frame, and (iii) it scales gracefully as DOF count increases.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Brachistochrone & The Roller Coaster Track**
>
> Imagine you are asked to design the *smoothest possible slide* between two fixed points, $A$ and $B$, separated by both a horizontal and a vertical distance. You can choose any curve you like — a straight line, a parabola, an arc of a circle, or a bizarre zig-zag. Naively, a straight line feels "shortest," but the *fastest* descent under gravity (the famous Brachistochrone problem posed by Johann Bernoulli in 1696) is actually a **cycloid** — a curve traced by a point on a rolling wheel.
>
> Now here is the magical part: you did not solve Newton's second law directly along each candidate curve. Instead, you varied the *entire curve as a single object* and demanded that a single scalar number — the **total travel time**, written as a *functional* $T[y] = \int_{A}^{B} \frac{ds}{v}$ — be stationary (minimum) with respect to small shape perturbations. The differential equation that emerges from enforcing this "shape-sensitivity equals zero" condition is exactly the **Euler-Lagrange Equation**.
>
> **In robotics**, the *path of every joint angle* $q_i(t)$ between an initial configuration and a final configuration plays the same role as the slide's curve. The "score" we minimize is the integral of $\mathcal{L} = \mathcal{K} - \mathcal{P}$. The Euler-Lagrange equation tells us precisely which $q_i(t)$ makes that score stationary — that is, which joint trajectory is *physically realized* by Newton's laws without ever writing a single constraint force.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Quantities (Bolded)

* **Action functional $S[q]$** — scalar whose stationary value defines the true physical trajectory.
* **Generalized coordinate $q_i$** — independent variable parameterizing system configuration.
* **Generalized velocity $\dot{q}_i = dq_i/dt$** — time rate of change of the coordinate.
* **Lagrangian $\mathcal{L}$** — scalar energy difference, units of **Joules (J)** in SI.
* **Generalized force $Q_i$** — appears when non-conservative forces are added; units of **Newton (N)** or **Newton-meter (N·m)** depending on coordinate type.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic of the Euler-Lagrange Derivation

The derivation is built on five structured logical pillars. Each pillar must hold simultaneously for the final equation to be valid.

1. **Statement of Hamilton's Principle (Variational Postulate):** The physical trajectory taken by a holonomic system between two fixed configurations at times $t_1$ and $t_2$ is the one for which the **action functional**
   $$S[q(t)] = \int_{t_1}^{t_2} \mathcal{L}\big(q(t), \dot{q}(t), t\big)\, dt$$
   is stationary with respect to infinitesimal variations $\delta q(t)$ that vanish at the endpoints: $\delta q(t_1) = \delta q(t_2) = 0$.

2. **First Variation of the Functional:** Demand $\delta S = 0$. Expand $\delta \mathcal{L}$ via the multivariate chain rule to separate dependencies on $q$ and $\dot{q}$:
   $$\delta \mathcal{L} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial q}\,\delta q + \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \dot{q}}\,\delta \dot{q}$$

3. **Integration by Parts on the Velocity Term:** Because $\delta \dot{q} = \dfrac{d}{dt}(\delta q)$, integrate the second term by parts in time to remove the derivative from the variation. The boundary term vanishes because $\delta q$ is zero at $t_1, t_2$.

4. **Fundamental Lemma of the Calculus of Variations:** The remaining integral has the form $\int_{t_1}^{t_2} F(t)\,\delta q(t)\, dt = 0$ for an arbitrary smooth $\delta q(t)$. This forces the **integrand $F(t)$ to vanish identically** on the open interval $(t_1, t_2)$.

5. **Assembly of the Differential Equation:** Setting $F(t) = 0$ yields the canonical Euler-Lagrange equation. For multi-DOF systems, the variation is taken independently with respect to each $q_i$, producing $n$ independent scalar equations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Why" Behind the Equation — Physical Interpretation:**
> The term $\partial \mathcal{L}/\partial q$ represents the *generalized force* (more precisely, the negative of the conservative generalized force), while $d/dt(\partial \mathcal{L}/\partial \dot{q})$ represents the *generalized momentum rate*. The Euler-Lagrange equation is therefore a restatement of **Newton's Second Law** $F = d(mv)/dt$ but expressed in *arbitrary* coordinates rather than Cartesian ones. This is precisely why it is so powerful in robotics — we can write the dynamics directly in joint angles without ever needing to compute reaction forces at the joints.

---

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **exam-ready cheat sheet** for any Euler-Lagrange problem in KTU ESE. Memorize the boundary conditions and units.

| Symbol / Equation | Meaning | Boundary Condition / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $S = \int_{t_1}^{t_2} \mathcal{L}(q, \dot{q}, t)\, dt$ | Hamilton's Action Functional | $\delta q(t_1) = \delta q(t_2) = 0$ |
| $\mathcal{L} = \mathcal{K} - \mathcal{P}$ | Lagrangian (Kinetic minus Potential) | Units: **Joules (J)** |
| $\mathcal{K} = \tfrac{1}{2}\dot{q}^{T} M(q) \dot{q}$ | Kinetic Energy (matrix form, $n$-DOF) | $M(q)$ is $n \times n$ symmetric PD matrix |
| $\frac{d}{dt}\left(\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \dot{q}_i}\right) - \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial q_i} = 0$ | Euler-Lagrange Equation (scalar form) | Valid for conservative systems only |
| $\frac{d}{dt}\left(\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \dot{q}_i}\right) - \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial q_i} = Q_i$ | Euler-Lagrange Equation with Non-Conservative Force | $Q_i$ is generalized non-conservative force |
| $p_i = \partial \mathcal{L}/\partial \dot{q}_i$ | Canonical (Generalized) Momentum | Units: **kg·m/s** or **kg·m²/s** |
| $M(q)\ddot{q} + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q) = \tau$ | Standard Manipulator Dynamic Model | This is the *robotics form* of E-L output |
| $\delta S = 0$ | Hamilton's Stationary-Action Principle | First-order necessary condition for physical path |

---

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

* **Industrial Robotics:** Derivation of joint torques $\tau_i$ required to drive a 6-DOF arm along a desired trajectory.
* **Spacecraft Attitude Dynamics:** Satellite orientation control where kinetic energy involves rotation matrices and inertia tensors.
* **Vehicle Suspension Design:** Quarter-car and half-car models where the Lagrangian cleanly incorporates spring and damper energies.
* **Animation & Biomechanics:** Lagrangian-based character skinning and human gait simulations where constraint-free coordinate choices (quaternions, generalized angles) are essential.
* **Optimal Control & Trajectory Planning:** Foundation for Pontryagin's Minimum Principle and the Indirect Methods of optimal trajectory generation.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Scalar Euler-Lagrange Equation

We begin from Hamilton's Principle and execute every algebraic transition explicitly.

**Step 1: Define the action functional.**

$$S[q(t)] = \int_{t_1}^{t_2} \mathcal{L}\big(q(t), \dot{q}(t), t\big)\, dt$$

**Step 2: Introduce an admissible variation $\delta q(t)$.**

The varied path is $q_{\epsilon}(t) = q(t) + \epsilon\,\eta(t)$, where $\eta(t)$ is an arbitrary smooth function with $\eta(t_1) = \eta(t_2) = 0$. The corresponding varied velocity is $\dot{q}_{\epsilon} = \dot{q} + \epsilon\,\dot{\eta}$.

**Step 3: Compute the first variation $\delta S$.**

By definition, $\delta S = \left.\dfrac{dS}{d\epsilon}\right|_{\epsilon=0}$.

$$\frac{dS}{d\epsilon} = \int_{t_1}^{t_2} \left[ \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial q}\frac{dq_{\epsilon}}{d\epsilon} + \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \dot{q}}\frac{d\dot{q}_{\epsilon}}{d\epsilon} + \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial t}\frac{dt}{d\epsilon} \right] dt$$

Since $t$ does not depend on $\epsilon$, $dt/d\epsilon = 0$. Also, $dq_{\epsilon}/d\epsilon = \eta(t)$ and $d\dot{q}_{\epsilon}/d\epsilon = \dot{\eta}(t)$. Setting $\epsilon = 0$:

$$\delta S = \int_{t_1}^{t_2} \left[ \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial q}\,\eta(t) + \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \dot{q}}\,\dot{\eta}(t) \right] dt$$

**Step 4: Integrate the velocity-coupling term by parts.**

Use $u = \partial \mathcal{L}/\partial \dot{q}$ and $dv = \dot{\eta}\,dt$, so $du = \dfrac{d}{dt}\!\left(\dfrac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \dot{q}}\right)dt$ and $v = \eta$.

$$\int_{t_1}^{t_2} \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \dot{q}}\,\dot{\eta}\,dt = \left[\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \dot{q}}\,\eta\right]_{t_1}^{t_2} - \int_{t_1}^{t_2} \frac{d}{dt}\!\left(\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \dot{q}}\right)\eta\,dt$$

**Step 5: Apply the endpoint boundary condition.**

Because $\eta(t_1) = \eta(t_2) = 0$, the boundary term evaluates to zero identically.

**Step 6: Substitute back into $\delta S$ and apply the stationarity condition.**

$$\delta S = \int_{t_1}^{t_2} \left[ \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial q} - \frac{d}{dt}\!\left(\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \dot{q}}\right) \right] \eta(t)\, dt = 0$$

**Step 7: Apply the Fundamental Lemma of the Calculus of Variations.**

Since $\eta(t)$ is arbitrary, the only way the integral can vanish for all admissible variations is if the bracketed coefficient is zero everywhere on $(t_1, t_2)$. Therefore:

$$\boxed{\;\frac{d}{dt}\!\left(\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \dot{q}}\right) - \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial q} = 0\;}$$

**This is the scalar Euler-Lagrange equation.** $\blacksquare$

---

### 3.2 Worked Derivation — Simple Pendulum (KTU-Style Analytical Exercise)

To make the abstract machinery concrete, we apply the Euler-Lagrange equation to a simple pendulum of mass $m$ and length $\ell$ in a uniform gravitational field $g$.

**Step A: Choose the generalized coordinate.**

The system has one DOF. Choose $q = \theta$, the angular displacement from the vertical.

**Step B: Express the kinetic and potential energies.**

The bob's velocity magnitude is $v = \ell \dot{\theta}$, so:

$$\mathcal{K} = \tfrac{1}{2} m \ell^{2} \dot{\theta}^{2}$$

Taking the pivot as zero potential reference, the bob's height is $-\ell \cos\theta$, so:

$$\mathcal{P} = -m g \ell \cos\theta$$

**Step C: Form the Lagrangian.**

$$\mathcal{L} = \mathcal{K} - \mathcal{P} = \tfrac{1}{2} m \ell^{2} \dot{\theta}^{2} + m g \ell \cos\theta$$

**Step D: Compute the partial derivatives.**

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \dot{\theta}} = m \ell^{2} \dot{\theta}$$

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \theta} = -m g \ell \sin\theta$$

**Step E: Apply the Euler-Lagrange equation.**

$$\frac{d}{dt}\!\left(m \ell^{2} \dot{\theta}\right) - \left(-m g \ell \sin\theta\right) = 0$$

$$m \ell^{2} \ddot{\theta} + m g \ell \sin\theta = 0$$

**Step F: Solve / interpret.**

$$\ddot{\theta} + \frac{g}{\ell}\sin\theta = 0$$

For small angles, $\sin\theta \approx \theta$, recovering the simple harmonic oscillator $\ddot{\theta} + (g/\ell)\theta = 0$ with natural frequency $\omega = \sqrt{g/\ell}$. $\blacksquare$

---

### 3.3 Generalization to $n$-DOF Manipulator Dynamics (Vector Form)

For a serial-chain robot with $n$ revolute/prismatic joints and generalized coordinates $q \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$, define $\mathcal{K} = \tfrac{1}{2}\dot{q}^{T} M(q) \dot{q}$ where $M(q)$ is the symmetric positive-definite **mass (inertia) matrix**. If gravity acts through the potential $\mathcal{P}(q)$, then the Lagrangian is $\mathcal{L} = \tfrac{1}{2}\dot{q}^{T} M(q) \dot{q} - \mathcal{P}(q)$.

Carrying out the Euler-Lagrange procedure component-wise:

$$\frac{d}{dt}\!\left(\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \dot{q}_i}\right) - \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial q_i} = \tau_i$$

collects in matrix form as:

$$\boxed{\;M(q)\ddot{q} + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q) = \tau\;}$$

where $M(q)$ is the inertia matrix, $C(q,\dot{q})$ is the Coriolis/centripetal matrix, $G(q)$ is the gravity vector, and $\tau$ is the generalized joint torque vector. This is the canonical **manipulator equation of motion** in the KTU 2024 Robotics syllabus.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Conceptual Flow Diagram — From Physical System to Equation of Motion

The following Mermaid block renders the block-level functional architecture describing how a real robotic mechanism is converted, step-by-step, into its dynamical equations via the Euler-Lagrange framework.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Physical Robotic System n DOF Manipulator] --> B[Step 1 Identify Generalized Coordinates q1 q2 ... qn]
    B --> C[Step 2 Compute Kinetic Energy K of q and dq dt]
    B --> D[Step 3 Compute Potential Energy P of q only]
    C --> E[Step 4 Form Lagrangian L = K minus P]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Step 5 Compute Partial Derivatives partial L by partial qi]
    E --> G[Step 6 Compute Partial Derivatives partial L by partial dqi dt]
    G --> H[Step 7 Time Derivative d dt of partial L by partial dqi dt]
    F --> I[Step 8 Combine via Euler Lagrange Equation]
    H --> I
    I --> J[Step 9 Expand and Group Terms]
    J --> K[Step 10 Rearrange into Standard Form M q ddot q + C q dq dt dq dt + G q = tau]
    K --> L[Final Manipulator Dynamics Equation of Motion]
```

**Reading the diagram:** Each rectangular node represents a logical stage. The two parallel branches (compute $\mathcal{K}$ and compute $\mathcal{P}$) converge at the Lagrangian, after which the partial-derivative and time-derivative branches re-converge at the Euler-Lagrange assembly node, finally producing the standard manipulator form.

---

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Energy Flow Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[Input Stage]
        A1[Generalized Coordinates q]
        A2[Generalized Velocities dq dt]
    end
    subgraph ENERGY[Energy Computation Stage]
        B1[Kinetic Energy K]
        B2[Potential Energy P]
        B3[Lagrangian L = K minus P]
    end
    subgraph VARIATION[Variational Stage]
        C1[partial L by partial q]
        C2[partial L by partial dq dt]
        C3[d dt of partial L by partial dq dt]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT[Output Stage]
        D1[Euler Lagrange Equation = 0]
        D2[Add Non Conservative Q]
        D3[Standard Manipulator Dynamics]
    end
    A1 --> B1
    A2 --> B1
    A1 --> B2
    B1 --> B3
    B2 --> B3
    B3 --> C1
    B3 --> C2
    C2 --> C3
    C1 --> D1
    C3 --> D1
    D1 --> D2
    D2 --> D3
```

**Reading the diagram:** This block diagram mirrors the four stages that the KTU Robotics examiner expects students to articulate verbally: *Input → Energy → Variation → Output*. The non-conservative force $Q$ is shown as an augmentation port (per the generalized form with $Q_i$ on the right-hand side) which becomes the joint torque vector $\tau$ in the manipulator equation.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**State Hamilton's Principle and write the corresponding Euler-Lagrange equation for a holonomic conservative system with $n$ generalized coordinates.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Hamilton's Principle (1 Mark):** Of all the possible paths connecting two fixed configurations at times $t_1$ and $t_2$, the actual path taken by a holonomic conservative system is the one for which the **action functional**
> $$S = \int_{t_1}^{t_2} \mathcal{L}(q, \dot{q}, t)\, dt$$
> is stationary, i.e., $\delta S = 0$, subject to the boundary condition $\delta q(t_1) = \delta q(t_2) = 0$.

> **Euler-Lagrange Equation (2 Marks):** The necessary condition for stationarity yields, for each $i = 1, 2, \dots, n$:
> $$\frac{d}{dt}\!\left(\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \dot{q}_i}\right) - \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial q_i} = 0$$
> This is a system of $n$ second-order ordinary differential equations whose solution gives the equations of motion in the chosen generalized coordinates.

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define the Lagrangian of a mechanical system and explain its physical significance in deriving equations of motion.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Definition (1 Mark):** The Lagrangian $\mathcal{L}$ of a mechanical system is defined as the difference between the total kinetic energy $\mathcal{K}$ and the total potential energy $\mathcal{P}$ of the system:
> $$\mathcal{L}(q, \dot{q}, t) = \mathcal{K}(q, \dot{q}) - \mathcal{P}(q)$$

> **Physical Significance (2 Marks):**
> (i) It is a scalar quantity that contains the complete dynamical information of the system in a coordinate-independent form.
> (ii) It enables derivation of equations of motion without computing internal constraint forces — particularly useful for multi-link robotic manipulators where joint reaction forces are tedious to evaluate.
> (iii) It transforms the vector Newton–Euler framework into a single scalar variational principle, from which the Euler-Lagrange equation automatically extracts the equations of motion in *any* chosen generalized coordinates.

---

### 5.2 Part B — ESE Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a)** Derive the Euler-Lagrange equation from Hamilton's principle of stationary action for a system described by a single generalized coordinate $q(t)$ and Lagrangian $\mathcal{L}(q, \dot{q}, t)$. State clearly the boundary conditions on the variation $\delta q(t)$. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A simple pendulum of mass $m = 2\,\text{kg}$ and length $\ell = 1\,\text{m}$ is suspended from a pivot in a uniform gravitational field $g = 9.81\,\text{m/s}^2$. Using the Euler-Lagrange equation, derive the equation of motion and determine its natural frequency of small oscillation. **[7 Marks]**

---

### **Model Solution for Question A**

**(a) Derivation of the Euler-Lagrange Equation — 7 Marks**

**[1 Mark]** *State Hamilton's principle:* The actual path $q(t)$ between fixed endpoints makes the action $S = \int_{t_1}^{t_2} \mathcal{L}(q, \dot{q}, t)\, dt$ stationary: $\delta S = 0$, with $\delta q(t_1) = \delta q(t_2) = 0$.

**[2 Marks]** *Compute the first variation:* With $q_\epsilon = q + \epsilon \eta$ and $\dot{q}_\epsilon = \dot{q} + \epsilon \dot{\eta}$,

$$\delta S = \left.\frac{dS}{d\epsilon}\right|_{\epsilon=0} = \int_{t_1}^{t_2} \left[ \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial q}\,\eta + \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \dot{q}}\,\dot{\eta} \right] dt$$

**[2 Marks]** *Integrate the second term by parts:*

$$\int_{t_1}^{t_2} \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \dot{q}}\,\dot{\eta}\,dt = \left[\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \dot{q}}\,\eta\right]_{t_1}^{t_2} - \int_{t_1}^{t_2} \frac{d}{dt}\!\left(\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \dot{q}}\right)\eta\,dt$$

Since $\eta(t_1) = \eta(t_2) = 0$, the boundary term vanishes.

**[1 Mark]** *Apply Fundamental Lemma and equate integrand to zero:*

$$\int_{t_1}^{t_2} \left[ \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial q} - \frac{d}{dt}\!\left(\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \dot{q}}\right) \right]\eta\,dt = 0 \;\;\forall\,\eta$$

**[1 Mark]** *Conclude:*

$$\boxed{\;\frac{d}{dt}\!\left(\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \dot{q}}\right) - \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial q} = 0\;}$$

---

**(b) Pendulum Equation of Motion — 7 Marks**

**[1 Mark]** Generalized coordinate: $q = \theta$, the angular displacement.

**[1 Mark]** Kinetic energy: $\mathcal{K} = \tfrac{1}{2} m \ell^{2} \dot{\theta}^{2} = \tfrac{1}{2}(2)(1)^2 \dot{\theta}^{2} = \dot{\theta}^{2}\,\text{J}$.

**[1 Mark]** Potential energy: $\mathcal{P} = -m g \ell \cos\theta = -2(9.81)(1)\cos\theta = -19.62\cos\theta\,\text{J}$.

**[1 Mark]** Lagrangian: $\mathcal{L} = \mathcal{K} - \mathcal{P} = \dot{\theta}^{2} + 19.62\cos\theta$.

**[1 Mark]** Partial derivatives: $\partial \mathcal{L}/\partial \dot{\theta} = 2\dot{\theta}$ and $\partial \mathcal{L}/\partial \theta = -19.62\sin\theta$.

**[1 Mark]** Apply Euler-Lagrange: $\dfrac{d}{dt}(2\dot{\theta}) - (-19.62\sin\theta) = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; 2\ddot{\theta} + 19.62\sin\theta = 0$.

**[1 Mark]** Small-angle approximation ($\sin\theta \approx \theta$): $\ddot{\theta} + (19.62/2)\theta = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; \ddot{\theta} + 9.81\,\theta = 0$. Natural frequency:

$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{g}{\ell}} = \sqrt{\frac{9.81}{1}} = 3.132\,\text{rad/s}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a)** Starting from the Euler-Lagrange equation with non-conservative generalized forces $Q_i$, derive the standard manipulator dynamic model in the form $M(q)\ddot{q} + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q) = \tau$ for an $n$-DOF serial robot. Clearly identify each term. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A 2-DOF planar manipulator has two links of masses $m_1 = m_2 = 1\,\text{kg}$ and lengths $l_1 = l_2 = 1\,\text{m}$, both rotating about horizontal axes in the vertical plane under gravity $g = 9.81\,\text{m/s}^2$. The joint variables are $q_1$ and $q_2$. Compute the kinetic energy of the system (ignoring the joint motor inertias), and write the form of the Lagrangian and the corresponding Euler-Lagrange equations. **[7 Marks]**

---

### **Model Solution for Question B**

**(a) Derivation of Manipulator Dynamic Model — 7 Marks**

**[1 Mark]** *Start:* Euler-Lagrange with non-conservative forces,
$$\frac{d}{dt}\!\left(\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \dot{q}_i}\right) - \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial q_i} = Q_i, \quad i = 1, \dots, n$$

**[2 Marks]** *Substitute* $\mathcal{L} = \mathcal{K} - \mathcal{P}$ with $\mathcal{K} = \tfrac{1}{2}\dot{q}^{T} M(q) \dot{q}$. Carrying out the partial derivatives and time derivatives and using the symmetry of $M$:

$$\sum_{j} M_{ij}(q)\ddot{q}_j + \sum_{j,k} \Gamma_{ijk}(q)\dot{q}_j \dot{q}_k + \frac{\partial \mathcal{P}}{\partial q_i} = Q_i$$

where $\Gamma_{ijk} = \tfrac{1}{2}(\partial M_{ij}/\partial q_k + \partial M_{ik}/\partial q_j - \partial M_{jk}/\partial q_i)$ are the **Christoffel symbols**.

**[1 Mark]** *Define* the Coriolis/centripetal matrix element $C_{ij} = \sum_k \Gamma_{ijk}\dot{q}_k$ and the gravity vector $G_i = \partial \mathcal{P}/\partial q_i$.

**[1 Mark]** *Identify* the generalized force as the joint torque vector $\tau_i = Q_i$.

**[1 Mark]** *Combine and rearrange into standard form:*

$$\boxed{\;M(q)\ddot{q} + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q) = \tau\;}$$

**[1 Mark]** *Identify each term:*
- $M(q)\in\mathbb{R}^{n\times n}$ — symmetric positive-definite inertia matrix.
- $C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q}\in\mathbb{R}^n$ — Coriolis and centripetal vector.
- $G(q)\in\mathbb{R}^n$ — gravitational torque vector.
- $\tau\in\mathbb{R}^n$ — generalized joint torque/force vector.

---

**(b) 2-DOF Planar Manipulator — 7 Marks**

**[2 Marks]** *Position of link 1 CoM:* $x_1 = \tfrac{l_1}{2}\cos q_1$, $y_1 = \tfrac{l_1}{2}\sin q_1$.

**[1 Mark]** *Position of link 2 CoM:* $x_2 = l_1\cos q_1 + \tfrac{l_2}{2}\cos(q_1+q_2)$, $y_2 = l_1\sin q_1 + \tfrac{l_2}{2}\sin(q_1+q_2)$.

**[2 Marks]** *Compute velocities and kinetic energies:*

$$v_1^2 = \left(\tfrac{l_1}{2}\dot{q}_1\right)^2 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \mathcal{K}_1 = \tfrac{1}{2}m_1\!\left(\tfrac{l_1}{2}\right)^2\dot{q}_1^2 = 0.125\,\dot{q}_1^2$$

$$v_2^2 = \left[l_1\dot{q}_1 + \tfrac{l_2}{2}(\dot{q}_1+\dot{q}_2)\cos q_2\right]^2 + \left[\tfrac{l_2}{2}(\dot{q}_1+\dot{q}_2)\sin q_2\right]^2$$

Simplifying: $v_2^2 = l_1^2 \dot{q}_1^2 + l_1 l_2 \dot{q}_1(\dot{q}_1+\dot{q}_2)\cos q_2 + \tfrac{l_2^2}{4}(\dot{q}_1+\dot{q}_2)^2$.

With $l_1=l_2=1$, $m_2=1$:
$$\mathcal{K}_2 = \tfrac{1}{2}\!\left[\dot{q}_1^2 + \dot{q}_1(\dot{q}_1+\dot{q}_2)\cos q_2 + \tfrac{1}{4}(\dot{q}_1+\dot{q}_2)^2\right]$$

**[1 Mark]** *Total kinetic energy:* $\mathcal{K} = \mathcal{K}_1 + \mathcal{K}_2$.

**[1 Mark]** *Potential energy:* $\mathcal{P} = m_1 g \tfrac{l_1}{2}\sin q_1 + m_2 g [l_1 \sin q_1 + \tfrac{l_2}{2}\sin(q_1+q_2)] = 9.81[1.5\sin q_1 + 0.5\sin(q_1+q_2)]\,\text{J}$.

**[1 Mark]** *Form Lagrangian* $\mathcal{L} = \mathcal{K} - \mathcal{P}$ and write the two Euler-Lagrange equations:
$$\frac{d}{dt}\!\left(\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \dot{q}_1}\right) - \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial q_1} = 0, \qquad \frac{d}{dt}\!\left(\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \dot{q}_2}\right) - \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial q_2} = 0$$

(The explicit numerical expansion is typically expected by KTU examiners only for the simpler 2-DOF case; the symbolic form above earns full marks with appropriate kinetic-energy derivation.)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Typically Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the boundary condition** on $\delta q$ during the derivation part — this is worth a full **1 mark** and is the single most common deduction.
> 2. **Sign error in the Lagrangian definition:** KTU strictly follows $\mathcal{L} = \mathcal{K} - \mathcal{P}$. Writing $\mathcal{L} = \mathcal{P} - \mathcal{K}$ is a **2-mark penalty**.
> 3. **Conflating $\partial \mathcal{L}/\partial q_i$ with the generalized force:** The generalized force is actually $-\partial \mathcal{L}/\partial q_i = -\partial \mathcal{P}/\partial q_i$ in conservative systems; the equation is *force balance*, so the sign must be exact.
> 4. **Skipping the Coriolis derivation** in the manipulator form: Christoffel symbols must appear explicitly. A directly-written final manipulator form without derivation loses up to **3 marks**.
> 5. **Unit inconsistency:** Forgetting that lengths must be in **meters**, masses in **kg**, and angles in **radians** when computing numerical natural frequencies.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Summary — Euler-Lagrange Equation**

* **Core Postulate:** Hamilton's Principle $\delta S = 0$ with $\delta q(t_1) = \delta q(t_2) = 0$.
* **Canonical Form (Scalar):** $\dfrac{d}{dt}\!\left(\dfrac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \dot{q}}\right) - \dfrac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial q} = 0$.
* **Canonical Form (Vector, $n$-DOF):** $\dfrac{d}{dt}\!\left(\dfrac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \dot{q}_i}\right) - \dfrac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial q_i} = 0, \;\; i = 1, \dots, n$.
* **Lagrangian Definition:** $\mathcal{L} = \mathcal{K} - \mathcal{P}$ (kinetic minus potential).
* **With Non-Conservative Forces:** Add $Q_i$ to the right-hand side; in robotics, $Q_i = \tau_i$.
* **Kinetic Energy Standard Form:** $\mathcal{K} = \tfrac{1}{2}\dot{q}^{T} M(q) \dot{q}$.
* **Standard Manipulator Form:** $M(q)\ddot{q} + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q) = \tau$.
* **Christoffel Symbols:** $\Gamma_{ijk} = \tfrac{1}{2}\!\left(\dfrac{\partial M_{ij}}{\partial q_k} + \dfrac{\partial M_{ik}}{\partial q_j} - \dfrac{\partial M_{jk}}{\partial q_i}\right)$.
* **Generalized Momentum:** $p_i = \partial \mathcal{L}/\partial \dot{q}_i$.
* **Why E-L over Newton-Euler in Robotics:** Avoids internal joint reaction force computation; coordinate-independent; scales with DOF.
* **Key Derivation Tools to Memorize:** First variation $\delta S$, integration by parts on the velocity term, Fundamental Lemma of Calculus of Variations.
* **Boundary Condition:** Always state $\delta q(t_1) = \delta q(t_2) = 0$ explicitly in derivations.
* **Sign Convention:** Conservative force appears as $-\partial \mathcal{P}/\partial q_i$; KTU follows this strictly.
* **Units Audit:** $\mathcal{L}$ in Joules, $\tau$ in N·m (revolute) or N (prismatic), $M(q)$ in kg·m².
* **Common KTU Pitfalls:** Sign error in Lagrangian; forgetting Christoffel derivation; missing boundary condition statement; failing to convert angles to radians in numerical problems.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Dynamic Model of Planar and Spatial Serial Robots up to 2 DOF
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET722 - ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Dynamic Model of Planar and Spatial Serial Robots up to 2 DOF

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🔷 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Dynamic Model of a Serial Robot (KTU Standard Definition):**
> The *dynamic model* of a serial manipulator is the set of non-linear, coupled, second-order Ordinary Differential Equations (ODEs) that relate the **generalized joint forces/torques** $\boldsymbol{\tau} \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ acting at the actuated joints to the resulting **generalized joint positions** $\mathbf{q} \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$, **velocities** $\dot{\mathbf{q}} \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$, and **accelerations** $\ddot{\mathbf{q}} \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$. For a manipulator with $n$ degrees of freedom (DOF), it is universally expressed in the canonical manipulator form:

$$
\mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})\ddot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{C}(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}})\dot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{G}(\mathbf{q}) = \boldsymbol{\tau}
$$

where:
- $\mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q}) \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$ — **Symmetric Positive-Definite (SPD) Inertia / Mass Matrix**
- $\mathbf{C}(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}}) \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$ — **Coriolis and Centrifugal Coefficient Matrix**
- $\mathbf{G}(\mathbf{q}) \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ — **Gravitational Torque Vector**
- $\boldsymbol{\tau} \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ — **Generalized Joint Force/Torque Vector** (includes actuator efforts and friction)

For the KTU 2024 Module 3 scope, $n \le 2$.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Why" of Dynamics

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition (Plain English Analogy):**
> Imagine a **human arm holding a heavy bucket of water**. 
> - **Kinematics** tells you *where* the bucket is in space given your shoulder and elbow angles. 
> - **Statics** tells you *how much force* your muscles must apply *at a single frozen instant* to hold the bucket still.
> - **Dynamics**, however, asks the real question: *What muscle torques are needed to swing the bucket up smoothly, accounting for the swinging motion, the spinning water, the weight, and the centripetal "pull" of the elbow on the shoulder?*
> 
> In a robot, gravity (the bucket's weight), Coriolis forces (the bucket swinging sideways as you accelerate the elbow), and centrifugal forces (the bucket "flying outward" when you spin your shoulder fast) all couple the two joints. The dynamic model mathematically *unfolds* all these hidden effects into a clean equation.

## 1.3 Why is the Dynamic Model Critical in Engineering?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The dynamic model is the **backbone of all advanced robotic control and simulation**. KTU examiners frequently test this concept because it is the gateway to:
> 1. **Computed Torque Control** (Model-Based Control): Cancels $M, C, G$ to linearize the robot.
> 2. **Trajectory Planning** with Time-Optimality (e.g., minimum-time bang-bang control).
> 3. **Actuator Sizing** — choosing motors strong enough for worst-case torques.
> 4. **Digital-Twin Simulation** — software-in-the-loop testing before deployment.
> 5. **Structural / Mechanical Design** — predicting deflection, vibration, fatigue.

## 1.4 Standard KTU 2024 Approach: The Lagrangian Method

The KTU Module 3 syllabus prescribes the **Euler–Lagrange (Energy-based) formulation** for low-DOF (up to 2 DOF) robots. The standard procedure is:

1. Assign **Denavit–Hartenberg (D-H) parameters** and obtain COM positions.
2. Compute the **Kinetic Energy** $T = \sum T_i$ (sum of translational + rotational).
3. Compute the **Potential Energy** $V = \sum V_i$ (gravitational).
4. Form the **Lagrangian** $L = T - V$.
5. Apply the **Euler–Lagrange equation** for each generalized coordinate $q_i$:

$$
\frac{d}{dt}\left(\frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{q}_i}\right) - \frac{\partial L}{\partial q_i} = \tau_i
$$

6. Rearrange into the canonical form $\mathbf{M}\ddot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{C}\dot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{G} = \boldsymbol{\tau}$.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🔷 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Mathematical Pillars of Robot Dynamics

| Approach | Core Idea | KTU Exam Frequency | Best For |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Lagrangian (Energy)** | $L = T - V$; scalar energy approach | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ (Highest) | Low DOF (≤ 2), symbolic derivation |
| **Newton–Euler (Force-Balance)** | Recursive $\mathbf{F} = m\mathbf{a}$ & $\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mathbf{I}\boldsymbol{\alpha}$ | ⭐⭐⭐ | High DOF, real-time computation |

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Operational Logic (Lagrangian Recipe)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why each step works (the 'How' and 'Why'):**

1. **Express positions of all link COMs** in the *base frame* as functions of $\mathbf{q}$.  
   *Why:* Kinetic energy is a function of velocities, which are derivatives of positions.
2. **Differentiate** to get velocities $\Rightarrow$ compute $v_i^2$ and angular velocities $\boldsymbol{\omega}_i$.  
   *Why:* $T_i = \frac{1}{2}m_i v_i^2 + \frac{1}{2}\boldsymbol{\omega}_i^\top \mathbf{I}_i \boldsymbol{\omega}_i$.
3. **Form the scalar** $T(\mathbf{q}, \dot{\mathbf{q}})$ and $V(\mathbf{q})$; assemble $L$.  
   *Why:* The Lagrangian isolates the "inertial minus gravitational" content of the system.
4. **Apply the Euler–Lagrange operator** for each joint.  
   *Why:* This is Hamilton's Principle of Stationary Action; it automatically enforces Newton's laws in generalized coordinates.
5. **Collect terms** in $\ddot{\mathbf{q}}$, $\dot{\mathbf{q}}$, and constant.  
   *Why:* Exposes the **Mass**, **Coriolis**, and **Gravity** matrices used in control.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master the following equations — they are KTU "must-knows" for Module 3.**

| Symbol | Formula | Meaning / Units |
|---|---|---|
| Lagrangian | $L = T - V$ | Scalar energy function [J] |
| Euler–Lagrange | $\dfrac{d}{dt}\!\left(\dfrac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{q}_i}\right) - \dfrac{\partial L}{\partial q_i} = \tau_i$ | One ODE per joint |
| Canonical Form | $\mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})\ddot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{C}(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}})\dot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{G}(\mathbf{q}) = \boldsymbol{\tau}$ | Standard $n$-DOF robot dynamics |
| Trans. KE (link $i$) | $T_{i,\text{trans}} = \tfrac{1}{2} m_i v_{c_i}^2$ | Linear motion KE [J] |
| Rot. KE (link $i$) | $T_{i,\text{rot}} = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\boldsymbol{\omega}_i^\top \mathbf{I}_i \boldsymbol{\omega}_i$ | Spinning KE [J] |
| Inertia Tensor (body) | $\mathbf{I}_i = \begin{bmatrix} I_{xx} & -I_{xy} & -I_{xz} \\ -I_{yx} & I_{yy} & -I_{yz} \\ -I_{zx} & -I_{zy} & I_{zz} \end{bmatrix}$ | Inertia about COM in body frame [kg·m²] |
| PE (uniform $g$) | $V_i = m_i g h_{c_i}$ | $h_{c_i}$ = COM height [m] |
| Christoffel symbol | $c_{ijk} = \tfrac{1}{2}\!\left(\dfrac{\partial m_{ij}}{\partial q_k} + \dfrac{\partial m_{ik}}{\partial q_j} - \dfrac{\partial m_{jk}}{\partial q_i}\right)$ | Entry of Coriolis matrix |
| Coriolis entry | $C_{ij} = \sum_{k=1}^{n} c_{ijk} \dot{q}_k$ | Component of $\mathbf{C}$ |
| Gravity entry | $G_i = \dfrac{\partial V}{\partial q_i}$ | Component of $\mathbf{G}$ |
| Skew-symmetry | $\dot{\mathbf{M}} - 2\mathbf{C}$ is **skew-symmetric** | Passivity property of robot |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Properties of $\mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})$ (Examiner favourite):**
> 1. $\mathbf{M}$ is **symmetric**: $\mathbf{M} = \mathbf{M}^\top$.
> 2. $\mathbf{M}$ is **positive-definite**: $\mathbf{x}^\top \mathbf{M} \mathbf{x} > 0$ for all $\mathbf{x} \neq \mathbf{0}$.
> 3. Therefore $\mathbf{M}$ is always **invertible** — crucial for the *Computed Torque Controller*: $\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mathbf{M}(\mathbf{M}^{-1}\mathbf{u} - \mathbf{C}\dot{\mathbf{q}} - \mathbf{G})$.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Industrial Welding Robots (e.g., KUKA, FANUC):** The 2-DOF dynamic model is the building block of their full 6-DOF controllers; the same $\mathbf{M}, \mathbf{C}, \mathbf{G}$ pattern *scales up*.
- **Space Manipulators (Canadarm):** Micro-gravity ($g \approx 0$) ⇒ $\mathbf{G} \to 0$; the model simplifies to $\mathbf{M}\ddot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{C}\dot{\mathbf{q}} = \boldsymbol{\tau}$.
- **Humanoid Robots (e.g., ASIMO):** Lagrangian dynamics underpin the whole-body controller (WBC) that balances against gravity.
- **CNC Machine Tool Feed Drives:** Dynamics of each axis (treated as 1-DOF prismatic) predict chatter and required servo stiffness.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🔷 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Problem Setup: Planar 2R Robot (Reference Case)

We will derive the full dynamic model for the **canonical 2R planar manipulator** (the KTU "go-to" question).

**Given (per KTU standard problem):**
- Link 1: mass $m_1$, length $l_1$, COM at distance $l_{c1}$ from joint 1, moment of inertia $I_1$ about its COM (about $\hat{z}$ axis, out of plane).
- Link 2: mass $m_2$, length $l_2$, COM at distance $l_{c2}$ from joint 2, moment of inertia $I_2$ about its COM.
- Joint angles: $\mathbf{q} = \begin{bmatrix} \theta_1 \\ \theta_2 \end{bmatrix}$; both revolute, both rotating in the horizontal plane.
- Gravity $g$ acting along $-\hat{y}$ (vertical down).

> [!NOTE]
> **Geometric diagram (mental picture):**
> - Base frame $\{0\}$ at joint 1, $\hat{x}_0$ horizontal, $\hat{y}_0$ vertical up.
> - Link 1 makes angle $\theta_1$ with $\hat{x}_0$.
> - Link 2 makes angle $\theta_1 + \theta_2$ with $\hat{x}_0$ (relative angle $\theta_2$ between links).

## 3.2 Step 1 — Position of Centre of Mass of Each Link

The position vector of the COM of link 1 (in base frame):

$$
\mathbf{p}_{c1} = \begin{bmatrix} l_{c1}\cos\theta_1 \\ l_{c1}\sin\theta_1 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

The position vector of the COM of link 2 (in base frame):

$$
\mathbf{p}_{c2} = \begin{bmatrix} l_1 \cos\theta_1 + l_{c2}\cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2) \\ l_1 \sin\theta_1 + l_{c2}\sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2) \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

*Reasoning:* Link 2's COM is the vector sum of link 1's end-effector (at $l_1$ along link 1) and the offset along link 2 (at $l_{c2}$).

## 3.3 Step 2 — Velocities by Direct Time Differentiation

Differentiate $\mathbf{p}_{c1}$ with respect to time (chain rule):

$$
\dot{\mathbf{p}}_{c1} = \begin{bmatrix} -l_{c1}\dot{\theta}_1 \sin\theta_1 \\ \phantom{-}l_{c1}\dot{\theta}_1 \cos\theta_1 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Speed squared of COM 1:

$$
v_{c1}^2 = \dot{\mathbf{p}}_{c1}^\top \dot{\mathbf{p}}_{c1} = l_{c1}^2 \dot{\theta}_1^2
$$

Differentiate $\mathbf{p}_{c2}$ with respect to time:

$$
\dot{\mathbf{p}}_{c2} = \begin{bmatrix} -l_1 \dot{\theta}_1 \sin\theta_1 - l_{c2}(\dot{\theta}_1 + \dot{\theta}_2)\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2) \\ \phantom{-}l_1 \dot{\theta}_1 \cos\theta_1 + l_{c2}(\dot{\theta}_1 + \dot{\theta}_2)\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2) \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

Speed squared of COM 2 (after algebraic expansion):

$$
v_{c2}^2 = l_1^2 \dot{\theta}_1^2 + l_{c2}^2 (\dot{\theta}_1 + \dot{\theta}_2)^2 + 2 l_1 l_{c2}\,\dot{\theta}_1(\dot{\theta}_1+\dot{\theta}_2)\cos\theta_2
$$

*Reasoning for the cosine term:* Use $\cos(A-B) = \cos A \cos B + \sin A \sin B$; the dot product of the two velocity components produces a $\cos\theta_2$ cross-term.

## 3.4 Step 3 — Angular Velocities (Planar Case)

In a planar (2D) rotation about the $\hat{z}$ axis, the angular velocity of link $i$ is:

$$
\boldsymbol{\omega}_1 = \dot{\theta}_1\,\hat{z}, \qquad \boldsymbol{\omega}_2 = (\dot{\theta}_1 + \dot{\theta}_2)\,\hat{z}
$$

Magnitude squared: $\omega_1^2 = \dot{\theta}_1^2$, $\omega_2^2 = (\dot{\theta}_1 + \dot{\theta}_2)^2$.

## 3.5 Step 4 — Total Kinetic Energy

Translational KE of link 1:

$$
T_{1,\text{trans}} = \tfrac{1}{2} m_1 l_{c1}^2 \dot{\theta}_1^2
$$

Rotational KE of link 1:

$$
T_{1,\text{rot}} = \tfrac{1}{2} I_1 \dot{\theta}_1^2
$$

Translational KE of link 2:

$$
T_{2,\text{trans}} = \tfrac{1}{2} m_2\!\left[ l_1^2 \dot{\theta}_1^2 + l_{c2}^2 (\dot{\theta}_1 + \dot{\theta}_2)^2 + 2 l_1 l_{c2}\,\dot{\theta}_1(\dot{\theta}_1+\dot{\theta}_2)\cos\theta_2 \right]
$$

Rotational KE of link 2:

$$
T_{2,\text{rot}} = \tfrac{1}{2} I_2 (\dot{\theta}_1 + \dot{\theta}_2)^2
$$

**Total Kinetic Energy** $T = T_{1,\text{trans}} + T_{1,\text{rot}} + T_{2,\text{trans}} + T_{2,\text{rot}}$:

$$
T = \tfrac{1}{2}\!\left[I_1 + m_1 l_{c1}^2 + m_2 l_1^2\right]\dot{\theta}_1^2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\!\left[I_2 + m_2 l_{c2}^2\right](\dot{\theta}_1+\dot{\theta}_2)^2 + m_2 l_1 l_{c2}\,\dot{\theta}_1(\dot{\theta}_1+\dot{\theta}_2)\cos\theta_2
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why expand?** Multiplying out $(\dot{\theta}_1+\dot{\theta}_2)^2 = \dot{\theta}_1^2 + 2\dot{\theta}_1\dot{\theta}_2 + \dot{\theta}_2^2$ is essential to recognize the matrix form. Let us re-arrange to expose $\mathbf{M}$:

$$
T = \tfrac{1}{2}\!\left[\,m_{11}\,\dot{\theta}_1^2 + 2\,m_{12}\,\dot{\theta}_1\dot{\theta}_2 + m_{22}\,\dot{\theta}_2^2\,\right]
$$

Comparing coefficients, we directly identify the **Mass Matrix** entries:

$$
\boxed{
\begin{aligned}
m_{11} &= I_1 + m_1 l_{c1}^2 + m_2 l_1^2 + m_2 l_{c2}^2 + 2 m_2 l_1 l_{c2}\cos\theta_2 \\[2pt]
m_{12} &= m_{21} = m_2 l_{c2}^2 + m_2 l_1 l_{c2}\cos\theta_2 \\[2pt]
m_{22} &= I_2 + m_2 l_{c2}^2
\end{aligned}
}
$$

*General rule (Spong et al.):* The mass matrix is obtained by **collecting** the coefficients of $\tfrac{1}{2}\dot{q}_i \dot{q}_j$ in the expanded $T$.

## 3.6 Step 5 — Total Potential Energy

Height of COM 1: $h_{c1} = l_{c1}\sin\theta_1$.  
Height of COM 2: $h_{c2} = l_1 \sin\theta_1 + l_{c2}\sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2)$.

$$
V = m_1 g\,l_{c1}\sin\theta_1 + m_2 g\!\left[l_1 \sin\theta_1 + l_{c2}\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2)\right]
$$

## 3.7 Step 6 — The Euler–Lagrange Equations

For joint 1: $i = 1$:

$$
\frac{d}{dt}\!\left(\frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{\theta}_1}\right) - \frac{\partial L}{\partial \theta_1} = \tau_1
$$

For joint 2: $i = 2$:

$$
\frac{d}{dt}\!\left(\frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{\theta}_2}\right) - \frac{\partial L}{\partial \theta_2} = \tau_2
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **These two equations will produce the final canonical form.** Each $\tau_i$ on the right-hand side is the *sum of* the corresponding row of $\mathbf{M}\ddot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{C}\dot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{G}$. The next three steps show the explicit evaluation.

### 3.7.1 Compute $\partial T / \partial \dot{\theta}_1$ and $\partial T / \partial \dot{\theta}_2$

Using $T = \tfrac{1}{2}\!\left[\,m_{11}\dot{\theta}_1^2 + 2 m_{12}\dot{\theta}_1\dot{\theta}_2 + m_{22}\dot{\theta}_2^2\,\right]$:

$$
\frac{\partial T}{\partial \dot{\theta}_1} = m_{11}\dot{\theta}_1 + m_{12}\dot{\theta}_2
$$

$$
\frac{\partial T}{\partial \dot{\theta}_2} = m_{12}\dot{\theta}_1 + m_{22}\dot{\theta}_2
$$

### 3.7.2 Time Derivatives of These (Christoffel Route)

$$
\frac{d}{dt}\!\left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial \dot{\theta}_1}\right) = m_{11}\ddot{\theta}_1 + m_{12}\ddot{\theta}_2 + \dot{m}_{11}\dot{\theta}_1 + \dot{m}_{12}\dot{\theta}_2
$$

$$
\frac{d}{dt}\!\left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial \dot{\theta}_2}\right) = m_{12}\ddot{\theta}_1 + m_{22}\ddot{\theta}_2 + \dot{m}_{12}\dot{\theta}_1 + \dot{m}_{22}\dot{\theta}_2
$$

Time-derivatives of mass entries ($\theta_2$ is the only configuration variable here, and $\dot{\theta}_2$ is non-zero in general):

$$
\dot{m}_{11} = -2 m_2 l_1 l_{c2} \sin\theta_2 \cdot \dot{\theta}_2
$$

$$
\dot{m}_{12} = \dot{m}_{21} = -m_2 l_1 l_{c2} \sin\theta_2 \cdot \dot{\theta}_2
$$

$$
\dot{m}_{22} = 0
$$

### 3.7.3 Compute $\partial T / \partial \theta_1$ and $\partial T / \partial \theta_2$

Note that $T$ depends on $\theta_1$ only through $\dot{\theta}_1$ (kinematic), and on $\theta_2$ through the cos terms:

$$
\frac{\partial T}{\partial \theta_1} = 0
$$

$$
\frac{\partial T}{\partial \theta_2} = -m_2 l_1 l_{c2}\,\dot{\theta}_1(\dot{\theta}_1 + \dot{\theta}_2)\sin\theta_2
$$

### 3.7.4 Compute $\partial V / \partial \theta_1$ and $\partial V / \partial \theta_2$

$$
\frac{\partial V}{\partial \theta_1} = m_1 g\,l_{c1}\cos\theta_1 + m_2 g\,l_1 \cos\theta_1 + m_2 g\,l_{c2}\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2)
$$

$$
\frac{\partial V}{\partial \theta_2} = m_2 g\,l_{c2}\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2)
$$

## 3.8 Step 7 — Assembling the Canonical Form

After substituting all the above into the Euler–Lagrange equations and rearranging, the planar 2R dynamic model in canonical form is:

$$
\boxed{
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q}) &= \begin{bmatrix} m_{11} & m_{12} \\ m_{12} & m_{22} \end{bmatrix} \\[4pt]
\mathbf{C}(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}}) &= \begin{bmatrix} h\,\dot{\theta}_2 & h\,(\dot{\theta}_1+\dot{\theta}_2) \\ -h\,\dot{\theta}_1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}, \quad h = -m_2 l_1 l_{c2}\sin\theta_2 \\[4pt]
\mathbf{G}(\mathbf{q}) &= \begin{bmatrix} (m_1 l_{c1} + m_2 l_1)g\cos\theta_1 + m_2 l_{c2} g\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2) \\ m_2 l_{c2} g\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2) \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
}
$$

with the **Mass Matrix** entries already defined in §3.5.

> [!NOTE]
> **Useful sanity check:** If the arm moves only in a horizontal plane ($g = 0$), then $\mathbf{G} = \mathbf{0}$ and the dynamics reduces to $\mathbf{M}\ddot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{C}\dot{\mathbf{q}} = \boldsymbol{\tau}$ — exactly the "space-manipulator-like" form. KTU examiners often test this simplification.

## 3.9 Spatial 2R Robot (2-DOF, Out-of-Plane Joints)

For a **spatial 2R manipulator** (e.g., the shoulder yaw + shoulder pitch of a humanoid arm), the **kinetic energy** must be computed with the full $3\times 3$ body inertia tensor $\mathbf{I}_i$:

$$
T_{i,\text{rot}} = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\boldsymbol{\omega}_i^\top \mathbf{I}_i \boldsymbol{\omega}_i
$$

For a generic 2R spatial chain, let $\mathbf{R}_i(\mathbf{q})$ be the rotation matrix from the COM frame of link $i$ to the base frame, and $\mathbf{J}_{v_i}, \mathbf{J}_{\omega_i}$ the linear and angular parts of the geometric Jacobian. The kinetic energy **always** has the compact form:

$$
T = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\dot{\mathbf{q}}^\top \mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})\,\dot{\mathbf{q}}, \quad \text{with}\ \mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q}) = \sum_{i=1}^{n}\!\left[\,m_i \mathbf{J}_{v_i}^\top \mathbf{J}_{v_i} + \mathbf{J}_{\omega_i}^\top \mathbf{R}_i \mathbf{I}_i \mathbf{R}_i^\top \mathbf{J}_{\omega_i}\,\right]
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For the KTU exam (2-DOF spatial case) the typical sub-questions are:**
> 1. State the inertia tensor in the body frame (with products of inertia $I_{xy}, I_{xz}, I_{yz}$).
> 2. Express the angular velocity of link 2 as $\boldsymbol{\omega}_2 = \mathbf{J}_{\omega_2}\dot{\mathbf{q}}$.
> 3. Compute the entry $m_{12}$ of $\mathbf{M}$ and show it is symmetric.
> 4. Identify the **gravity vector** $\mathbf{G}$ using $V_i = m_i g \hat{g}^\top \mathbf{p}_{c_i}$, where $\hat{g}$ is the unit gravity vector in the base frame.

## 3.10 Extension: Planar RP (1R + 1P) 2-DOF Robot

A common KTU "variant" question: replace the second revolute joint with a **prismatic** joint of variable length $d_2$.

- COM 1 position: $\mathbf{p}_{c1} = \begin{bmatrix} l_{c1}\cos\theta_1 \\ l_{c1}\sin\theta_1 \end{bmatrix}$ — unchanged.
- COM 2 position: $\mathbf{p}_{c2} = \begin{bmatrix} l_1\cos\theta_1 + (l_{c2} + d_2)\cos\theta_1 \\ l_1\sin\theta_1 + (l_{c2} + d_2)\sin\theta_1 \end{bmatrix}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters:** A *prismatic* joint contributes $\dot{q}_2$ to the linear velocity of link 2 but **does not contribute to $\boldsymbol{\omega}_2$**. Hence the rotational KE of link 2 vanishes (for a sliding block with $I_2 = 0$ about the sliding axis), simplifying $m_{22}$ and the Christoffel symbols. KTU exams love this twist.

## 4.4 Energy-Equivalence Truth Table (for Sanity Check)

| Configuration | $V$ | $\mathbf{G}$ | Reduces to |
|---|---|---|---|
| Horizontal plane motion | $= 0$ | $\mathbf{0}$ | $\mathbf{M}\ddot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{C}\dot{\mathbf{q}} = \boldsymbol{\tau}$ |
| Static (quasi-static) | non-zero | $\mathbf{G} = \dfrac{\partial V}{\partial \mathbf{q}}$ | $\boldsymbol{\tau}_{\text{static}} = \mathbf{G}$ |
| Constant $\dot{\mathbf{q}} \to 0$ | non-zero | non-zero | $\mathbf{M}\ddot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{G} = \boldsymbol{\tau}$ (gravity dominates) |
| All-zero initial state | depends on $g$ | non-zero | $\boldsymbol{\tau}(0) = \mathbf{G}(\mathbf{q}_0)$ (holding torque) |
