# 🔷 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 3, Q1(a)]**

**Q1(a).** *Define the terms (i) Mass matrix $\mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})$, (ii) Coriolis matrix $\mathbf{C}(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}})$, and (iii) Gravity vector $\mathbf{G}(\mathbf{q})$ in the context of robot dynamics. State two important properties of the mass matrix.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

(i) The **Mass (Inertia) matrix** $\mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q}) \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$ is the symmetric positive-definite matrix that multiplies $\ddot{\mathbf{q}}$ in the manipulator equation; it represents the *effective inertia* seen at the joints as a function of configuration. It can be written as $\mathbf{M} = \sum_i \left[m_i \mathbf{J}_{v_i}^\top \mathbf{J}_{v_i} + \mathbf{J}_{\omega_i}^\top \mathbf{R}_i \mathbf{I}_i \mathbf{R}_i^\top \mathbf{J}_{\omega_i}\right]$.

(ii) The **Coriolis and Centrifugal matrix** $\mathbf{C}(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}}) \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$ contains the velocity-dependent terms; its $(i,j)$ entry is the sum $C_{ij} = \sum_k c_{ijk} \dot{q}_k$ of the Christoffel symbols of the first kind. It captures forces arising from the rotation of one link about another.

(iii) The **Gravity vector** $\mathbf{G}(\mathbf{q}) = \dfrac{\partial V(\mathbf{q})}{\partial \mathbf{q}}$ is the gradient of the total gravitational potential energy. Its $i$-th component is the joint torque needed to hold the robot *stationary* at configuration $\mathbf{q}$.

**Two properties of $\mathbf{M}$:** (1) $\mathbf{M} = \mathbf{M}^\top$ (symmetric); (2) $\mathbf{M}$ is positive-definite, hence invertible. *(1.5 marks each sub-part.)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 3, Q1(b)]**

**Q1(b).** *Write the Lagrangian $L$ for a robot manipulator and state the Euler–Lagrange equation. Why is the Lagrangian approach often preferred for low-DOF (≤2) manipulators?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Lagrangian** of a robot manipulator is defined as the difference between total kinetic energy and total potential energy:

$$
L(\mathbf{q}, \dot{\mathbf{q}}) = T(\mathbf{q}, \dot{\mathbf{q}}) - V(\mathbf{q})
$$

The **Euler–Lagrange equation** for the $i$-th generalized coordinate $q_i$ is:

$$
\frac{d}{dt}\!\left(\frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{q}_i}\right) - \frac{\partial L}{\partial q_i} = \tau_i, \quad i = 1, \dots, n
$$

The Lagrangian approach is preferred for low-DOF manipulators because (i) it requires only scalar energy functions, avoiding vector/tensor bookkeeping; (ii) it is **systematic** — the same recipe works for any open chain; (iii) it yields the *closed-form symbolic* $\mathbf{M}, \mathbf{C}, \mathbf{G}$ matrices needed for model-based control; and (iv) it does not require explicit computation of internal constraint forces (unlike Newton–Euler). *(1 mark for $L$, 1 mark for E-L equation, 1 mark for justification.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 3, Q4]**

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks)

**(a) [7 marks]** For the planar 2R manipulator shown below, derive the **kinetic energy** $T$ in terms of the joint variables $\theta_1, \theta_2$ and their time derivatives. Clearly state the positions of the centres of mass, the velocity magnitudes, and the rotational kinetic energy of each link.

- Link 1: mass $m_1$, length $l_1$, COM at $l_{c1}$, inertia $I_1$ (about COM, $\hat{z}$-axis).
- Link 2: mass $m_2$, length $l_2$, COM at $l_{c2}$, inertia $I_2$.

**(b) [7 marks]** Using the result of (a), obtain the **potential energy** $V$ and derive the **complete dynamic model** $\mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})\ddot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{C}(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}})\dot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{G}(\mathbf{q}) = \boldsymbol{\tau}$. Identify each term explicitly and verify that $\mathbf{M}$ is symmetric.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution to Question A

**Part (a) — Kinetic Energy [7 marks]**

*Step 1 (1 mark) — Positions of COMs.* (as derived in §3.2)

$$
\mathbf{p}_{c1} = \begin{bmatrix} l_{c1}\cos\theta_1 \\ l_{c1}\sin\theta_1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad
\mathbf{p}_{c2} = \begin{bmatrix} l_1\cos\theta_1 + l_{c2}\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2) \\ l_1\sin\theta_1 + l_{c2}\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2) \end{bmatrix}
$$

*Step 2 (2 marks) — Velocities.* Differentiating each component:

$$
v_{c1}^2 = l_{c1}^2 \dot{\theta}_1^2
$$

$$
v_{c2}^2 = l_1^2 \dot{\theta}_1^2 + l_{c2}^2(\dot{\theta}_1+\dot{\theta}_2)^2 + 2 l_1 l_{c2} \dot{\theta}_1(\dot{\theta}_1+\dot{\theta}_2)\cos\theta_2
$$

*Step 3 (1 mark) — Angular velocities.* $\omega_1 = \dot{\theta}_1$, $\omega_2 = \dot{\theta}_1 + \dot{\theta}_2$ (planar).

*Step 4 (3 marks) — Total kinetic energy:*

$$
T = \tfrac{1}{2} m_1 l_{c1}^2 \dot{\theta}_1^2 + \tfrac{1}{2} I_1 \dot{\theta}_1^2 + \tfrac{1}{2} m_2\!\left[l_1^2 \dot{\theta}_1^2 + l_{c2}^2 (\dot{\theta}_1+\dot{\theta}_2)^2 + 2 l_1 l_{c2}\dot{\theta}_1(\dot{\theta}_1+\dot{\theta}_2)\cos\theta_2\right] + \tfrac{1}{2} I_2(\dot{\theta}_1+\dot{\theta}_2)^2
$$

Expanding $(\dot{\theta}_1+\dot{\theta}_2)^2 = \dot{\theta}_1^2 + 2\dot{\theta}_1\dot{\theta}_2 + \dot{\theta}_2^2$ and collecting terms:

$$
T = \tfrac{1}{2}\!\left[I_1 + m_1 l_{c1}^2 + m_2 l_1^2 + m_2 l_{c2}^2 + 2 m_2 l_1 l_{c2}\cos\theta_2\right]\dot{\theta}_1^2 + \left[m_2 l_{c2}^2 + m_2 l_1 l_{c2}\cos\theta_2\right]\dot{\theta}_1\dot{\theta}_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\!\left[I_2 + m_2 l_{c2}^2\right]\dot{\theta}_2^2
$$

---

**Part (b) — Potential Energy & Full Dynamic Model [7 marks]**

*Step 1 (1 mark) — Potential energy:*

$$
V = m_1 g l_{c1}\sin\theta_1 + m_2 g \!\left[l_1\sin\theta_1 + l_{c2}\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2)\right]
$$

*Step 2 (1 mark) — Lagrangian:* $L = T - V$.

*Step 3 (3 marks) — Euler–Lagrange equations:*

For $i=1$ (after applying $d/dt$ derivatives and noting $h = -m_2 l_1 l_{c2}\sin\theta_2$):

$$
\tau_1 = m_{11}\ddot{\theta}_1 + m_{12}\ddot{\theta}_2 + h\dot{\theta}_2(2\dot{\theta}_1 + \dot{\theta}_2) + (m_1 l_{c1} + m_2 l_1)g\cos\theta_1 + m_2 l_{c2} g\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2)
$$

For $i=2$:

$$
\tau_2 = m_{12}\ddot{\theta}_1 + m_{22}\ddot{\theta}_2 + h\,\dot{\theta}_1^2 + m_2 l_{c2} g\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2)
$$

*Step 4 (2 marks) — Assembling the canonical form:*

$$
\boxed{
\begin{bmatrix} m_{11} & m_{12} \\ m_{12} & m_{22} \end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix} \ddot{\theta}_1 \\ \ddot{\theta}_2 \end{bmatrix}
+
\begin{bmatrix} h\dot{\theta}_2 & h(\dot{\theta}_1+\dot{\theta}_2) \\ -h\dot{\theta}_1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix} \dot{\theta}_1 \\ \dot{\theta}_2 \end{bmatrix}
+
\begin{bmatrix} (m_1 l_{c1}+m_2 l_1)g\cos\theta_1 + m_2 l_{c2} g\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2) \\ m_2 l_{c2} g\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2) \end{bmatrix}
=
\begin{bmatrix} \tau_1 \\ \tau_2 \end{bmatrix}
}
$$

with $m_{11}, m_{12}, m_{22}$ as in §3.5 and $h = -m_2 l_1 l_{c2}\sin\theta_2$.

**Symmetry check (1 mark):** $m_{12} = m_{21} = m_2 l_{c2}^2 + m_2 l_1 l_{c2}\cos\theta_2$. ✅ The off-diagonal entries match, so $\mathbf{M} = \mathbf{M}^\top$. Also, $\mathbf{M}$ is positive-definite because its leading principal minors $m_{11} > 0$ and $\det \mathbf{M} = m_{11} m_{22} - m_{12}^2 = (I_2 + m_2 l_{c2}^2)\!\left[I_1 + m_1 l_{c1}^2 + m_2 l_1^2 \sin^2\theta_2\right] > 0$ for all $\theta_2$.

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a) [7 marks]** Consider a 2-DOF spatial manipulator with two revolute joints, with link COM inertia tensors $\mathbf{I}_1, \mathbf{I}_2$ given in their respective body frames. Using the **Jacobian-based** formulation, derive the compact expression for the **kinetic energy**:

$$
T = \tfrac{1}{2}\dot{\mathbf{q}}^\top \mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})\dot{\mathbf{q}}
$$

Show that $\mathbf{M}$ is **symmetric positive-definite** and state the condition under which it remains well-conditioned.

**(b) [7 marks]** For the same spatial 2R robot, derive the **gravity vector** $\mathbf{G}(\mathbf{q}) = \dfrac{\partial V}{\partial \mathbf{q}}$ when the base-frame gravity vector is $\mathbf{g} = -g\hat{y}$. Show that if the joint axes are parallel to the base-frame $\hat{x}$-axis (instead of $\hat{z}$), the gravity vector takes a specific *trigonometric* form. Compute the gravity vector for a configuration $\mathbf{q} = \begin{bmatrix} \pi/4 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}$.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution to Question B

**Part (a) — Jacobian-based $\mathbf{M}$ [7 marks]**

For each link $i$, the linear velocity of the COM is $\mathbf{v}_{c_i} = \mathbf{J}_{v_i}(\mathbf{q})\dot{\mathbf{q}}$, and the angular velocity is $\boldsymbol{\omega}_i = \mathbf{J}_{\omega_i}(\mathbf{q})\dot{\mathbf{q}}$. The kinetic energy of link $i$ is:

$$
T_i = \tfrac{1}{2} m_i \mathbf{v}_{c_i}^\top \mathbf{v}_{c_i} + \tfrac{1}{2}\boldsymbol{\omega}_i^\top \mathbf{I}_{c_i} \boldsymbol{\omega}_i
$$

with $\mathbf{I}_{c_i} = \mathbf{R}_i \mathbf{I}_i \mathbf{R}_i^\top$ being the inertia tensor in the base frame. Substituting the Jacobians:

$$
T_i = \tfrac{1}{2} \dot{\mathbf{q}}^\top \!\left[\,m_i \mathbf{J}_{v_i}^\top \mathbf{J}_{v_i} + \mathbf{J}_{\omega_i}^\top \mathbf{I}_{c_i} \mathbf{J}_{\omega_i}\,\right] \dot{\mathbf{q}}
$$

Summing over $i = 1, 2$:

$$
T = \tfrac{1}{2}\dot{\mathbf{q}}^\top \mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})\dot{\mathbf{q}}, \quad \text{where} \quad \mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q}) = \sum_{i=1}^{2}\!\left[\,m_i \mathbf{J}_{v_i}^\top \mathbf{J}_{v_i} + \mathbf{J}_{\omega_i}^\top \mathbf{R}_i \mathbf{I}_i \mathbf{R}_i^\top \mathbf{J}_{\omega_i}\,\right]
$$

*Symmetry:* Each summand has the form $\mathbf{A}^\top \mathbf{A}$ (linear part) or $\mathbf{A}^\top \mathbf{B}^\top \mathbf{B} \mathbf{A}$ (rotational part, with $\mathbf{B}\mathbf{B}^\top$ symmetric). Hence each summand is symmetric, and so is $\mathbf{M}$. (3 marks)

*Positive-definiteness:* The kinetic energy of any real moving rigid body is non-negative and zero *iff* $\dot{\mathbf{q}} = \mathbf{0}$. Hence $T = \tfrac{1}{2}\dot{\mathbf{q}}^\top \mathbf{M}\dot{\mathbf{q}} \ge 0$, with equality only at $\dot{\mathbf{q}} = \mathbf{0}$. This implies $\mathbf{M} \succ 0$. (2 marks)

*Conditioning:* $\mathbf{M}$ is well-conditioned as long as the manipulator is **not at a singular configuration** (where $\mathbf{J}_{v_i}$ or $\mathbf{J}_{\omega_i}$ loses rank). For the 2R case, the only singularities are at $\theta_2 = 0, \pi$ (planar) or at configurations where the link axes align. (2 marks)

---

**Part (b) — Gravity vector [7 marks]**

*Step 1 (2 marks) —* In the base frame, the gravitational potential of link $i$ is:

$$
V_i = -m_i \mathbf{g}^\top \mathbf{p}_{c_i} = m_i g\,(\mathbf{p}_{c_i})_y \quad \text{(when } \mathbf{g} = -g\hat{y}\text{)}
$$

*Step 2 (2 marks) —* Sum and differentiate:

$$
\mathbf{G}(\mathbf{q}) = \frac{\partial V}{\partial \mathbf{q}} = \sum_{i=1}^{2} m_i \frac{\partial \mathbf{p}_{c_i}^\top}{\partial \mathbf{q}}\mathbf{g}
$$

This is the **operational-space projection** form, equivalent to $\mathbf{G} = \mathbf{J}_v^\top(\mathbf{q})\mathbf{g}_{\text{total}}$ where $\mathbf{J}_v$ is the *linear-velocity Jacobian of the total COM* (extended for multi-link).

*Step 3 (2 marks) —* If the joint axes are parallel to the **base-frame $\hat{x}$-axis** (instead of $\hat{z}$), the COM heights become:

$$
h_{c_1} = l_{c1}\sin\theta_1, \quad h_{c_2} = l_1\sin\theta_1 + l_{c2}\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2)
$$

*Step 4 (1 mark) —* Evaluating the gradient:

$$
\mathbf{G}(\mathbf{q}) = \begin{bmatrix} (m_1 l_{c1} + m_2 l_1)g\cos\theta_1 + m_2 l_{c2} g\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2) \\ m_2 l_{c2} g\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2) \end{bmatrix}
$$

*Step 5 (1 mark) —* For $\mathbf{q} = \begin{bmatrix} \pi/4 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}$:

$$
\cos\!\tfrac{\pi}{4} = \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}, \quad \cos\!\tfrac{\pi}{2} = 0
$$

$$
\mathbf{G} = \begin{bmatrix} (m_1 l_{c1} + m_2 l_1)\tfrac{g}{\sqrt{2}} + 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} (m_1 l_{c1} + m_2 l_1)\tfrac{g}{\sqrt{2}} \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the rotational KE of link 2** (only counting translational) — loses 1–2 marks. The full $T_{2,\text{rot}} = \tfrac{1}{2} I_2 (\dot{\theta}_1 + \dot{\theta}_2)^2$ is mandatory.
> 2. **Mis-identifying COM distances** — beginners write $l_1$ and $l_2$ where the problem says $l_{c1}$ and $l_{c2}$. Always read carefully: $l_{ci}$ = *distance from joint $i$ to COM of link $i$*, *not* link length.
> 3. **Sign error in $h$** — The Coriolis coefficient $h = -m_2 l_1 l_{c2}\sin\theta_2$ is *negative*; mixing up the sign of $\mathbf{C}$ violates the skew-symmetry property $\dot{\mathbf{M}} - 2\mathbf{C} = -(\dot{\mathbf{M}} - 2\mathbf{C})^\top$, which examiners check.
> 4. **Skipping the symmetry check** — even a 1-line statement "$\mathbf{M} = \mathbf{M}^\top$" earns an extra mark. Don't leave it implicit.
> 5. **Failing to convert the cross-term** $\cos\theta_2$ correctly in the $v_{c2}^2$ expansion — most marks are lost here. Practice this expansion 3 times before the exam.

---

## 5.3 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 — Module 3 — Dynamic Model (≤ 2 DOF) — Master Checklist**

- 🔹 **Canonical Form:** Always end your derivation at $\mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})\ddot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{C}(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}})\dot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{G}(\mathbf{q}) = \boldsymbol{\tau}$.
- 🔹 **Lagrangian** $L = T - V$; one Euler–Lagrange equation per joint.
- 🔹 **Position of COMs first**, then differentiate, then square, then collect $T$ as a quadratic form in $\dot{\mathbf{q}}$.
- 🔹 **Mass matrix extraction rule:** $T = \tfrac{1}{2}\sum_{i,j} m_{ij}\dot{q}_i\dot{q}_j$ $\Rightarrow$ $\mathbf{M} = [m_{ij}]$.
- 🔹 **Christoffel symbols** $c_{ijk} = \tfrac{1}{2}\!\left(\partial m_{ij}/\partial q_k + \partial m_{ik}/\partial q_j - \partial m_{jk}/\partial q_i\right)$.
- 🔹 **Coriolis entry** $C_{ij} = \sum_k c_{ijk}\dot{q}_k$.
- 🔹 **Gravity entry** $G_i = \partial V / \partial q_i$.
- 🔹 **Properties of $\mathbf{M}$:** symmetric, positive-definite, configuration-dependent.
- 🔹 **Skew-symmetry** of $\dot{\mathbf{M}} - 2\mathbf{C}$ — used in passivity-based control proofs.
- 🔹 **Planar 2R is symmetric** ($m_{12} = m_{21}$); cross-terms depend only on $\theta_2$.
- 🔹 **Spatial 2R** needs the body inertia tensor $\mathbf{I}_i$ and the geometric Jacobians $\mathbf{J}_{v_i}, \mathbf{J}_{\omega_i}$.
- 🔹 **Special cases** the examiner loves: $g = 0$ (horizontal plane), $\dot{\mathbf{q}} = 0$ (static), prismatic vs. revolute (RP variant).
- 🔹 **Numerical sanity check:** $m_{11} > 0$ and $\det \mathbf{M} > 0$ ⇒ $\mathbf{M}$ is SPD.
- 🔹 **Units check:** $[\mathbf{M}] = $ kg·m², $[\mathbf{C}\dot{\mathbf{q}}] = $ N·m, $[\mathbf{G}] = $ N·m, $[\boldsymbol{\tau}] = $ N·m.
- 🔹 **Remember** the $\sin\theta_2$ in $h$ (Coriolis) versus the $\cos\theta_2$ in $m_{11}$ and $m_{12}$ (inertia). This is the #1 mix-up in KTU scripts.
- 🔹 **Final answer must always be boxed** and clearly show $\mathbf{M}, \mathbf{C}, \mathbf{G}$ as separate blocks for full credit.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Modelling Including Motor and Gearbox
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET722 - ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Modelling Including Motor and Gearbox

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In robotic manipulator dynamics, the **actuator model** refers to the mathematical representation of the energy conversion and transmission subsystem that physically drives each joint of the robot. A complete joint actuation system typically consists of three integrated sub-models:

1. **DC Servo Motor Model** – captures the electromechanical conversion of electrical energy into mechanical torque using armature current and back-EMF.
2. **Gearbox / Transmission Model** – represents the mechanical reduction mechanism that trades rotational speed for torque amplification.
3. **Coupled Motor–Gear–Load Model** – combines the motor dynamics with the reflected (referred) inertia, damping, and disturbance torque of the gearbox and link.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (OEMET722 Module 3):** The modelling of actuators (motors) and gear trains is a foundational requirement before writing the complete joint-space dynamic equation. Without including motor and gearbox effects, the computed joint torque is physically unrealizable.

The standard armature-controlled **DC servo motor** is governed by two coupled first-order differential equations – one electrical and one mechanical – which together define the *motor transfer function* relating applied armature voltage to the resulting shaft angular position.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Intuition: The Bicycle-Dynamo Analogy**

Imagine you are turning the pedals of a bicycle connected to a rear wheel through a chain-and-sprocket system. The chain-and-sprocket is a **gearbox** – the large front sprocket (driving gear) and small rear sprocket (driven gear) cause the rear wheel to spin faster than the pedals but with less force. Conversely, if the rear sprocket were larger than the front, the wheel would turn slower but with much greater force.

Now replace your legs with an **electric motor**. The motor has its own inertia (the spinning rotor), its own friction (bearings, brushes), and needs electrical current to produce torque. A gearbox is placed between the motor and the load (the robot link) for two engineering reasons:

- **Torque Amplification** – robots need large torques at low speeds; motors naturally produce high speed with low torque.
- **Inertia Mismatch Reduction** – the motor's rotor inertia, when *reflected* to the load side, gets divided by the square of the gear ratio, making the motor "feel lighter" to the controller.

So, when KTU asks you to "model including motor and gearbox," they are asking you to account for:
- The motor's **electrical time constant** (how fast current builds up),
- The motor's **mechanical time constant** (how fast the shaft speeds up),
- The **gear ratio** $N$ that scales torque ($\times N$) and inertia ($\times 1/N^2$).

## 1.3 Standard Physical Constants and Metrics

The following **bold** values are standard, textbook-grade parameters used universally in KTU robotics problems:

- **Armature resistance** $R_a$ – measured in **ohms ($\Omega$)**
- **Armature inductance** $L_a$ – measured in **henries (H)**
- **Motor torque constant** $K_t$ – measured in **N·m/A**
- **Back-EMF constant** $K_b$ – measured in **V·s/rad** (or V/(rad/s))
- **Motor (rotor) inertia** $J_m$ – measured in **kg·m²**
- **Motor viscous damping** $B_m$ – measured in **N·m·s/rad**
- **Gear ratio** $N = \theta_m / \theta_L$ – dimensionless (motor turns per load turn)
- **Load (link) inertia** $J_L$ – measured in **kg·m²**
- **Load damping** $B_L$ – measured in **N·m·s/rad**

> [!TIP]
> **Critical Engineering Convention:** In SI units, for an *ideal* DC motor, $K_t$ (in N·m/A) and $K_b$ (in V·s/rad) are numerically equal: $K_t = K_b$. This is a direct consequence of energy conservation and is *heavily* tested in KTU valuation.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Component-Wise Theoretical Breakdown

### 2.1.1 The DC Servo Motor — Electrical Sub-Model

Apply **Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL)** across the armature circuit (supply voltage $v_a$, resistance $R_a$, inductance $L_a$, back-EMF $e_b$):

$$v_a(t) = R_a \, i_a(t) + L_a \, \frac{di_a(t)}{dt} + e_b(t)$$

The **back-EMF** $e_b$ is generated whenever the motor shaft rotates and is proportional to angular velocity $\omega_m$:

$$e_b(t) = K_b \, \omega_m(t) = K_b \, \frac{d\theta_m(t)}{dt}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why back-EMF matters:** It is the *natural feedback mechanism* of the motor. As the shaft spins faster, the induced voltage opposes the applied voltage, naturally limiting the current and hence the torque. Without back-EMF modelling, a KTU exam answer is considered physically incomplete.

### 2.1.2 The DC Servo Motor — Mechanical Sub-Model

Apply **Newton's Second Law for Rotation** at the motor shaft, summing torques:

$$J_m \, \ddot{\theta}_m(t) + B_m \, \dot{\theta}_m(t) = \tau_m(t) - \tau_{L,ref}(t)$$

Where:
- $J_m \ddot{\theta}_m$ – inertial torque of rotor
- $B_m \dot{\theta}_m$ – viscous friction torque
- $\tau_m$ – generated electromagnetic torque
- $\tau_{L,ref}$ – load torque *reflected* back to the motor shaft

The electromagnetic torque is proportional to armature current:

$$\tau_m(t) = K_t \, i_a(t)$$

### 2.1.3 The Gearbox / Transmission Model

A **single-stage gear train** with gear ratio $N$ relates motor-side and load-side quantities. Two conventions exist, and KTU accepts both with explicit declaration:

| Convention | Gear Ratio $N$ Definition | Torque Relation | Velocity Relation |
|------------|---------------------------|-----------------|-------------------|
| Load reduction | $N = \theta_m / \theta_L > 1$ | $\tau_m = \tau_L / N$ | $\omega_m = N \, \omega_L$ |
| Speed reduction | $N = \theta_L / \theta_m < 1$ | $\tau_m = N \, \tau_L$ | $\omega_m = \omega_L / N$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Standard Convention:** Throughout this note we use the **load-reduction** convention $N = \theta_m / \theta_L \geq 1$ (motor turns $N$ times for one load turn). This is the convention followed by Spong, Hutchinson, and Vidyasagar, the standard KTU reference.

### 2.1.4 Reflected (Referred) Quantities Across the Gearbox

For an ideal (lossless) gearbox with ratio $N$, quantities transform as:

$$\theta_m = N \, \theta_L, \quad \omega_m = N \, \omega_L, \quad \ddot{\theta}_m = N \, \ddot{\theta}_L$$

$$\tau_{L,ref} = \frac{\tau_L}{N}$$

When the load inertia $J_L$ and load damping $B_L$ are **reflected to the motor side**, they appear divided by the square of $N$:

$$J_{L,ref} = \frac{J_L}{N^2}, \quad B_{L,ref} = \frac{B_L}{N^2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Divided by N-squared" Rule — KTU Favourite:** This single rule accounts for nearly 30% of the marks in any motor–gearbox problem. The factor of $N^2$ arises from the kinetic energy equivalence: $\frac{1}{2} J_L \omega_L^2 = \frac{1}{2} J_{L,ref} \omega_m^2$, which gives $J_{L,ref} = J_L (\omega_L / \omega_m)^2 = J_L / N^2$.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

The following table consolidates every equation you must memorize for this topic. Note the use of `\vert` in place of absolute-value pipes to preserve table syntax.

| # | Equation Name | Mathematical Form | Units | Physical Meaning |
|---|---------------|-------------------|-------|------------------|
| 1 | Armature KVL | $v_a = R_a i_a + L_a \dot{i}_a + K_b \dot{\theta}_m$ | V | Motor electrical circuit |
| 2 | Back-EMF | $e_b = K_b \dot{\theta}_m$ | V | Voltage opposing motion |
| 3 | Motor torque | $\tau_m = K_t \, i_a$ | N·m | Electromagnetic torque |
| 4 | Shaft dynamics | $J_m \ddot{\theta}_m + B_m \dot{\theta}_m = \tau_m - \tau_{L,ref}$ | N·m | Newton-Euler at shaft |
| 5 | Gear position | $\theta_m = N \, \theta_L$ | rad | Gear kinematics |
| 6 | Gear torque | $\tau_m = \tau_L / N$ | N·m | Torque scaling |
| 7 | Reflected inertia | $J_{L,ref} = J_L / N^2$ | kg·m² | Inertia at motor side |
| 8 | Reflected damping | $B_{L,ref} = B_L / N^2$ | N·m·s/rad | Damping at motor side |
| 9 | Total reflected inertia | $J_{eq} = J_m + J_L / N^2$ | kg·m² | Effective inertia at motor |
| 10 | Open-loop TF | $\dfrac{\Theta_m(s)}{V_a(s)} = \dfrac{K_t}{s \left[ (L_a s + R_a)(J_{eq} s + B_{eq}) + K_t K_b \right]}$ | rad/V | Motor position TF |
| 11 | Simplified TF (neglect $L_a$) | $\dfrac{\Theta_m(s)}{V_a(s)} = \dfrac{K_t / (R_a B_{eq})}{s \left[ \tau_m \tau_e \, s^2 + (\tau_m + \tau_e) s + 1 \right]}$ | rad/V | Second-order motor TF |
| 12 | Motor time constant | $\tau_m = J_{eq} R_a / (K_t K_b)$ (approx with $B$) | s | Mechanical time constant |
| 13 | Electrical time const. | $\tau_e = L_a / R_a$ | s | Electrical time constant |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

This exact motor-gearbox modelling framework is the backbone of:

- **Industrial robot joint controllers** (ABB, KUKA, Fanuc joint servo loops).
- **CNC machine tool feed drives** where DC or brushless motors drive ball-screws through gear reducers.
- **Electric vehicle drivetrains** where motor–gearbox–wheel inertia matching directly affects range and acceleration.
- **Haptic device design** where reflected inertia must be minimized to give the user a "transparent" feel.
- **Drone gimbal stabilizers** where gearbox backlash and reflected friction dominate tracking error.

A robot engineer who *cannot* derive the motor–gear transfer function cannot select the right motor for a given joint, cannot size the gearbox, and cannot tune the PID gains that close the position loop.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: The Complete Motor-Only Transfer Function (Armature Voltage to Motor Position)

**Goal:** Derive $\Theta_m(s) / V_a(s)$ starting from the two coupled differential equations.

**Step 1 — Write the two governing ODEs in the time domain.**

Electrical (KVL around the armature circuit):

$$v_a(t) = R_a \, i_a(t) + L_a \, \frac{di_a(t)}{dt} + K_b \, \dot{\theta}_m(t)$$

Mechanical (Newton's second law at the motor shaft, no load torque for now):

$$J_m \, \ddot{\theta}_m(t) + B_m \, \dot{\theta}_m(t) = K_t \, i_a(t)$$

**Step 2 — Take the Laplace transform of both equations assuming zero initial conditions.**

$$V_a(s) = (L_a s + R_a) \, I_a(s) + K_b \, s \, \Theta_m(s)$$

$$(J_m s^2 + B_m s) \, \Theta_m(s) = K_t \, I_a(s)$$

**Step 3 — Solve the mechanical equation for $I_a(s)$.**

$$I_a(s) = \frac{(J_m s + B_m) \, s \, \Theta_m(s)}{K_t}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute this expression for $I_a(s)$ back into the electrical equation.**

$$V_a(s) = (L_a s + R_a) \cdot \frac{(J_m s + B_m) \, s \, \Theta_m(s)}{K_t} + K_b \, s \, \Theta_m(s)$$

**Step 5 — Factor out the common term $s \, \Theta_m(s)$.**

$$V_a(s) = s \, \Theta_m(s) \left[ \frac{(L_a s + R_a)(J_m s + B_m)}{K_t} + K_b \right]$$

**Step 6 — Multiply numerator and denominator to obtain the standard form.**

$$V_a(s) = s \, \Theta_m(s) \cdot \frac{(L_a s + R_a)(J_m s + B_m) + K_t K_b}{K_t}$$

**Step 7 — Invert to obtain the open-loop transfer function.**

$$\frac{\Theta_m(s)}{V_a(s)} = \frac{K_t}{s \left[ (L_a s + R_a)(J_m s + B_m) + K_t K_b \right]}$$

This is the canonical form used in every KTU robotics exam for "derive the motor transfer function."

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Including the Gearbox and Load

**Goal:** Show how the load inertia and load damping appear on the motor side.

**Step 1 — Write the mechanical equation including the reflected load torque.**

$$J_m \, \ddot{\theta}_m + B_m \, \dot{\theta}_m + \tau_{L,ref} = K_t \, i_a$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $\tau_{L,ref} = \tau_L / N$.**

$$J_m \, \ddot{\theta}_m + B_m \, \dot{\theta}_m + \frac{\tau_L}{N} = K_t \, i_a$$

**Step 3 — Express the load torque $\tau_L$ in terms of load-side inertia and damping.**

The load (link) has its own equation:

$$\tau_L = J_L \, \ddot{\theta}_L + B_L \, \dot{\theta}_L$$

**Step 4 — Apply the gear constraint $\theta_L = \theta_m / N$, hence $\dot{\theta}_L = \dot{\theta}_m / N$ and $\ddot{\theta}_L = \ddot{\theta}_m / N$.**

$$\tau_L = J_L \cdot \frac{\ddot{\theta}_m}{N} + B_L \cdot \frac{\dot{\theta}_m}{N}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute $\tau_L$ into the reflected torque term.**

$$\frac{\tau_L}{N} = \frac{1}{N} \left( \frac{J_L \ddot{\theta}_m} {N} + \frac{B_L \dot{\theta}_m}{N} \right) = \frac{J_L}{N^2} \ddot{\theta}_m + \frac{B_L}{N^2} \dot{\theta}_m$$

**Step 6 — Collect terms and define the equivalent inertia and damping at the motor side.**

$$J_{eq} \triangleq J_m + \frac{J_L}{N^2}$$

$$B_{eq} \triangleq B_m + \frac{B_L}{N^2}$$

**Step 7 — The motor–gear–load system has the same form as the motor alone.**

$$J_{eq} \, \ddot{\theta}_m + B_{eq} \, \dot{\theta}_m = K_t \, i_a$$

**Step 8 — The complete transfer function with gearbox is identical to the bare-motor result, with $(J_m, B_m)$ replaced by $(J_{eq}, B_{eq})$.**

$$\frac{\Theta_m(s)}{V_a(s)} = \frac{K_t}{s \left[ (L_a s + R_a)(J_{eq} s + B_{eq}) + K_t K_b \right]}$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Simplified Second-Order Model (Neglecting Armature Inductance)

In many practical DC servomotors, $L_a$ is very small (microhenries to a few millihenries), so the electrical transient is much faster than the mechanical one. Setting $L_a \approx 0$ reduces the system order.

**Step 1 — Set $L_a = 0$ in the transfer function.**

$$\frac{\Theta_m(s)}{V_a(s)} = \frac{K_t}{s \left[ R_a (J_{eq} s + B_{eq}) + K_t K_b \right]}$$

**Step 2 — Expand the denominator.**

$$s \left[ R_a J_{eq} s + R_a B_{eq} + K_t K_b \right] = s \left[ R_a J_{eq} s + (R_a B_{eq} + K_t K_b) \right]$$

**Step 3 — Factor out $(R_a B_{eq} + K_t K_b)$ to obtain a normalized time-constant form.**

Denote:

$$K_{DC} = \frac{K_t}{R_a B_{eq} + K_t K_b}$$

$$\tau_M = \frac{R_a J_{eq}}{R_a B_{eq} + K_t K_b}$$

**Step 4 — The simplified motor transfer function is a standard first-order-with-integrator form.**

$$\frac{\Theta_m(s)}{V_a(s)} = \frac{K_{DC}}{s (\tau_M s + 1)}$$

**Step 5 — Expand to a second-order denominator for the complete model.**

If $L_a$ is small but nonzero, the standard form is:

$$\frac{\Theta_m(s)}{V_a(s)} = \frac{K_t / R_a}{s \left[ \tau_e \tau_M s^2 + (\tau_e + \tau_M) s + 1 \right]}$$

where the **electrical** and **mechanical** time constants are:

$$\tau_e = \frac{L_a}{R_a}, \quad \tau_M = \frac{R_a J_{eq}}{K_t K_b + R_a B_{eq}}$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparing Modelling Levels

| Level of Model | Parameters Used | System Order | Use Case | KTU Weightage |
|----------------|-----------------|--------------|----------|---------------|
| Ideal Torque Source | None (perfect $\tau$ = input) | 0 | Pure link dynamics | Introductory |
| Motor only, no $L_a$ | $J_m, B_m, R_a, K_t, K_b$ | 2 (one integrator + one pole) | Quick control design | **High** |
| Motor only, with $L_a$ | $+ L_a$ | 3 (integrator + two poles) | Accurate simulation | Medium |
| Motor + Gearbox | $+ N, J_L, B_L$ | Same as motor order | **Standard KTU model** | **Highest** |
| Motor + Gearbox + Disturbance | $+ \tau_{dist}$ | Same + external input | Robust control | Advanced |

## 3.5 Numerical Worked Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem Statement:** A DC servomotor has $R_a = 1 \, \Omega$, $L_a = 0.01$ H, $K_t = K_b = 0.05$ N·m/A, $J_m = 0.001$ kg·m², $B_m = 0.0001$ N·m·s/rad. It is geared to a link with $J_L = 1.0$ kg·m², $B_L = 0.5$ N·m·s/rad, through a gear ratio $N = 10$. Find (a) the equivalent inertia and damping at the motor shaft, (b) the electrical and mechanical time constants, (c) the transfer function $\Theta_L(s) / V_a(s)$.

**Step 1 — Compute equivalent inertia.**

$$J_{eq} = J_m + \frac{J_L}{N^2} = 0.001 + \frac{1.0}{100} = 0.001 + 0.01 = 0.011 \text{ kg·m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Compute equivalent damping.**

$$B_{eq} = B_m + \frac{B_L}{N^2} = 0.0001 + \frac{0.5}{100} = 0.0001 + 0.005 = 0.0051 \text{ N·m·s/rad}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the electrical time constant.**

$$\tau_e = \frac{L_a}{R_a} = \frac{0.01}{1} = 0.01 \text{ s} = 10 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the mechanical time constant.**

$$\tau_M = \frac{R_a \, J_{eq}}{K_t K_b + R_a \, B_{eq}} = \frac{1 \times 0.011}{0.05 \times 0.05 + 1 \times 0.0051} = \frac{0.011}{0.0025 + 0.0051} = \frac{0.011}{0.0076} \approx 1.447 \text{ s}$$

**Step 5 — Build the transfer function from motor voltage to motor position.**

$$\frac{\Theta_m(s)}{V_a(s)} = \frac{K_t}{s \left[ (L_a s + R_a)(J_{eq} s + B_{eq}) + K_t K_b \right]}$$

Substitute numerical values:

$$\frac{\Theta_m(s)}{V_a(s)} = \frac{0.05}{s \left[ (0.01 s + 1)(0.011 s + 0.0051) + 0.0025 \right]}$$

**Step 6 — Convert from motor position to load position using $\theta_L = \theta_m / N$.**

$$\frac{\Theta_L(s)}{V_a(s)} = \frac{1}{N} \cdot \frac{0.05}{s \left[ (0.01 s + 1)(0.011 s + 0.0051) + 0.0025 \right]} = \frac{0.005}{s \left[ (0.01 s + 1)(0.011 s + 0.0051) + 0.0025 \right]}$$

**Step 7 — Expand the denominator for the final polynomial form.**

$$(0.01 s + 1)(0.011 s + 0.0051) = 0.00011 s^2 + 0.000051 s + 0.011 s + 0.0051$$

$$= 0.00011 s^2 + 0.011051 s + 0.0051$$

Adding $K_t K_b = 0.0025$:

$$0.00011 s^2 + 0.011051 s + 0.0076$$

**Step 8 — Write the final transfer function.**

$$\frac{\Theta_L(s)}{V_a(s)} = \frac{0.005}{s (0.00011 s^2 + 0.011051 s + 0.0076)}$$

This can be normalised by dividing numerator and denominator by $0.0076$:

$$\frac{\Theta_L(s)}{V_a(s)} = \frac{0.6579}{s (0.01447 s^2 + 1.4541 s + 1)}$$

The poles are at $s = 0$ (integrator), and the roots of the quadratic in the denominator – which are real and negative for this heavily damped system, confirming the motor + gearbox behaves as expected.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram: Complete Motor–Gearbox–Load Signal Flow

The following Mermaid block diagram captures the *physical energy flow* and *signal flow* of the entire actuator subsystem, from electrical input to mechanical link output.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Va[Armature Voltage Va] --> SumA{Summing Node}
    SumA --> Ra[Armature Resistance Ra]
    Ra --> La[Armature Inductance La]
    La --> Ia[Armature Current Ia]
    Ia --> Kt[Torque Constant Kt]
    Kt --> Tm[Generated Torque Tm]
    Tm --> Shaft[Motor Shaft]
    Shaft --> Jm[Rotor Inertia Jm]
    Jm --> Bm[Rotor Damping Bm]
    Bm --> Gearbox[Gearbox Ratio N]
    Gearbox --> Link[Link Side Output]
    Shaft -.-> Kb[Back EMF Constant Kb]
    Kb --> Emf[Back EMF Voltage]
    Emf --> SumA
    Link --> JLref[Reflected Load J_L divided by N squared]
    JLref --> LinkOut[Effective Joint Output Torque and Position]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Reading the diagram:** The forward path (Va → Ia → τm → shaft) drives the motor, while the *feedback path* (shaft → Kb → summing node) is what makes back-EMF a stabilizing influence. Removing the feedback arrow would correspond to physically disconnecting the motor from the supply, which is *not* the KTU-accepted model.

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

For more rigorous KTU board-exam presentations, the following Mermaid block diagram maps each modelling level to its functional components:

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Electrical_Domain [Electrical Domain]
        EV1[Input Voltage Va] --> EV2[Subtract Back EMF]
        EV2 --> EV3[Divide by La s plus Ra]
        EV3 --> EC1[Armature Current Ia]
    end

    subgraph Coupling_Domain [Electromechanical Coupling]
        EC1 --> CM1[Multiply by Kt]
        CM1 --> MT1[Generated Torque Tm]
        Shaft_vel[Motor Angular Velocity] --> CM2[Multiply by Kb]
        CM2 --> EV2
    end

    subgraph Mechanical_Domain [Mechanical Domain]
        MT1 --> MM1[Subtract Friction Torque]
        MM1 --> MM2[Divide by J_eq s squared plus B_eq s]
        MM2 --> MS1[Motor Angular Position Theta_m]
    end

    subgraph Transmission_Domain [Gear Transmission]
        MS1 --> GT1[Divide by N for Load Position]
        GT1 --> LS1[Load Angular Position Theta_L]
        MS1 --> GT2[Divide by N for Load Torque]
        GT2 --> LS2[Effective Load Torque]
    end
```

## 4.3 State-Space Representation Block Diagram

In modern KTU questions, students are sometimes asked to write the state-space model. The architecture of the state equation $\dot{x} = A x + B u$ for the motor–gear system is:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    X1[State 1: Theta_m] --> X1dot[Derivative: Omega_m]
    X1dot --> X2[State 2: Omega_m]
    X2 --> X2dot[Derivative: Alpha_m]
    X2dot --> X3[State 3: Armature Current Ia]
    X3 --> X3dot[Derivative: dIa by dt]
    Va[Input: Armature Voltage Va] --> X3dot
    X1dot --> OutY1[Output 1: Theta_m]
    X2 --> OutY2[Output 2: Omega_m]
```

**State variables used:**
- $x_1 = \theta_m$ (motor angular position)
- $x_2 = \dot{\theta}_m$ (motor angular velocity)
- $x_3 = i_a$ (armature current)

**State matrix $A$ and input matrix $B$ for the motor–gear–load system:**

$$
A = \begin{bmatrix}
0 & 1 & 0 \\
0 & -\dfrac{B_{eq}}{J_{eq}} & \dfrac{K_t}{J_{eq}} \\
0 & -\dfrac{K_b}{L_a} & -\dfrac{R_a}{L_a}
\end{bmatrix}, \quad
B = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ \dfrac{1}{L_a} \end{bmatrix}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why state-space?** It is the cleanest way to handle MIMO robotic systems, where multiple motors and gearboxes are coupled. KTU Module 5 (Non-linear and advanced control) builds on this state-space foundation.

## 4.4 Block Diagram Showing Reflected Quantities

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph MotorSide [Motor Side]
        Jm[Rotor Jm] --> Jsum[Sum Inertias]
        Bm[Rotor Damping Bm] --> Bsum[Sum Dampings]
    end

    subgraph GearTrain [Gear Train Ratio N]
        N[Ratio N] --> Jdiv[Reflect Operation]
        N --> Bdiv[Reflect Operation]
    end

    subgraph LoadSide [Load Side]
        JL[Link J_L] --> Jdiv
        BL[Link B_L] --> Bdiv
        Jdiv --> Jref[Effective J_L divided by N squared]
        Bdiv --> Bref[Effective B_L divided by N squared]
    end

    Jref --> Jsum
    Bref --> Bsum
    Jsum --> Jeq[Equivalent Inertia J_eq]
    Bsum --> Beq[Equivalent Damping B_eq]
```

This block diagram makes the "divide by $N^2$" rule visually unambiguous – a key reason for KTU examiners' fondness for visual reasoning.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Define the term "back-EMF" in a DC servomotor. Explain its role in the motor's electromechanical behaviour.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Back-EMF (electromotive force) is the voltage induced in the armature winding of a DC motor when its rotor rotates in the magnetic field. By Faraday's law, it is proportional to the angular velocity:

$$e_b = K_b \, \dot{\theta}_m$$

Its role is two-fold:
1. **Opposes the applied voltage** (Lenz's law), thereby limiting the armature current and hence the developed torque.
2. **Acts as natural negative feedback** in the motor's electrical circuit, providing inherent speed-stabilization without external sensors.

In the armature KVL equation $v_a = R_a i_a + L_a \dot{i}_a + e_b$, the back-EMF term couples the mechanical shaft speed to the electrical circuit, which is the very reason the motor is a *coupled electromechanical* system.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Note (1 mark reserved):** Many students forget to state the proportionality constant $K_b$ explicitly. Always write the equation.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**What is meant by "reflected inertia" of the load at the motor shaft? Show the relevant expression.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The reflected inertia $J_{L,ref}$ is the equivalent inertia that the load presents to the motor shaft when a gearbox of ratio $N$ couples them. It is obtained by equating the kinetic energy on the load side to the equivalent kinetic energy on the motor side:

$$\frac{1}{2} J_L \, \omega_L^2 = \frac{1}{2} J_{L,ref} \, \omega_m^2$$

Using the gear constraint $\omega_m = N \, \omega_L$:

$$\boxed{J_{L,ref} = \frac{J_L}{N^2}}$$

This means that *for a gear ratio of 10, the load inertia is reduced by a factor of 100 at the motor shaft*, which is the fundamental reason high-ratio gearboxes make motor control easier.

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A [KTU University Exam - May 2024, 14 Marks]

**(a)** Derive the open-loop transfer function $\Theta_m(s) / V_a(s)$ of an armature-controlled DC servomotor starting from the governing differential equations. State all assumptions clearly. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A DC servomotor with $R_a = 2 \, \Omega$, $L_a = 0.005$ H, $K_t = K_b = 0.1$ N·m/A, $J_m = 0.005$ kg·m², $B_m = 0.001$ N·m·s/rad drives a load of $J_L = 0.5$ kg·m² through a gear ratio of $N = 5$. Compute (i) the equivalent inertia $J_{eq}$ and damping $B_{eq}$ referred to the motor shaft, (ii) the electrical and mechanical time constants, (iii) the transfer function $\Theta_L(s) / V_a(s)$. **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution to (a) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 — State the two governing ODEs (1 mark).**

Electrical: $v_a = R_a i_a + L_a \dot{i}_a + K_b \dot{\theta}_m$

Mechanical: $J_m \ddot{\theta}_m + B_m \dot{\theta}_m = K_t i_a$

**Step 2 — Take Laplace transform with zero initial conditions (1 mark).**

$$V_a = (L_a s + R_a) I_a + K_b s \Theta_m$$

$$(J_m s + B_m) s \Theta_m = K_t I_a$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $I_a$ (1 mark).**

$$I_a = \frac{s(J_m s + B_m) \Theta_m}{K_t}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute into the electrical equation and factor (2 marks).**

$$V_a = \frac{(L_a s + R_a)(J_m s + B_m) s \Theta_m}{K_t} + K_b s \Theta_m = s \Theta_m \left[ \frac{(L_a s + R_a)(J_m s + B_m) + K_t K_b}{K_t} \right]$$

**Step 5 — Final transfer function (2 marks).**

$$\frac{\Theta_m(s)}{V_a(s)} = \frac{K_t}{s \left[ (L_a s + R_a)(J_m s + B_m) + K_t K_b \right]}$$

**Assumptions:** Armature-controlled DC motor (field current constant), linear magnetic circuit, no saturation, no gearbox (motor alone), viscous friction only.

---

**Solution to (b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 — Equivalent inertia and damping (2 marks).**

$$J_{eq} = J_m + \frac{J_L}{N^2} = 0.005 + \frac{0.5}{25} = 0.005 + 0.02 = 0.025 \text{ kg·m}^2$$

$$B_{eq} = B_m + \frac{B_L}{N^2} = 0.001 + \frac{0}{25} = 0.001 \text{ N·m·s/rad} \quad (\text{since } B_L \text{ not given})$$

**Step 2 — Electrical time constant (1 mark).**

$$\tau_e = \frac{L_a}{R_a} = \frac{0.005}{2} = 0.0025 \text{ s} = 2.5 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 3 — Mechanical time constant (1 mark).**

$$\tau_M = \frac{R_a J_{eq}}{K_t K_b + R_a B_{eq}} = \frac{2 \times 0.025}{0.1 \times 0.1 + 2 \times 0.001} = \frac{0.05}{0.01 + 0.002} = \frac{0.05}{0.012} \approx 4.167 \text{ s}$$

**Step 4 — Build the motor-side transfer function (1 mark).**

$$\frac{\Theta_m(s)}{V_a(s)} = \frac{0.1}{s \left[ (0.005 s + 2)(0.025 s + 0.001) + 0.01 \right]}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to load position using $\theta_L = \theta_m / N$ (1 mark).**

$$\frac{\Theta_L(s)}{V_a(s)} = \frac{0.02}{s \left[ (0.005 s + 2)(0.025 s + 0.001) + 0.01 \right]}$$

**Step 6 — Expand and present final form (1 mark).**

$$(0.005 s + 2)(0.025 s + 0.001) = 0.000125 s^2 + 0.000005 s + 0.05 s + 0.002 = 0.000125 s^2 + 0.050005 s + 0.002$$

Adding $K_t K_b = 0.01$: $0.000125 s^2 + 0.050005 s + 0.012$

$$\boxed{\frac{\Theta_L(s)}{V_a(s)} = \frac{0.02}{s (0.000125 s^2 + 0.050005 s + 0.012)}}$$

---

### Question B (Alternative Choice) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, 14 Marks]

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the functional architecture of a DC servomotor–gearbox–load system. Show how the load inertia and damping are "reflected" to the motor side, and derive the equivalent parameters $J_{eq}$ and $B_{eq}$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A robot joint is driven by a DC servomotor coupled to the link through a harmonic-drive gearhead of ratio $N = 100$. The motor parameters are $R_a = 1.5 \, \Omega$, $L_a = 0.002$ H, $K_t = 0.08$ N·m/A, $K_b = 0.08$ V·s/rad, $J_m = 0.0005$ kg·m², $B_m = 0.0002$ N·m·s/rad. The link has $J_L = 50$ kg·m², $B_L = 100$ N·m·s/rad. The armature is fed 24 V. Compute the steady-state (a) motor speed, (b) motor torque, (c) load torque, (d) load speed. **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution to (a) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 — Block diagram explanation (2 marks).** Draw the motor–gearbox–load block diagram showing:
- Forward path: $V_a \to I_a \to \tau_m \to$ shaft $\to$ gearbox $\to$ link.
- Feedback path: shaft velocity $\to K_b \to$ armature KVL.
- Load side feeding back: link inertia $J_L$ and damping $B_L$ enter the gearbox block.

**Step 2 — Reflected inertia derivation (2 marks).** From kinetic energy equivalence:

$$\frac{1}{2} J_L \omega_L^2 = \frac{1}{2} J_{L,ref} \omega_m^2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad J_{L,ref} = J_L \left(\frac{\omega_L}{\omega_m}\right)^2 = \frac{J_L}{N^2}$$

**Step 3 — Reflected damping derivation (1 mark).** From power dissipated by viscous friction:

$$B_L \omega_L^2 = B_{L,ref} \omega_m^2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad B_{L,ref} = \frac{B_L}{N^2}$$

**Step 4 — Equivalent parameters (2 marks).**

$$J_{eq} = J_m + \frac{J_L}{N^2}, \quad B_{eq} = B_m + \frac{B_L}{N^2}$$

These are the parameters that govern the motor shaft dynamics in the simplified single-inertia model.

---

**Solution to (b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 — Compute reflected load parameters (1 mark).**

$$J_{L,ref} = \frac{50}{100^2} = \frac{50}{10000} = 0.005 \text{ kg·m}^2$$

$$B_{L,ref} = \frac{100}{10000} = 0.01 \text{ N·m·s/rad}$$

**Step 2 — Equivalent inertia and damping (1 mark).**

$$J_{eq} = 0.0005 + 0.005 = 0.0055 \text{ kg·m}^2$$

$$B_{eq} = 0.0002 + 0.01 = 0.0102 \text{ N·m·s/rad}$$

**Step 3 — Steady-state motor speed (1.5 marks).** At steady state, $\dot{i}_a = 0$ and $\ddot{\theta}_m = 0$. From the steady-state balance:

$$V_a = R_a I_a + K_b \omega_m, \quad K_t I_a = B_{eq} \omega_m$$

From the second equation, $I_a = B_{eq} \omega_m / K_t$. Substituting into the first:

$$V_a = R_a \frac{B_{eq} \omega_m}{K_t} + K_b \omega_m = \omega_m \left( \frac{R_a B_{eq}}{K_t} + K_b \right)$$

$$\omega_m = \frac{V_a}{\dfrac{R_a B_{eq}}{K_t} + K_b} = \frac{24}{\dfrac{1.5 \times 0.0102}{0.08} + 0.08} = \frac{24}{\dfrac{0.0153}{0.08} + 0.08} = \frac{24}{0.19125 + 0.08} = \frac{24}{0.27125} \approx 88.48 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 4 — Steady-state motor torque (1.5 marks).**

$$\tau_m = K_t I_a = K_t \cdot \frac{B_{eq} \omega_m}{K_t} = B_{eq} \omega_m = 0.0102 \times 88.48 \approx 0.9025 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 5 — Load torque and load speed (2 marks).**

$$\tau_L = N \, \tau_m = 100 \times 0.9025 = 90.25 \text{ N·m}$$

$$\omega_L = \frac{\omega_m}{N} = \frac{88.48}{100} \approx 0.8848 \text{ rad/s} \approx 8.45 \text{ RPM}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting the gearbox division by $N^2$** for reflected inertia — *most common mistake*, costs 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Using $K_t \neq K_b$** in SI units — they are numerically equal, and many students incorrectly treat them as different parameters.
> 3. **Omitting the $B_{eq} \omega_m$ term** in steady-state torque calculation — without damping, the motor would have no load torque at all, which is physically absurd.
> 4. **Confusing $N = \theta_m / \theta_L$ with $N = \theta_L / \theta_m$** — always declare the convention in the first line of your answer.
> 5. **Not converting $\omega_m$ to $\omega_L$** for the final load speed — a 1-mark deduction per occurrence.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 3, Motor & Gearbox Modelling**

- **Two coupled ODEs define the motor**: electrical (KVL) and mechanical (Newton's 2nd law). Always write both.
- **Back-EMF $e_b = K_b \dot{\theta}_m$** is the *coupling term* between electrical and mechanical domains — never omit it.
- **In SI units, $K_t = K_b$** numerically. Commit this to memory.
- **Gearbox convention $N = \theta_m / \theta_L \geq 1$** is KTU standard. State it explicitly in every answer.
- **Reflected inertia $J_{L,ref} = J_L / N^2$** — the "divided by N-squared" rule is non-negotiable.
- **Reflected damping $B_{L,ref} = B_L / N^2$** — same rule, often forgotten.
- **Equivalent inertia $J_{eq} = J_m + J_L / N^2$** and **damping $B_{eq} = B_m + B_L / N^2$** — substitute these into the bare-motor transfer function to get the geared-motor transfer function.
- **Open-loop motor TF** (from armature voltage to motor position) is third-order: $1/[s(L_a s + R_a)(J_{eq} s + B_{eq}) + s K_t K_b]$ after simplifications, second-order if $L_a$ is neglected.
- **Time constants:** $\tau_e = L_a / R_a$ (electrical) and $\tau_M = R_a J_{eq} / (K_t K_b + R_a B_{eq})$ (mechanical).
- **Steady-state speed** is set by the voltage divider: $\omega_m^{ss} = V_a / (K_b + R_a B_{eq} / K_t)$.
- **Steady-state torque** is set by the damping: $\tau_m^{ss} = B_{eq} \, \omega_m^{ss}$ (for a load with only viscous friction).
- **Gearbox trades speed for torque:** $\tau_L = N \tau_m$ and $\omega_L = \omega_m / N$.
- **Practical engineering rule:** the larger the gear ratio, the more the load inertia "disappears" at the motor, but the larger the friction and backlash torques (which KTU advanced problems may ask you to model as disturbances).
- **Always draw the block diagram** — it earns 1–2 free marks and demonstrates understanding of signal flow.
- **Final transfer function for the load**: $\Theta_L(s) / V_a(s) = (1/N) \cdot \Theta_m(s) / V_a(s)$ — do not forget the $1/N$ factor.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **One-Line Takeaway for KTU Exam:** *The motor–gearbox–load system is fully described by two coupled first-order ODEs whose Laplace-domain form is a single transfer function with integrator + electrical pole + mechanical pole, where the gear ratio $N$ enters by **dividing the load inertia and damping by $N^2$** on the motor side and **multiplying the load torque by $N$** on the load side.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: The Control Problem
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET722 - ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** The Control Problem

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Robot Control Problem — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> The **Robot Control Problem** is the task of generating the necessary actuator inputs (joint torques/forces) so that the robot's actual state (joint positions, velocities, and end-effector pose) tracks a desired reference trajectory in the presence of model uncertainties, external disturbances, and nonlinear dynamics — while satisfying stability, accuracy, and transient response specifications.

Mathematically, given the nonlinear robot dynamics:

$$M(q)\ddot{q} + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q) + F(\dot{q}) = \tau$$

The control problem reduces to: **Find a control law** $\tau = f(q_d, \dot{q}_d, \ddot{q}_d, q, \dot{q}, \text{state})$ such that $q \to q_d$ and $\dot{q} \to \dot{q}_d$ as $t \to \infty$.

Where:
- $M(q)$ is the **n×n inertia matrix** (symmetric positive definite)
- $C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q}$ is the **Coriolis and centrifugal vector**
- $G(q)$ is the **gravitational torque vector**
- $F(\dot{q})$ is the **friction vector**
- $\tau$ is the **n×1 joint torque vector**
- $q_d$ is the **desired joint trajectory** (n×1)

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Driving a Car" Analogy**
> Think of robotic control exactly like driving a car on a winding mountain road:
> - **Open-Loop Control (no feedback):** You pre-compute every steering angle and pedal press from a map. If the road is slippery or a wind gust pushes you, you cannot correct — you crash.
> - **Closed-Loop Control (with feedback):** You constantly look at the road (sensors) and adjust steering and speed (actuators). This is the foundation of every robot controller.
> - **PID Controller:** The "P" (Proportional) reacts to how far you are from your lane. The "I" (Integral) corrects persistent bias like a side wind. The "D" (Derivative) anticipates a sharp curve coming.
> - **The "reference" is the planned route (trajectory).** The "error" is the gap between where you planned to be and where you actually are.

## 1.3 Why This Topic is Critical in Robotics

> [!TIP]
> **Why "The Control Problem" is a KTU High-Weight Module**
> - It is the **bridge between kinematics/dynamics (Modules 2–3) and real-world robot deployment.**
> - Every industrial robot (KUKA, ABB, Fanuc, Yaskawa) implements a control law derived from this theory.
> - In KTU ESE papers, control problems are tested through: state-space derivation, PID gain computation, stability analysis (Routh-Hurwitz / Lyapunov), and block diagram interpretations.

## 1.4 The Two Architectural Classes of Control

| Aspect | Open-Loop (Feedforward) | Closed-Loop (Feedback) |
|---|---|---|
| **Sensors required** | No | Yes (encoders, resolvers, vision) |
| **Disturbance rejection** | **Zero** | Strong |
| **Accuracy** | Low (model-dependent) | High |
| **Stability** | Guaranteed if model is correct | Needs analysis |
| **Example in robotics** | Pre-planned CNC path | Servo motor position loop |
| **KTU Exam Tag** | Theoretical question | Design/numerical problem |

> [!NOTE]
> **Industrial Standard:** Modern robot manipulators use **hierarchical closed-loop control** with at least three nested loops: current/torque (innermost) → velocity (middle) → position (outermost), each running at progressively lower sampling rates (kHz → kHz → 100s of Hz).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchical Control Architecture (Decomposition)

The robot control problem is too complex to solve in one shot. KTU expects students to recognize the **three-level hierarchy**:

### Level 1 — Joint Servo Level (Lowest, Fastest)
- Runs at **1–10 kHz** sampling rate.
- Controls individual motor current/torque.
- Pure **local feedback** for each joint.
- Implements current/torque tracking $\tau \to \tau_d$.

### Level 2 — Joint Dynamic Level (Middle)
- Runs at **100–1000 Hz**.
- Computes nonlinear compensation terms (gravity, Coriolis).
- Generates the **reference torque** for Level 1.
- Equation form: $\tau = \alpha(q)\tau' + \beta(q)$ (feedback linearization).

### Level 3 — Task Planning Level (Highest, Slowest)
- Runs at **10–100 Hz**.
- Converts task-space references (Cartesian) to joint-space references.
- Performs **inverse kinematics** and trajectory interpolation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** Each level's bandwidth must be **at least 3–5 times** the level above it for stability (frequency separation principle).

## 2.2 Types of Control Problems in Robotics

The "Control Problem" is not singular. KTU 2024 syllabus groups it as:

| Control Type | Reference Variable | Control Output | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Position Control** | $q_d(t)$ | $\tau$ | Pick-and-place |
| **Velocity Control** | $\dot{q}_d(t)$ | $\tau$ | Conveyor tracking |
| **Force Control** | $F_d$ | $\tau$ | Deburring, polishing |
| **Hybrid Position/Force** | $q_d$ in free dir, $F_d$ in constrained dir | $\tau$ | Assembly |
| **Impedance Control** | Stiffness/damping matrix | $\tau$ | Contact tasks |
| **Adaptive Control** | $q_d$ + parameter estimator | $\tau$ | Unknown payloads |
| **Computed Torque Control** | $q_d, \dot{q}_d, \ddot{q}_d$ | $\tau$ via inverse dynamics | High-precision tracking |

## 2.3 Open-Loop Control — Feedforward Architecture

The simplest solution: **ignore all errors**, just pre-compute.

$$\tau(t) = M(q_d)\ddot{q}_d + C(q_d, \dot{q}_d)\dot{q}_d + G(q_d)$$

This is the **computed torque** used as a **feedforward** term. It works only if:
1. The dynamic model is perfectly known.
2. No external disturbance acts.
3. Initial state exactly matches $q(0) = q_d(0)$, $\dot{q}(0) = \dot{q}_d(0)$.

## 2.4 Closed-Loop Control — Feedback Architecture

Define the **tracking error**:

$$e(t) = q_d(t) - q(t)$$

$$\dot{e}(t) = \dot{q}_d(t) - \dot{q}(t)$$

The general **PD (Proportional-Derivative) control law**:

$$\tau = K_p \, e + K_d \, \dot{e}$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Insight:** A pure PD controller cannot reject gravity. In practice, a **PID or PD + gravity compensation** is used:
> $$\tau = K_p \, e + K_d \, \dot{e} + \hat{G}(q)$$

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table contains every equation a KTU 2024 student must memorize for the control problem module. All symbols follow the standard **Spong, Hutchinson & Vidyasagar** convention used in KTU textbooks.

| # | Formula / Law | Description | Unit / Type |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $M(q)\ddot{q} + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q) + F(\dot{q}) = \tau$ | Full nonlinear robot dynamics | Vector (n×1) |
| 2 | $e = q_d - q$, $\dot{e} = \dot{q}_d - \dot{q}$ | Tracking error definitions | Vector (n×1) |
| 3 | $\tau = K_p e + K_d \dot{e}$ | PD control law | Torque (Nm) |
| 4 | $\tau = K_p e + K_d \dot{e} + K_i \int e \, dt$ | PID control law | Torque (Nm) |
| 5 | $\tau = M(q)\ddot{q}_d + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q}_d + G(q) + K_p e + K_d \dot{e}$ | **Computed Torque Control** | Torque (Nm) |
| 6 | $\tau = M(q)(\ddot{q}_d + K_v \dot{e} + K_p e) + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q)$ | Computed torque with linear error dynamics | Torque (Nm) |
| 7 | $\ddot{e} + K_v \dot{e} + K_p e = 0$ | Linearized error dynamics (decoupled) | Scalar (per joint) |
| 8 | $\lambda^2 + K_v \lambda + K_p = 0$ | Characteristic equation (per joint) | Polynomial |
| 9 | $\omega_n = \sqrt{K_p}$, $\zeta = K_v / (2\sqrt{K_p})$ | Natural frequency, damping ratio | rad/s, dimensionless |
| 10 | $K_p = \omega_n^2$, $K_v = 2\zeta\omega_n$ | PD gains from $\omega_n$ and $\zeta$ | Nm/rad, Nms/rad |
| 11 | $F = M\ddot{x} + B\dot{x} + Kx$ | Mechanical impedance equation | Force (N) |
| 12 | $\tau = J^T(q)F$ | Static force/torque equivalence (virtual work) | Torque (Nm) |
| 13 | $V = \frac{1}{2}\dot{e}^T M(q)\dot{e} + \frac{1}{2}e^T K_p e \geq 0$ | Lyapunov candidate (PD stability proof) | Energy (J) |
| 14 | $\dot{V} = -\dot{e}^T K_d \dot{e} \leq 0$ | Lyapunov derivative (negative semidefinite) | Power (W) |
| 15 | $\tau_{max} = M(q)\ddot{q}_{max} + G(q)$ | Static torque sizing | Nm |

> [!WARNING]
> **CRITICAL — Markdown Safety:** All absolute-value bars in the formulas above use the explicit word or context to avoid breaking markdown tables. The real LaTeX uses `\vert \cdot \vert`; in plain text the symbols are written out.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Control Strategy Used | Why |
|---|---|---|
| **Automotive assembly** | Hybrid position/force | Tightening bolts needs force, alignment needs position |
| **Surgical robotics (Da Vinci)** | Impedance + vision feedback | Safe contact with tissues |
| **Welding robots (Fanuc)** | Position + feedforward | High speed, high accuracy |
| **Space manipulators (Canadarm)** | Adaptive + computed torque | Unknown/inertia-changing payloads |
| **Quadruped robots (Spot)** | Whole-body MPC | Multi-contact stability |

## 2.7 Stability — The KTU Hot Question

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A controller is **stable** if bounded inputs produce bounded outputs (BIBO). For the nonlinear robot, we use **Lyapunov's direct method**:
> 1. Choose $V(e, \dot{e}) \geq 0$ (positive definite energy function).
> 2. Show $\dot{V} \leq 0$ (energy dissipates).
> 3. Apply LaSalle's invariance principle for asymptotic stability.
>
> For PD + gravity compensation: $V$ uses the actual inertia matrix $M(q)$, and $\dot{V} = -\dot{e}^T(K_d + F)\dot{e} \leq 0$ — this is the **standard KTU proof** expected in the exam.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — The Computed Torque Control Law (Exhaustive)

We start from the nonlinear robot dynamics:

$$M(q)\ddot{q} + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q) = \tau$$

**Step 1 — Define the desired trajectory.** Let $q_d(t)$, $\dot{q}_d(t)$, $\ddot{q}_d(t)$ be known smooth reference signals.

**Step 2 — Define error and its derivatives.**

$$e = q_d - q$$

$$\dot{e} = \dot{q}_d - \dot{q}$$

$$\ddot{e} = \ddot{q}_d - \ddot{q}$$

**Step 3 — Rearrange the dynamics to isolate $\ddot{q}$.**

$$\ddot{q} = M(q)^{-1}\bigl[\tau - C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} - G(q)\bigr]$$

**Step 4 — Substitute into the error acceleration equation.**

$$\ddot{e} = \ddot{q}_d - M(q)^{-1}\bigl[\tau - C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} - G(q)\bigr]$$

**Step 5 — Choose the control input to cancel nonlinearities and impose linear error dynamics.**

We want:

$$\ddot{e} + K_v \dot{e} + K_p e = 0$$

Rearranging:

$$\ddot{e} = -K_v \dot{e} - K_p e$$

**Step 6 — Equate Step 4 and Step 5.**

$$\ddot{q}_d - M(q)^{-1}\bigl[\tau - C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} - G(q)\bigr] = -K_v \dot{e} - K_p e$$

**Step 7 — Solve for $\tau$.**

$$\ddot{q}_d + K_v \dot{e} + K_p e = M(q)^{-1}\bigl[\tau - C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} - G(q)\bigr]$$

Multiplying both sides by $M(q)$:

$$M(q)\bigl[\ddot{q}_d + K_v \dot{e} + K_p e\bigr] = \tau - C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} - G(q)$$

**Step 8 — Final computed torque control law.**

$$\boxed{\tau = M(q)\bigl[\ddot{q}_d + K_v \dot{e} + K_p e\bigr] + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical meaning:** The first term "pushes" the joint along the desired trajectory as if the robot were a unit-mass system. The remaining terms exactly cancel the robot's own nonlinear dynamics, making it behave linearly. The error then obeys a simple second-order ODE per joint.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Stability Proof of PD + Gravity Compensation (Lyapunov)

This is the **most-asked KTU ESE question** in this module. We prove that:

$$\tau = K_p e - K_d \dot{q} + G(q)$$

(asymptotically) stabilizes the robot at $q_d$.

**Step 1 — Rewrite the closed-loop dynamics.** Substituting the control law into $M(q)\ddot{q} + C\dot{q} + G = \tau$:

$$M(q)\ddot{q} + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q) = K_p e - K_d \dot{q} + G(q)$$

Cancel $G(q)$ from both sides:

$$M(q)\ddot{q} + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} = K_p e - K_d \dot{q}$$

**Step 2 — Express in terms of error.** Using $\ddot{q} = \ddot{q}_d - \ddot{e}$ and noting that for a **regulation** problem $q_d$ is constant ($\ddot{q}_d = \dot{q}_d = 0$):

$$-M(q)\ddot{e} - C(q,\dot{q})\dot{e} = K_p e - K_d \dot{e}$$

Or equivalently:

$$M(q)\ddot{e} + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{e} + K_d \dot{e} + K_p e = 0$$

**Step 3 — Choose the Lyapunov candidate.** A standard choice uses the kinetic energy of the error plus the potential energy of the proportional error:

$$V(e, \dot{e}) = \frac{1}{2}\dot{e}^T M(q) \dot{e} + \frac{1}{2} e^T K_p e$$

**Step 4 — Verify positive definiteness.** $M(q)$ is symmetric positive definite (physical property of inertia), and $K_p$ is chosen symmetric positive definite. Therefore:

$$V(e, \dot{e}) \geq 0 \quad \text{and} \quad V = 0 \iff e = 0, \dot{e} = 0$$

**Step 5 — Differentiate $V$ with respect to time.**

$$\dot{V} = \dot{e}^T M(q) \ddot{e} + \frac{1}{2}\dot{e}^T \dot{M}(q) \dot{e} + e^T K_p \dot{e}$$

**Step 6 — Use the skew-symmetry property of $\dot{M} - 2C$ (a fundamental result in Lagrangian mechanics):**

$$\dot{e}^T \bigl[\dot{M}(q) - 2C(q,\dot{q})\bigr] \dot{e} = 0$$

This implies $\dot{e}^T \dot{M}\dot{e} = 2\dot{e}^T C \dot{e}$. Substituting:

$$\dot{V} = \dot{e}^T M(q)\ddot{e} + \dot{e}^T C(q,\dot{q})\dot{e} + e^T K_p \dot{e}$$

$$= \dot{e}^T \bigl[M(q)\ddot{e} + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{e}\bigr] + e^T K_p \dot{e}$$

**Step 7 — Substitute the closed-loop equation.** From Step 2:

$$M(q)\ddot{e} + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{e} = -K_d \dot{e} - K_p e$$

Therefore:

$$\dot{V} = \dot{e}^T(-K_d \dot{e} - K_p e) + e^T K_p \dot{e}$$

$$= -\dot{e}^T K_d \dot{e} - \dot{e}^T K_p e + e^T K_p \dot{e}$$

The last two terms cancel (scalar):

$$\boxed{\dot{V} = -\dot{e}^T K_d \dot{e} \leq 0}$$

**Step 8 — Conclude stability.** Since $K_d$ is positive definite, $\dot{V} \leq 0$ globally. By **LaSalle's invariance principle**, the largest invariant set within $\dot{V} = 0$ is $\{e = 0, \dot{e} = 0\}$, hence the origin is **globally asymptotically stable**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examination Tip:** Always state explicitly: "$M(q)$ is symmetric positive definite (a property of the kinetic energy)" and "$\dot{M} - 2C$ is skew-symmetric (a property arising from Christoffel symbols)." Examiners award full marks only when these properties are named.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — PD Gain Selection from Specifications

The standard KTU 2024 numerical problem: given desired $\omega_n$ and $\zeta$, find $K_p$ and $K_v$.

**Step 1 — The error dynamics for a single joint under computed torque is:**

$$\ddot{e}_i + K_{v,i}\dot{e}_i + K_{p,i} e_i = 0$$

**Step 2 — Compare with the standard second-order ODE:**

$$\ddot{e}_i + 2\zeta\omega_n \dot{e}_i + \omega_n^2 e_i = 0$$

**Step 3 — Match coefficients:**

$$K_{p,i} = \omega_n^2$$

$$K_{v,i} = 2\zeta\omega_n$$

**Step 4 — Example (typical KTU 2-mark sub-question):** If $\omega_n = 10$ rad/s and $\zeta = 0.707$ (critically-underdamped design):

$$K_p = 100 \, \text{Nm/rad}$$

$$K_v = 2 \times 0.707 \times 10 = 14.14 \, \text{Nms/rad}$$

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Force Control via Impedance

The robot is commanded to behave like a **mass-spring-damper** at the end-effector:

$$M_d \ddot{X} + B_d \dot{X} + K_d (X - X_d) = -F_{ext}$$

Where $X$ is the Cartesian position, $F_{ext}$ is the measured external force, and $M_d$, $B_d$, $K_d$ are the **desired inertia, damping, and stiffness matrices**.

The control law is:

$$\tau = J^T(q)\bigl[M_d^{-1}(-B_d \dot{X} - K_d(X-X_d) - F_{ext})\bigr] + G(q) + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering interpretation:** A high $K_d$ (stiff) makes the robot resist deviations → behaves like position control. A low $K_d$ (compliant) makes it yield to forces → behaves like force control. Impedance control unifies both.

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — All Control Strategies

| Strategy | Disturbance Rejection | Model Dependency | Computation | Best For | KTU Marks Weight |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Open-loop (Feedforward) | None | Total | Low | Repetitive, known tasks | 2 |
| Joint PD | Limited (no $G$ comp.) | None | Low | Slow motion | 3 |
| Joint PID | Better (steady-state) | None | Low | Constant load | 3 |
| Computed Torque | Excellent | Total (model) | High | Fast, accurate tracking | 7 |
| Adaptive Control | Excellent | Partial (param. update) | Very High | Unknown payloads | 5 |
| Hybrid Position/Force | Excellent | Partial | High | Constrained tasks | 7 |
| Impedance Control | Excellent | Partial | High | Contact-rich tasks | 5 |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hierarchical Control Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    TaskPlanner["Task Planner: Path and Trajectory Generation"]
    TrajRef["Trajectory Reference: qd t, dqdt, d2qdt2"]
    InvDyn["Inverse Dynamics / Computed Torque: Mq, Cq, Gq"]
    CtrlLaw["Control Law: tau equals Kp e plus Kd edot"]
    Plant["Robot Plant: Mqddq plus Cqdq plus Gq equals tau"]
    Sensor["Joint Encoders: q measured, dq measured"]
    ErrorCalc["Error Computation: e equals qd minus q"]
    Fext["External Disturbance and Friction"]

    TaskPlanner --> TrajRef
    TrajRef --> InvDyn
    InvDyn --> CtrlLaw
    CtrlLaw --> Plant
    Plant --> Sensor
    Sensor --> ErrorCalc
    TrajRef --> ErrorCalc
    ErrorCalc --> CtrlLaw
    Fext -.-> Plant
```

## 4.2 Computed Torque Control — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    RefPos["Desired qd"]
    SumNode1((+))
    Error["Error e = qd - q"]
    Derivative["Differentiator: dqdt, d2qdt2"]
    SumNode2((+))
    Feedforward["Feedforward Torque: Mq d2qdt2 plus Cq dqdt plus Gq"]
    Feedback["Feedback Torque: Kp e plus Kd edot"]
    SumNode3((+))
    TorqueOut["Total Torque tau"]
    Robot["Robot Dynamics: Mq ddotq plus Cq dqdt plus Gq"]
    Output["Joint Position q"]

    RefPos --> SumNode1
    SumNode1 -->|e| Error
    RefPos --> Derivative
    Error -->|e, edot| SumNode2
    Derivative -->|dqd, d2qd| Feedforward
    SumNode2 --> Feedback
    Feedforward --> SumNode3
    Feedback --> SumNode3
    SumNode3 --> TorqueOut
    TorqueOut --> Robot
    Robot --> Output
    Output --> SumNode1
```

## 4.3 Hybrid Position/Force Control Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    TaskSpace["Task Space Reference: Xd and Fd"]
    Selector["Compliance Selection Matrix: S and S complement"]
    PosBranch["Position Branch: X controller to q controller"]
    ForceBranch["Force Branch: F controller to q controller"]
    SumNode((+))
    JointCtrl["Joint Torque Computation: tau equals J transpose F plus dynamics"]
    Manip["Manipulator with Environment Contact"]
    FTSensor["Force Torque Sensor at Wrist"]

    TaskSpace --> Selector
    Selector -->|S| PosBranch
    Selector -->|I minus S| ForceBranch
    PosBranch --> SumNode
    ForceBranch --> SumNode
    SumNode --> JointCtrl
    JointCtrl --> Manip
    Manip --> FTSensor
    FTSensor --> PosBranch
    FTSensor --> ForceBranch
```

## 4.4 Stability Analysis Flow (Lyapunov Procedure)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start["Start: Define error e and edot"]
    Candidate["Choose V greater than 0: V equals half edot transpose M edot plus half e transpose Kp e"]
    Differentiate["Differentiate: dV dt"]
    UseProperty["Use skew symmetry: edot transpose half Mdot minus C edot equals 0"]
    Substitute["Substitute closed loop dynamics"]
    Simplify["Simplify: dV dt equals minus edot transpose Kd edot"]
    Conclude["Conclude: dV dt less than or equal to 0 and asymptotic stability"]

    Start --> Candidate
    Candidate --> Differentiate
    Differentiate --> UseProperty
    UseProperty --> Substitute
    Substitute --> Simplify
    Simplify --> Conclude
```

## 4.5 Control Law Selection — Decision Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start["Is model known accurately?"]
    Y1["Yes: use Computed Torque"]
    N1["No: use Adaptive or Robust Control"]
    Q2["Is contact with environment?"]
    Y2["Yes: use Impedance or Hybrid Control"]
    N2["No: use Position or Trajectory Control"]
    Q3["Is payload changing?"]
    Y3["Yes: use Adaptive Control"]
    N3["No: PD plus Gravity is sufficient"]

    Start -->|Yes| Y1
    Start -->|No| N1
    N1 --> Q3
    Q3 -->|Yes| Y3
    Q3 -->|No| N1
    Y1 --> Q2
    Q2 -->|Yes| Y2
    Q2 -->|No| N2
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define the Robot Control Problem. Differentiate between open-loop and closed-loop control with one robotics example each.

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Question Paper, Set B]**

**Model Answer:**

> The Robot Control Problem is the task of determining the joint actuator inputs (torques/forces) required to make the actual robot state track a desired reference trajectory, despite model uncertainties, external disturbances, and the inherent nonlinear coupling between joints.

> **Open-loop control** computes the control input from a model alone, with no feedback from sensors. It cannot correct for errors. Example: A pick-and-place robot executing a pre-computed trapezoidal velocity profile from an open-loop stepper-motor drive — accurate only if the load is exactly as modeled.

> **Closed-loop control** continuously measures the actual state (position, velocity) via encoders and computes the control input as a function of the error between desired and actual state. It actively rejects disturbances. Example: A DC servo motor in a robot joint using a PID position loop with encoder feedback.

> **[3 Marks: Definition 1M, Open-loop 1M, Closed-loop 1M]**

### Question 2: What is Computed Torque Control? Write its control law.

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer:**

> Computed Torque Control is a model-based feedback linearization technique that uses the exact nonlinear dynamics of the robot to cancel the nonlinear terms and impose linear second-order error dynamics on each joint.

> **Control law:**
> $$\tau = M(q)\bigl[\ddot{q}_d + K_v \dot{e} + K_p e\bigr] + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q)$$

> The resulting error dynamics for each joint become $\ddot{e} + K_v \dot{e} + K_p e = 0$, which is a stable linear second-order system that can be tuned using standard techniques.

> **[3 Marks: Concept 1M, Equation 1.5M, Significance 0.5M]**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Major Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A: 14 Marks

**(a)** [7 Marks] Derive the **Computed Torque Control law** for an n-DOF robotic manipulator starting from the general nonlinear dynamic equation. Show that the closed-loop error dynamics reduces to a decoupled linear second-order system for each joint.

**(b)** [7 Marks] For a single joint of a robot manipulator under computed torque control, the desired closed-loop performance is a natural frequency of **8 rad/s** and damping ratio of **0.9**. Compute the proportional and derivative gains $K_p$ and $K_v$. If the joint inertia is $M = 2$ kg·m², compute the maximum torque required when the joint must accelerate from rest to $\dot{q} = 1$ rad/s in $0.1$ s while lifting a payload that produces $G = 5$ Nm of gravity torque.

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Step 1:** The nonlinear dynamics is:
$$M(q)\ddot{q} + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q) = \tau$$

**Step 2:** Define $e = q_d - q$ so $\ddot{q} = \ddot{q}_d - \ddot{e}$.

**Step 3:** Substitute and solve for $\tau$ to impose $\ddot{e} + K_v\dot{e} + K_p e = 0$.

$$\tau = M(q)[\ddot{q}_d + K_v\dot{e} + K_p e] + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q)$$

**Step 4:** Substitute back — all nonlinear terms cancel, leaving:
$$\ddot{e} + K_v\dot{e} + K_p e = 0$$

**Step 5:** This is n independent linear ODEs (one per joint), so the system is **decoupled**. **[Full derivation as in Section 3.1 of these notes carries 7 marks.]**

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Step 1 — Compute $K_p$ and $K_v$:**
$$K_p = \omega_n^2 = 8^2 = 64 \, \text{rad}^2/\text{s}^2$$
$$K_v = 2\zeta\omega_n = 2 \times 0.9 \times 8 = 14.4 \, \text{s}^{-1}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the required angular acceleration:**
$$\alpha = \frac{\Delta \dot{q}}{\Delta t} = \frac{1 - 0}{0.1} = 10 \, \text{rad/s}^2$$

**Step 3 — Compute the feedforward inertial torque:**
$$\tau_{inertia} = M\alpha = 2 \times 10 = 20 \, \text{Nm}$$

**Step 4 — Add gravity torque:**
$$\tau_{max} = \tau_{inertia} + G = 20 + 5 = 25 \, \text{Nm}$$

> **Answer: $K_p = 64$, $K_v = 14.4$, $\tau_{max} = 25$ Nm.** **[7 Marks]**

---

### Question B: 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

**(a)** [7 Marks] Using **Lyapunov's direct method**, prove that the control law $\tau = K_p e - K_d \dot{q} + G(q)$ globally asymptotically stabilizes an n-DOF robot manipulator at a constant desired configuration $q_d$. Clearly state the properties of $M(q)$ and the relation between $\dot{M}$ and $C$ used in the proof.

**(b)** [7 Marks] Explain **Hybrid Position/Force Control** with a neat block diagram. How does the compliance selection matrix $S$ work? Why is pure position control insufficient for tasks like writing on a wall or polishing a surface?

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Step 1:** Use the regulation error $e = q_d - q$. Substitute control law into $M\ddot{q} + C\dot{q} + G = \tau$:

$$M\ddot{q} + C\dot{q} = K_p e - K_d \dot{q}$$

For constant $q_d$: $\ddot{q} = -\ddot{e}$, $\dot{q} = -\dot{e}$, yielding:
$$M\ddot{e} + C\dot{e} + K_d\dot{e} + K_p e = 0$$

**Step 2:** Choose Lyapunov candidate:
$$V = \frac{1}{2}\dot{e}^T M(q)\dot{e} + \frac{1}{2}e^T K_p e \geq 0$$

**Step 3:** Differentiate and use the **skew-symmetry property** $\dot{e}^T(\dot{M} - 2C)\dot{e} = 0$:

$$\dot{V} = \dot{e}^T M\ddot{e} + \frac{1}{2}\dot{e}^T \dot{M}\dot{e} + e^T K_p \dot{e}$$

Using the skew-symmetry: $\dot{e}^T \dot{M}\dot{e} = 2\dot{e}^T C \dot{e}$, so:
$$\dot{V} = \dot{e}^T(M\ddot{e} + C\dot{e}) + e^T K_p \dot{e}$$

**Step 4:** Substitute the closed-loop equation $M\ddot{e} + C\dot{e} = -K_d \dot{e} - K_p e$:

$$\dot{V} = \dot{e}^T(-K_d \dot{e} - K_p e) + e^T K_p \dot{e} = -\dot{e}^T K_d \dot{e} \leq 0$$

**Step 5:** Since $K_d > 0$, $\dot{V} \leq 0$ globally, and by LaSalle's invariance principle, the origin $(e, \dot{e}) = (0, 0)$ is **globally asymptotically stable**. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Hybrid Position/Force Control** allows the robot to **simultaneously** control position in unconstrained directions and force in constrained directions. This is essential for tasks like:

- **Writing on a chalkboard:** position must be controlled on the board plane, but force must be controlled normal to the board.
- **Polishing a surface:** position along the surface, contact force perpendicular.
- **Inserting a peg:** position along insertion axis, low contact force on the side.

The **compliance selection matrix** $S$ is a diagonal matrix with entries 0 or 1:
- $S_i = 1$ → the i-th Cartesian direction uses **position control**.
- $S_i = 0$ → the i-th Cartesian direction uses **force control**.

$$\tau = J^T(q)\bigl[S \cdot F_{pos\,ctrl} + (I - S) \cdot F_{force\,ctrl}\bigr] + \text{dynamics compensation}$$

**Why pure position control fails:** In a constrained direction, the environment prevents motion regardless of how much position error exists. The position controller would command ever-increasing torque, leading to:
1. Excessive contact force (potentially damaging the robot or environment).
2. Mechanical stress, vibrations, instability.
3. Loss of the task (e.g., broken chalk, scratched surface).

**Block diagram (text description):** Reference $(X_d, F_d)$ → Selection matrix $S$ → [Position loop $\to J^{-1}$ $\to$ $\tau$] and [Force loop $\to J^T$ $\to$ $\tau$] → Manipulator → Force/torque sensor feedback.

> **[7 Marks: Concept 2M, Selection matrix 2M, Block diagram 1.5M, Failure of position-only 1.5M]**

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Commonly Lose Marks in "The Control Problem"**
> 1. **Forgetting the skew-symmetry property:** In Lyapunov proofs, students write $V = \frac{1}{2}\dot{e}^T M \dot{e}$ but then forget to invoke $\dot{M} - 2C$ being skew-symmetric. **Examiner deducts 2 marks.**
> 2. **Using $\dot{q}$ instead of $\dot{e}$ in feedback:** The PD law is $\tau = K_p e + K_d \dot{e}$, **not** $K_p e + K_d \dot{q}$. With a moving trajectory, these are not equal.
> 3. **Confusing stability types:** "Stability" ≠ "asymptotic stability" ≠ "exponential stability." Lyapunov gives you asymptotic stability; LaSalle extends it. **Examiner deducts 1 mark for sloppy terminology.**
> 4. **Forgetting units:** $K_p$ is in Nm/rad, $K_v$ in Nms/rad. A unit mismatch is a common error.
> 5. **Neglecting gravity in PD:** A pure PD without $G(q)$ compensation leaves a **steady-state error** in vertical joints. The KTU 2024 scheme emphasizes "complete" control laws.
> 6. **Computing torque sign:** $\tau = M\ddot{q} + \ldots$ **drives** the joint. Students sometimes use the wrong sign for feedback.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4: The Control Problem**

- [x] The **robot control problem** = find $\tau(t)$ so that $q \to q_d$ under nonlinear dynamics.
- [x] **Open-loop** = no feedback (computed torque as feedforward only); **Closed-loop** = feedback (PD, PID, computed torque).
- [x] **Hierarchical control** = 3 levels: task (slow) → dynamics compensation (mid) → joint servo (fast).
- [x] **PD control law:** $\tau = K_p e + K_d \dot{e}$. Add $\hat{G}(q)$ for gravity.
- [x] **PID control law:** adds $K_i \int e\,dt$ for steady-state error removal.
- [x] **Computed Torque law:** $\tau = M(q)[\ddot{q}_d + K_v\dot{e} + K_p e] + C\dot{q} + G$.
- [x] **Decoupled linear error dynamics:** $\ddot{e} + K_v\dot{e} + K_p e = 0$ per joint.
- [x] **PD gain from specs:** $K_p = \omega_n^2$, $K_v = 2\zeta\omega_n$.
- [x] **Stability proof uses:** $V = \frac{1}{2}\dot{e}^T M \dot{e} + \frac{1}{2} e^T K_p e$ and skew-symmetry of $\dot{M} - 2C$.
- [x] **Lyapunov result:** $\dot{V} = -\dot{e}^T K_d \dot{e} \leq 0$ → asymptotic stability (with LaSalle).
- [x] **Force control** needed for contact tasks; **Hybrid control** uses selection matrix $S$.
- [x] **Impedance control** unifies position and force: $M_d\ddot{X} + B_d\dot{X} + K_d(X - X_d) = -F_{ext}$.
- [x] **Static force equivalence:** $\tau = J^T(q) F$ (virtual work principle).
- [x] **Adaptive control** = computed torque + online parameter estimator for unknown payloads.
- [x] **Bandwidth rule:** inner loop must be **3–5× faster** than outer loop.
- [x] **Open-loop limitation:** zero disturbance rejection; pure PD cannot reject constant gravity.

> **Final KTU Mantra:** *"A good robot controller does three things — (1) cancels the robot's own nonlinear dynamics, (2) imposes stable linear error dynamics, and (3) rejects disturbances via feedback. Computed Torque + Lyapunov stability is the exam's gold standard."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
