topics.title: Single Axis PID Control - Its Disadvantages
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET722 - ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Single Axis PID Control - Its Disadvantages

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

In KTU 2024 Scheme Robotics (OEMET722), **Single Axis PID Control** refers to a classical control strategy where each individual joint (axis) of a robotic manipulator is treated as an independent, decoupled **Single-Input Single-Output (SISO)** system and is controlled using a dedicated **Proportional-Integral-Derivative (PID)** controller. The control law for each joint generates a torque command $\tau_i(t)$ based solely on the local position error, its integral, and its derivative, without explicitly accounting for the dynamic interactions (coupling) that originate from the motion of the other links of the manipulator.

The generic continuous-time PID law applied to the $i$-th joint is given by:

$$\tau_i(t) = K_{P,i} \, e_i(t) + K_{I,i} \int_0^{t} e_i(\sigma)\, d\sigma + K_{D,i} \frac{d e_i(t)}{dt}$$

where $e_i(t) = q_{d,i}(t) - q_i(t)$ is the joint position error, and $K_P, K_I, K_D$ are the proportional, integral, and derivative gains, respectively.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** In Module 4, the syllabus explicitly demands a critical evaluation of single-axis PID control. The examiner expects students to articulate *why* this decentralized scheme, although widely used in industry, is fundamentally inadequate for high-performance robotic manipulators with coupled, nonlinear dynamics.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a novice driver learning to drive a **manual transmission car on a busy hill road**. They are told to control only the **brake pedal** (single axis) using simple rules: "press harder if speed is too high (P), remember how long you've been over-speed (I), and anticipate future speed using the slope ahead (D)." The driver is **not allowed to use the clutch, accelerator, or steering**.

- The car is **really a multi-axis system** (brake + accelerator + clutch + steering + gear).
- Controlling only the brake while ignoring the other axes will cause the car to **stall on inclines** or **slip on declines**.
- The road slope and gear state **constantly change**, so the "good" brake gain at one moment is terrible at another.

Similarly, in robotics, each joint of a multi-link manipulator is **dynamically coupled** with all other joints. Treating a single joint as if it existed in isolation is like our novice driver — it works for slow, light tasks but fails miserably during fast, coupled, or loaded motions. **This is the central disadvantage of single-axis PID control.**

## 1.3 Key Physical Parameters & Standards

The relevant physical metrics for a single-axis PID joint servo in robotics are:

- **Tracking error bound:** typically $\pm 0.1^{\circ}$ to $\pm 1^{\circ}$ in industrial arms.
- **Bandwidth:** **5 Hz to 20 Hz** for conventional single-axis PID vs. **50 Hz to 100 Hz** for advanced model-based schemes.
- **Settling time:** **100 ms – 500 ms** typical.
- **Steady-state error:** Ideally zero with integral action, but degrades under actuator saturation (integral windup).
- **Gain margin / Phase margin** requirements: **$\geq 6$ dB / $30^{\circ}$ – $60^{\circ}$** for robust stability.

> [!NOTE]
> The unit **radian (rad)** is used for joint position and **newton-meter (N·m)** for joint torque in the standard SI convention followed by KTU 2024 modules.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown — Why Single-Axis PID Fails

The complete rigid-body dynamics of an $n$-joint serial manipulator is described by the well-known **Euler–Lagrange equation**:

$$M(q)\,\ddot{q} + C(q,\dot{q})\,\dot{q} + G(q) + F(\dot{q}) = \tau$$

where:
- $M(q)$ is the $n \times n$ **symmetric positive-definite inertia matrix**,
- $C(q,\dot{q})\,\dot{q}$ is the **Coriolis and centrifugal torque vector**,
- $G(q)$ is the **gravitational torque vector**,
- $F(\dot{q})$ is the **friction torque vector**,
- $\tau$ is the **applied joint torque vector**,
- $q$ is the $n \times 1$ vector of joint positions.

A **single-axis PID controller** replaces this full vector equation with $n$ independent scalar equations of the form:

$$\tau_i = K_{P,i} e_i + K_{I,i} \int e_i\, dt + K_{D,i} \dot{e}_i \quad \text{for } i = 1, 2, \ldots, n$$

This implicit assumption of independence is the root of all disadvantages.

## 2.2 The Major Disadvantages (Structured Logic)

### 2.2.1 Disadvantage 1 — Neglect of Dynamic Coupling (Inter-Axis Coupling)
- **Why it happens:** Off-diagonal elements $M_{ij}(q), \; i \neq j$ of the inertia matrix couple adjacent joints. When joint $j$ accelerates, it injects a disturbance torque $M_{ij}(q)\ddot{q}_j$ into joint $i$.
- **How it manifests:** Tracking error, oscillation, and instability when multiple joints move simultaneously at high speed.
- **Why PID fails:** PID only "sees" the local error of joint $i$; it has no knowledge of $\ddot{q}_j$ or $M_{ij}$.

### 2.2.2 Disadvantage 2 — Configuration-Dependent Inertia (Non-stationary Plant)
- **Why it happens:** $M(q)$ depends on the pose $q$. For example, in a 2-link planar arm, the effective inertia at the shoulder is $I_1 + I_2 + 2 m_2 l_1 l_{c2} \cos(q_2) + m_2 l_1^2$, which is **minimum when the arm is fully extended** and **maximum when folded**.
- **How it manifests:** A PID gain tuned for one configuration is too aggressive (overshoot, instability) in another, and too sluggish in a third.
- **Why PID fails:** PID gains are constants; the plant varies with $q$.

### 2.2.3 Disadvantage 3 — Gravity and Payload Variation
- **Why it happens:** $G(q)$ depends on link masses and the external payload. Pick-and-place operations routinely change the payload.
- **How it manifests:** Steady-state position drift in vertical (gravity-loaded) joints, and asymmetric response between upward and downward motions.
- **Why PID fails:** Integral action eventually compensates, but only *after* a transient error; in high-speed tracking this lag is unacceptable.

### 2.2.4 Disadvantage 4 — Nonlinear Friction
- **Why it happens:** Real joints exhibit **Coulomb friction, static (stiction) friction, and viscous friction**, all of which are nonlinear in $\dot{q}$.
- **How it manifests:** **Stick–slip motion** at low velocities, **limit cycles** near setpoints, **dead zones** that the linear PID cannot resolve.
- **Why PID fails:** Linear superposition does not hold in the presence of nonlinear friction; derivative action amplifies high-frequency friction noise.

### 2.2.5 Disadvantage 5 — Integral Windup Under Actuator Saturation
- **Why it happens:** Actuators have a maximum torque $\tau_{\max}$. During large transients, the PID output exceeds this bound, but the integrator keeps accumulating error.
- **How it manifests:** Large **overshoot**, prolonged **undershoot**, and possible **instability** when the system finally desaturates.
- **Why PID fails:** Standard PID has no anti-windup mechanism in its basic textbook form.

### 2.2.6 Disadvantage 6 — Lack of Feed-forward Compensation
- **Why it happens:** Computed-torque control can pre-compute the inverse dynamics $\tau = M(q)\ddot{q}_d + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q)$ and apply it directly, but pure PID is purely reactive.
- **How it manifests:** **Latency** in tracking fast trajectories; the controller is always "catching up" to a known desired motion.
- **Why PID fails:** Feedback-only control reduces closed-loop bandwidth; reference tracking is fundamentally bandwidth-limited.

### 2.2.7 Disadvantage 7 — Tuning Difficulty
- **Why it happens:** Classical tuning (Ziegler–Nichols, Cohen–Coon) presumes a linear time-invariant (LTI) plant. Robotic joints are **nonlinear** and **time-varying**.
- **How it manifests:** Gains valid for one trajectory segment are invalid for another; frequent re-tuning is required.
- **Why PID fails:** It relies on a single operating-point linearization.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Quantity / Concept** | **Equation / Expression** | **Units** | **Remark** |
|---|---|---|---|
| PID control law | $\tau_i = K_P e_i + K_I \int e_i\, dt + K_D \dot{e}_i$ | N·m | Per-joint SISO form |
| Position error | $e_i(t) = q_{d,i}(t) - q_i(t)$ | rad | Tracking error |
| Full manipulator dynamics | $M(q)\ddot{q} + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q) + F(\dot{q}) = \tau$ | N·m | Euler–Lagrange form |
| Gravity torque (2-link planar) | $G_2 = m_2 g l_{c2} \cos(q_1 + q_2) + (m_1 l_{c1} + m_2 l_1)\cos(q_1)$ | N·m | Configuration-dependent |
| Effective inertia (2-link shoulder) | $M_{11} = I_1 + I_2 + 2 m_2 l_1 l_{c2}\cos(q_2) + m_2 l_1^2$ | kg·m² | $\cos(q_2)$ coupling term |
| Coriolis torque (2-link shoulder) | $C_{12} = -m_2 l_1 l_{c2} \sin(q_2)\dot{q}_2$ | N·m | Velocity-dependent coupling |
| Coulomb + viscous friction | $F(\dot{q}) = F_c\,\text{sgn}(\dot{q}) + F_v \dot{q}$ | N·m | Nonlinear, non-smooth |
| Integrator windup rate | $\dot{e}_I = e_i$ when $\vert\tau_i\vert < \tau_{\max}$ | — | Stops at saturation |
| Ziegler–Nichols $K_u$ (ultimate) | $K_u = 4 \xi \omega_n$ approx. for 2nd-order plant | — | Linear-only validity |
| Closed-loop bandwidth (PID) | $\omega_{bw} \approx \sqrt{K_P/M_{\text{eff}}}$ | rad/s | Plant-inertia-dependent |

> [!NOTE]
> In the table above, $\vert x \vert$ is rendered as `\vert x \vert` to keep the markdown table syntax intact — examiners frequently deduct marks when formulas break in answer sheets.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Although the KTU 2024 syllabus highlights the *disadvantages* of single-axis PID, this scheme is **not obsolete**. It remains the **workhorse of industrial robotics** in:
- **SCARA robots** in pick-and-place (low speed, decoupled motion),
- **Cartesian gantries** in PCB assembly (axes physically decoupled by linear rails),
- **CNC machines** where axes are independently driven,
- **Educational and low-cost manipulators** where simplicity outweighs performance.

The lesson is: **use single-axis PID when the application is slow, lightly coupled, and payload-constant**. Switch to **computed-torque, adaptive, or robust control** when high-speed, high-accuracy, or variable-payload manipulation is required.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Why a Single-Axis PID Cannot Stabilize a Coupled 2-Link Arm

We will show, through a rigorous analysis, why independent single-axis PID controllers fail on a coupled manipulator.

### Step 1 — Write the Coupled Dynamics

Consider a **2-link planar manipulator** with link masses $m_1, m_2$, lengths $l_1, l_2$, and center-of-mass distances $l_{c1}, l_{c2}$. The symmetric inertia matrix is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
M(q) &= \begin{bmatrix}
M_{11} & M_{12} \\
M_{21} & M_{22}
\end{bmatrix}, \quad
M_{11} = I_1 + I_2 + 2 m_2 l_1 l_{c2} \cos(q_2) + m_2 l_1^2 \\
M_{12} &= M_{21} = I_2 + m_2 l_1 l_{c2} \cos(q_2) \\
M_{22} &= I_2
\end{aligned}
$$

The Coriolis/centrifugal vector is:

$$
C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} = \begin{bmatrix}
- m_2 l_1 l_{c2} \sin(q_2) \, \dot{q}_2^2 - 2 m_2 l_1 l_{c2} \sin(q_2) \, \dot{q}_1 \dot{q}_2 \\
m_2 l_1 l_{c2} \sin(q_2) \, \dot{q}_1^2
\end{bmatrix}
$$

The gravity vector is:

$$
G(q) = \begin{bmatrix}
(m_1 l_{c1} + m_2 l_1) g \cos(q_1) + m_2 l_{c2} g \cos(q_1+q_2) \\
m_2 l_{c2} g \cos(q_1 + q_2)
\end{bmatrix}
$$

### Step 2 — Apply a Single-Axis PID Controller

Assume the user applies two **independent** single-axis PID controllers:

$$
\tau_1 = K_{P1} e_1 + K_{I1} \int e_1\, dt + K_{D1} \dot{e}_1, \quad \tau_2 = K_{P2} e_2 + K_{I2} \int e_2\, dt + K_{D2} \dot{e}_2
$$

Substituting into the dynamics:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau_1 &= M_{11}\ddot{q}_1 + M_{12}\ddot{q}_2 - m_2 l_1 l_{c2} \sin(q_2)\,\dot{q}_2^{\,2} - 2 m_2 l_1 l_{c2} \sin(q_2)\,\dot{q}_1\dot{q}_2 + G_1(q) \\
\tau_2 &= M_{21}\ddot{q}_1 + M_{22}\ddot{q}_2 + m_2 l_1 l_{c2} \sin(q_2)\,\dot{q}_1^{\,2} + G_2(q)
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Isolate the Coupling Torque

Re-arrange the first equation to express $\ddot{q}_1$:

$$
\ddot{q}_1 = \frac{1}{M_{11}}\Bigl[\tau_1 - M_{12}\ddot{q}_2 + m_2 l_1 l_{c2} \sin(q_2)\,\dot{q}_2^{\,2} + 2 m_2 l_1 l_{c2} \sin(q_2)\,\dot{q}_1\dot{q}_2 - G_1(q)\Bigr]
$$

The term $-M_{12}\ddot{q}_2$ is the **disturbance torque** that joint 2's acceleration injects into joint 1. The single-axis PID has **no mechanism to know about or cancel** this term.

### Step 4 — Compute the Uncompensated Tracking Error

Define the position error $e_1 = q_{d1} - q_1$. Differentiating twice and substituting the expression for $\ddot{q}_1$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\ddot{e}_1 &= \ddot{q}_{d1} - \ddot{q}_1 \\
&= \ddot{q}_{d1} - \frac{1}{M_{11}}\Bigl[\tau_1 - M_{12}\ddot{q}_2 + m_2 l_1 l_{c2} \sin(q_2)\,\dot{q}_2^{\,2} + 2 m_2 l_1 l_{c2} \sin(q_2)\,\dot{q}_1\dot{q}_2 - G_1(q)\Bigr]
\end{aligned}
$$

Substituting $\tau_1 = K_{P1} e_1 + K_{I1} \int e_1 + K_{D1} \dot{e}_1$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\ddot{e}_1 &= \ddot{q}_{d1} - \frac{K_{P1}}{M_{11}} e_1 - \frac{K_{I1}}{M_{11}} \int e_1\, dt - \frac{K_{D1}}{M_{11}} \dot{e}_1 \\
&\quad + \frac{M_{12}}{M_{11}}\ddot{q}_2 - \frac{m_2 l_1 l_{c2} \sin(q_2)}{M_{11}}\dot{q}_2^{\,2} - \frac{2 m_2 l_1 l_{c2} \sin(q_2)}{M_{11}}\dot{q}_1\dot{q}_2 + \frac{G_1(q)}{M_{11}}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Identify the Residual Error Sources

Group the terms that the PID **cannot cancel**:

$$
\ddot{e}_1 = -\frac{K_{P1}}{M_{11}} e_1 - \frac{K_{D1}}{M_{11}} \dot{e}_1 - \frac{K_{I1}}{M_{11}} \int e_1\, dt + \underbrace{\ddot{q}_{d1}}_{\text{reference accel.}} + \underbrace{\frac{M_{12}}{M_{11}}\ddot{q}_2 + \frac{m_2 l_1 l_{c2} \sin(q_2)}{M_{11}}\bigl(\dot{q}_2^{\,2} + 2 \dot{q}_1\dot{q}_2\bigr) + \frac{G_1(q)}{M_{11}}}_{\text{Uncompensated disturbance } d_1(q,\dot{q},\ddot{q})}
$$

The closed-loop tracking dynamics are therefore:

$$
\ddot{e}_1 + \frac{K_{D1}}{M_{11}}\dot{e}_1 + \frac{K_{P1}}{M_{11}} e_1 + \frac{K_{I1}}{M_{11}} \int e_1\, dt = d_1(q,\dot{q},\ddot{q}) + \ddot{q}_{d1}
$$

The PID drives the **homogeneous** part to zero, but the **disturbance** $d_1$ and the **reference acceleration** $\ddot{q}_{d1}$ remain. In steady state, the integrator tries to absorb $d_1$, but only after a visible transient.

### Step 6 — Show that $d_1$ is Bounded but Non-Zero

Taking the Euclidean norm of the disturbance vector:

$$
\|d_1\| \leq \frac{\vert M_{12}\vert}{M_{11}^{\min}} \ddot{q}_2^{\max} + \frac{m_2 l_1 l_{c2}}{M_{11}^{\min}} \bigl(\dot{q}_2^{\max}\bigr)^2 + 2 \frac{m_2 l_1 l_{c2}}{M_{11}^{\min}} \dot{q}_1^{\max}\dot{q}_2^{\max} + \frac{G_1^{\max}}{M_{11}^{\min}}
$$

This bound is **non-zero whenever $\ddot{q}_2, \dot{q}_1, \dot{q}_2$, or the gravity term is non-zero**. Hence, a single-axis PID cannot drive the steady-state error to zero *instantaneously*; it only attenuates it over a time-scale set by $K_{I1}$.

### Step 7 — Conclude: PID is Inherently Limited

A single-axis PID achieves **bounded tracking**, not **asymptotic perfect tracking**, on a coupled manipulator. This is the formal, mathematical foundation of the **first disadvantage**.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Disadvantage → Mechanism → Effect

| **#** | **Disadvantage** | **Underlying Mechanism** | **Observable Effect in Hardware** | **Remedy** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Inter-axis coupling** | Off-diagonal $M_{ij}$, $C_{ij}$ terms inject disturbance torques. | Oscillation when 2+ joints move together. | **Computed-torque / feedback linearization.** |
| 2 | **Configuration-varying inertia** | $M(q)$ depends on $q$; $M_{11}$ varies by 3–5× across workspace. | Overshoot at one pose, sluggishness at another. | **Gain scheduling** or **adaptive control.** |
| 3 | **Gravity / payload variation** | $G(q)$ depends on link masses and external load. | Sag in vertical joints; asymmetric up/down response. | **Gravity compensation feed-forward.** |
| 4 | **Nonlinear friction** | Coulomb + stiction + viscous terms. | Stick–slip near setpoint; tracking dead-band. | **Friction compensation** (LuGre, observer-based). |
| 5 | **Integral windup** | Integrator accumulates during actuator saturation. | Large overshoot, undershoot after desaturation. | **Anti-windup** (clamping, back-calculation). |
| 6 | **No feed-forward path** | PID reacts only to error, not to the desired trajectory. | Tracking lag in fast trajectories. | **Feed-forward + feedback** (two-degree-of-freedom control). |
| 7 | **Tuning difficulty** | Plant is nonlinear & time-varying; classical tuning assumes LTI. | One set of gains works only locally. | **Adaptive / robust / model-based controllers.** |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Single-Axis PID Control of One Joint

The following Mermaid block diagram shows the **classical single-axis PID structure** for one robot joint, including the disturbances that PID cannot reject.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    refA[Desired joint position qd] --> sumA[Sum junction]
    sumA --> ctrlA[PID Controller]
    ctrlA --> satA[Actuator saturation]
    satA --> plantA[Robot joint plant]
    plantA --> outA[Actual joint position q]
    outA --> fbA[Feedback to sum junction]
    distA[Coupling torque M12 qddot2] -.-> plantA
    gravA[Gravity torque G q] -.-> plantA
    fricA[Friction F qdot] -.-> plantA
```

> [!NOTE]
> The dotted lines represent **disturbance inputs** entering the plant. The PID has **no access** to these signals; it can only react after they propagate through the plant and alter $q$.

## 4.2 Functional Architecture — Why Multi-Axis Coupling Defeats Single-Axis PID

This is a high-level functional architecture mapping the **interaction** between two independently PID-controlled joints of a 2-link manipulator.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Joint1Axis["Joint 1 Independent PID Loop"]
        qd1[Reference qd1] --> e1[Error e1]
        e1 --> pid1[PID 1]
        pid1 --> tau1[Torque tau1]
        tau1 --> j1[Plant Joint 1]
        j1 --> q1[Position q1]
        q1 --> e1
    end

    subgraph Joint2Axis["Joint 2 Independent PID Loop"]
        qd2[Reference qd2] --> e2[Error e2]
        e2 --> pid2[PID 2]
        pid2 --> tau2[Torque tau2]
        tau2 --> j2[Plant Joint 2]
        j2 --> q2[Position q2]
        q2 --> e2
    end

    j1 <-. M12 qddot2 coupling .-> j2
    j1 <-. Coriolis from qdot2 .-> j2
    j2 <-. M21 qddot1 coupling .-> j1
    j2 <-. Coriolis from qdot1 .-> j1
    j1 <-. Gravity G1 q .-> grav[Gravity Vector]
    j2 <-. Gravity G2 q .-> grav
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The double-headed dotted arrows between the two plant blocks represent the **un-modelled dynamic coupling**. Two independent PID loops with **no coupling-aware feed-forward** will always leave residual tracking errors proportional to the magnitude of these interconnections.

## 4.3 Closed-Loop Error-Flow Diagram (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Trajectory Generator] --> B[qd, qdotd, qddotd]
    B --> C{PID Control}
    C --> D[Torque tau]
    D --> E[Coupled Manipulator Dynamics]
    E --> F[Actual q, qdot]
    F --> G[Error e = qd - q]
    G --> C
    H[Disturbances: Coupling, Gravity, Friction] -.-> E
    style H stroke-dasharray: 5 5
```

The dashed arrow entering the manipulator block from "Disturbances" is the central reason single-axis PID fails: those disturbances are **inside the loop** and **unmeasured** by the controller.

## 4.4 Counterpart — Computed-Torque Control Block (Why It Works)

For pedagogical comparison, the **model-based computed-torque controller** that *cures* these disadvantages is shown below.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R[qd] --> S[qd, qdotd, qddotd]
    S --> CT[Inverse Dynamics Model M qddot + C qdot + G]
    CT --> TF[Torque tau]
    TF --> M[True Manipulator]
    M --> Q[Actual q]
    Q --> FB[Feedback to outer loop]
    FB --> ERR[e = qd - q]
    ERR --> OUTPID[Outer PID: 7 to 20 Hz]
    OUTPID --> S
```

> [!NOTE]
> In computed-torque control, the **inverse dynamics** block explicitly computes and cancels the coupling, gravity, and Coriolis terms *before* they reach the plant, leaving a linear error dynamics to the outer PID. This is precisely what the single-axis PID lacks.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question Paper, Module 4]
**List any three disadvantages of single-axis PID control in robotic manipulators.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Neglect of inter-axis dynamic coupling:** The off-diagonal terms of the inertia matrix $M_{ij}(q), \; i \neq j$ and the Coriolis vector $C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q}$ couple adjacent joints, but a single-axis PID controller treats each joint as a SISO system and therefore cannot compensate for the disturbance torques injected by neighbouring joints. *(1 mark)*

2. **Configuration-dependent plant parameters:** The effective inertia $M_{ii}(q)$ and gravity torque $G_i(q)$ change with the manipulator pose. A PID with constant gains tuned at one operating point becomes either too aggressive (causing overshoot) or too sluggish at another. *(1 mark)*

3. **Nonlinear friction and integral windup:** Real joints exhibit Coulomb and stiction friction that linear PID cannot perfectly reject, and the integral term accumulates error during actuator saturation, producing large overshoots. *(1 mark)*

*(For full marks, any three distinct disadvantages with brief technical justification are acceptable. Examiner's discretion applies.)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Supplementary Paper, Module 4]
**Why is single-axis PID control said to have no feed-forward action? What is its consequence?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A standard single-axis PID controller computes its output **only as a function of the measured error $e(t)$ and its derivatives/integral**. It does **not** use the *desired* trajectory $q_d(t), \dot{q}_d(t), \ddot{q}_d(t)$ as an explicit input. *(1 mark)*

Hence, there is **no feed-forward path** to pre-emptively inject the torque required by the reference motion. *(1 mark)*

**Consequence:** Tracking of fast trajectories suffers from a **phase lag** and **finite steady-state error** proportional to the reference acceleration and the inter-axis coupling torques. The closed-loop bandwidth is limited because the controller only reacts *after* the error has appeared. *(1 mark)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students often *list* disadvantages without mathematically showing *why* they arise. For full marks, always (a) state the disadvantage, (b) write the equation that exposes it, and (c) explain the engineering consequence. Bullet points alone typically earn at most 8 of 14 marks.

### Question A — 14 Marks

**Q.A. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 4]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Derive the equation of motion of a 2-link planar manipulator. Identify the terms that couple the two joints.

**(b) [7 marks]** With reference to the derived model, explain in detail the **disadvantages of single-axis PID control** when applied independently to the two joints of this manipulator. Use equations to justify your answer.

---

#### Model Solution to Q.A

**(a) Derivation of 2-link planar dynamics [7 marks]**

Using the Euler–Lagrange formulation with generalized coordinates $q_1, q_2$, kinetic energy $T$ and potential energy $U$:

The position of the centre of mass of link 2 is:

$$
x_2 = l_1 \cos q_1 + l_{c2} \cos(q_1 + q_2), \quad y_2 = l_1 \sin q_1 + l_{c2} \sin(q_1 + q_2)
$$

Velocities:

$$
\dot{x}_2 = -l_1 \sin q_1 \, \dot{q}_1 - l_{c2} \sin(q_1+q_2)(\dot{q}_1 + \dot{q}_2)
$$
$$
\dot{y}_2 = l_1 \cos q_1 \, \dot{q}_1 + l_{c2} \cos(q_1+q_2)(\dot{q}_1 + \dot{q}_2)
$$

The kinetic energy is the sum of contributions from both links:

$$
T = \frac{1}{2} m_1 l_{c1}^2 \dot{q}_1^2 + \frac{1}{2} I_1 \dot{q}_1^2 + \frac{1}{2} m_2 (\dot{x}_2^2 + \dot{y}_2^2) + \frac{1}{2} I_2 (\dot{q}_1 + \dot{q}_2)^2
$$

After expansion and simplification:

$$
T = \frac{1}{2}\bigl(I_1 + m_1 l_{c1}^2 + m_2 l_1^2 + I_2 + 2 m_2 l_1 l_{c2} \cos q_2 + m_2 l_{c2}^2\bigr)\dot{q}_1^2 + \frac{1}{2}(I_2 + m_2 l_{c2}^2)\dot{q}_2^2 + (I_2 + m_2 l_{c1}l_{c2}\cos q_2)\dot{q}_1\dot{q}_2
$$

*(2 marks for kinetic energy derivation)*

The potential energy:

$$
U = m_1 g l_{c1} \sin q_1 + m_2 g \bigl[l_1 \sin q_1 + l_{c2} \sin(q_1+q_2)\bigr]
$$

*(1 mark for potential energy)*

The Lagrangian $\mathcal{L} = T - U$. Applying the Euler–Lagrange equation $\frac{d}{dt}\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \dot{q}_i} - \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial q_i} = \tau_i$ for $i = 1, 2$ yields the **equations of motion**:

$$
\tau_1 = M_{11}\ddot{q}_1 + M_{12}\ddot{q}_2 + C_{12}\dot{q}_2^{\,2} + 2 C_{12}\dot{q}_1\dot{q}_2 + G_1(q)
$$
$$
\tau_2 = M_{21}\ddot{q}_1 + M_{22}\ddot{q}_2 + C_{21}\dot{q}_1^{\,2} + G_2(q)
$$

where the **coupling terms** are $M_{12} = M_{21}$ (inertia coupling) and the $\dot{q}_i\dot{q}_j$ terms (Coriolis/centrifugal coupling). *(4 marks for final EOM, identification of coupling terms)*

---

**(b) Disadvantages of single-axis PID on this 2-link system [7 marks]**

Assume independent single-axis PID controllers are used:

$$
\tau_1 = K_{P1}e_1 + K_{I1}\int e_1\, dt + K_{D1}\dot{e}_1, \quad \tau_2 = K_{P2}e_2 + K_{I2}\int e_2\, dt + K_{D2}\dot{e}_2
$$

Substituting into the EOM and solving for the closed-loop error dynamics (as derived in Section 3.1):

$$
\ddot{e}_1 + \frac{K_{D1}}{M_{11}}\dot{e}_1 + \frac{K_{P1}}{M_{11}} e_1 + \frac{K_{I1}}{M_{11}} \int e_1\, dt = \frac{M_{12}}{M_{11}}\ddot{q}_2 + \frac{C_{12}}{M_{11}}\bigl(\dot{q}_2^{\,2} + 2\dot{q}_1\dot{q}_2\bigr) + \frac{G_1(q)}{M_{11}}
$$

**Disadvantage 1 — Inertia coupling:** *(2 marks)*
The term $\frac{M_{12}}{M_{11}}\ddot{q}_2$ on the right side is a **disturbance** proportional to the acceleration of joint 2. The single-axis PID has no way to cancel it. Practical consequence: tracking error in joint 1 grows with the speed of joint 2.

**Disadvantage 2 — Coriolis coupling:** *(2 marks)*
The terms $\frac{C_{12}}{M_{11}}\dot{q}_2^{\,2}$ and $\frac{2 C_{12}}{M_{11}}\dot{q}_1\dot{q}_2$ are **velocity-squared** disturbances. They cannot be rejected by a linear PID; they require explicit cancellation via computed-torque control.

**Disadvantage 3 — Configuration-dependent gravity and inertia:** *(2 marks)*
The coefficient $M_{12} = I_2 + m_2 l_1 l_{c2}\cos q_2$ varies between $I_2 + m_2 l_1 l_{c2}$ (folded) and $I_2 - m_2 l_1 l_{c2}$ (extended). The PID gain $K_{P1}/M_{11}$, designed for one value of $M_{11}$, becomes either too small or too large elsewhere. Gain scheduling or adaptive control is required.

**Disadvantage 4 — No trajectory feed-forward:** *(1 mark)*
Since the reference $\ddot{q}_{d1}$ is not used directly, the controller cannot pre-compensate for the reference acceleration, leading to systematic tracking lag.

*(Total: 7 marks. The 4-disadvantage breakdown with explicit equations is the KTU-mandated answer pattern.)*

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

**Q.B. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Main Exam, Module 4]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain with suitable diagrams the **structure of single-axis PID control** of a robotic joint. Write the PID control law and describe the role of each term (P, I, D).

**(b) [7 marks]** Discuss in detail the **major disadvantages** of this scheme, namely (i) gravity and payload sensitivity, (ii) nonlinearity due to friction, and (iii) integral windup. For each, give a practical example and suggest a remedy.

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B

**(a) Structure of single-axis PID control [7 marks]**

A single-axis PID controller treats a robot joint as an **independent rotational servo** driven by a torque $\tau$ produced by an electric motor. The block diagram (see Section 4.1) consists of:

1. **Reference input $q_d$** — desired joint angle in **radians**. *(0.5 mark)*
2. **Error detector** — computes $e(t) = q_d(t) - q(t)$. *(0.5 mark)*
3. **PID controller** — three parallel branches:
   - **Proportional (P):** $\tau_P = K_P e(t)$. Provides restoring torque proportional to instantaneous error. High $K_P$ gives fast response but causes overshoot. *(1.5 marks)*
   - **Integral (I):** $\tau_I = K_I \int_0^t e(\sigma)d\sigma$. Eliminates steady-state error caused by gravity or constant disturbance. Slow but ensures zero DC error. *(1.5 marks)*
   - **Derivative (D):** $\tau_D = K_D \dot{e}(t)$. Provides predictive damping, improves transient stability, and reduces overshoot. Amplifies sensor noise. *(1.5 marks)*
4. **Actuator saturation block** — limits $|\tau| \leq \tau_{\max}$. *(0.5 mark)*
5. **Plant (the joint)** — converts torque to angular position via the joint dynamics $J\ddot{q} + b\dot{q} = \tau$. *(1 mark)*

The combined law:

$$
\tau(t) = K_P e(t) + K_I \int_0^t e(\sigma)\, d\sigma + K_D \frac{de(t)}{dt}
$$

*(Total: 7 marks)*

---

**(b) Three major disadvantages with examples and remedies [7 marks]**

**(i) Gravity and payload sensitivity [2.5 marks]**
- *Problem:* A vertical (shoulder) joint of an industrial robot must support a varying payload. The gravity torque $G(q) = m_{\text{payload}} g l_{\text{arm}} \cos(q)$ changes with both $q$ and $m_{\text{payload}}$.
- *Example:* A 6-DOF arm lifts a 5 kg object at one instant and a 20 kg object at another. The integral action must "re-learn" the new $G$ every time, producing visible sag and recovery.
- *Remedy:* Add a **feed-forward gravity-compensation term** $G_{\text{ff}}(q) = \hat{M}(q)\hat{g}$ computed from the link model.

**(ii) Nonlinearity due to friction [2.5 marks]**
- *Problem:* The friction model $F(\dot{q}) = F_c\,\text{sgn}(\dot{q}) + F_s e^{-(\dot{q}/v_s)^2} + F_v \dot{q}$ has **stiction** (static) and **Coulomb** (dry) components, which are discontinuous at $\dot{q} = 0$.
- *Example:* A SCARA arm performing a low-speed horizontal motion exhibits **stick–slip** — it stops, then jerks, instead of moving smoothly.
- *Remedy:* Use a **friction compensator** (LuGre model, neural-network observer) that subtracts the estimated friction from the PID output, or use **dither** (high-frequency small oscillations) to keep the joint out of the stiction band.

**(iii) Integral windup [2 marks]**
- *Problem:* During large transients, the PID output exceeds $\tau_{\max}$ and saturates, but the integrator keeps accumulating $e(t)$. When the system finally desaturates, a large overshoot follows.
- *Example:* A robot commanded to move rapidly from $0^{\circ}$ to $90^{\circ}$ saturates the motor at start; on reaching the setpoint, it overshoots to $95^{\circ}$ and oscillates.
- *Remedy:* Implement **anti-windup** — either *clamping* (stop integrating when output saturates) or *back-calculation* (feed the difference between saturated and unsaturated output back to the integrator with gain $K_t$).

*(Total: 7 marks)*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Single-axis PID** = one independent PID per joint, treating each as a SISO system. *(Foundation definition.)*
- [ ] The full manipulator dynamics is **$M(q)\ddot{q} + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q) + F(\dot{q}) = \tau$**. PID ignores all of $M, C, G, F$.
- [ ] **Disadvantage 1: Inter-axis coupling** — off-diagonal $M_{ij}$ injects disturbance torques; PID cannot cancel them.
- [ ] **Disadvantage 2: Configuration-varying plant** — $M_{ii}(q)$ and $G_i(q)$ change with pose; constant gains fail across the workspace.
- [ ] **Disadvantage 3: Gravity and payload variation** — vertical joints sag; integrator compensates only with delay.
- [ ] **Disadvantage 4: Nonlinear friction** — stiction + Coulomb friction cause stick–slip, dead zones, and limit cycles.
- [ ] **Disadvantage 5: Integral windup** — saturation during transients causes overshoot; remedied with anti-windup schemes.
- [ ] **Disadvantage 6: No feed-forward** — purely reactive controller; tracking bandwidth is limited; no use of $\ddot{q}_d$.
- [ ] **Disadvantage 7: Tuning difficulty** — Ziegler–Nichols and similar LTI-based methods are not strictly valid for nonlinear, time-varying plants.
- [ ] The **remedy** is **computed-torque control**, **adaptive control**, or **feedback linearization**, which use the model to cancel coupling, gravity, and Coriolis terms explicitly.
- [ ] **Industrial rule of thumb:** Use single-axis PID for *slow, decoupled, constant-payload* tasks (SCARA, gantries). Use model-based control for *fast, coupled, variable-payload* tasks (6-DOF articulated arms in welding, machining, or assembly).
- [ ] In KTU answers, **always quote the equation that reveals the disadvantage** — listing bullets without equations is the single biggest cause of lost marks.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU 2024 Takeaway:** The single-axis PID is **not wrong**; it is **incomplete**. A model-based controller that *augments* (not replaces) the PID with feed-forward dynamics is the modern industry standard.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: PD Gravity Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET722 - ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** PD Gravity Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**PD Gravity Control** (also called **PD + Gravity Compensation** or **Gravity-Compensated PD Control**) is a *model-based motion control strategy* for robot manipulators in which the controller combines a classical **Proportional-Derivative (PD) feedback law** with a **feed-forward compensation term equal to the gravitational torque vector G(q)** of the manipulator.

The control law is mathematically expressed as:

$$
\tau(t) \;=\; K_{p}\, e(t) \;+\; K_{d}\, \dot{e}(t) \;+\; G(q(t))
$$

where the control torque $\tau \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ is the joint actuator effort, $K_p$ and $K_d$ are the **proportional** and **derivative gain matrices** (both symmetric positive-definite), $e(t) = q_d(t) - q(t)$ is the *joint position error vector*, $\dot{e}(t) = \dot{q}_d(t) - \dot{q}(t)$ is the *velocity error vector*, and $G(q)$ is the gravity-induced torque vector computed from the manipulator's dynamic model at the measured configuration $q$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight (Module 4):** PD Gravity Control is a foundational topic under *Computed-Torque-Like Controllers* and is essential for understanding stability proofs based on **Lyapunov's Direct Method** and **LaSalle's Invariance Principle** in the presence of gravitational disturbances.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Self-Balancing Tray" Intuition

Imagine a waiter carrying a tray loaded with a tall, top-heavy stack of glasses. Two distinct challenges must be handled **simultaneously**:

| Real-World Action | Mathematical Counterpart | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **Lean back to offset the weight of the glasses** | **$G(q)$ term (Feed-forward)** | The waiter's muscles pre-emptively push back against gravity's tipping moment. Similarly, the controller pre-computes the gravity torque and injects it directly. |
| **Make small wrist/hip adjustments to keep the tray level** | **$K_p e + K_d \dot{e}$ (Feedback)** | The proportional term ($K_p$) corrects large errors (the tray tilting), and the derivative term ($K_d$) damps oscillations (wobble). |
| **The combined smooth motion** | **Total Control Law** | Without gravity compensation, the wrist would always be fighting the load — slow, sluggish, and unstable. Adding $G(q)$ "unburdens" the PD loop, leaving it to focus purely on tracking. |

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

* **Gains $K_p$ and $K_d$**: chosen as **symmetric positive-definite** matrices (typically diagonal for decoupled joint control). Units are **Nm/rad** and **Nm·s/rad** respectively.
* **Standard metric** for assessing PD Gravity performance: the **steady-state error** $e_{ss} = \lim_{t \to \infty} e(t)$ and the **settling time** $t_s$ at $\pm 2\%$ tolerance band.
* **Fundamental equation of robot dynamics** (Lagrangian–Euler form), on which the controller acts:

$$
M(q)\,\ddot{q} \;+\; C(q,\dot{q})\,\dot{q} \;+\; G(q) \;+\; F(\dot{q}) \;=\; \tau
$$

where $M(q)$ is the **inertia matrix** (positive definite), $C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q}$ represents **Coriolis and centripetal torques**, and $F(\dot{q})$ is **friction**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why include gravity compensation at all?** A pure PD controller (without $G(q)$) suffers from a **non-zero steady-state error** under gravity loading because gravity acts as a constant disturbance. The $G(q)$ term *cancels* this disturbance exactly (in the ideal, model-perfect case), making the system behave like a *simple second-order linear system* around the equilibrium.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic — The "Why" and "How" Behind Each Term

The PD Gravity controller works on a three-layer reasoning principle:

1. **Layer 1 — Gravity Feed-forward (Compensation Layer)**
   * **Why?** Robot joints are subject to gravitational torques that depend on configuration $q$. For a 2-DOF arm holding a payload, for example, the second joint must produce a torque proportional to $\cos(q_2)$ just to *hold position*.
   * **How?** The model is inverted to compute $G(q)$ from known link masses, lengths, and the current $q$. This term is added *open-loop* — it requires no feedback.

2. **Layer 2 — Proportional Feedback (Stiffness Layer)**
   * **Why?** Without $K_p$, no restoring force exists to pull the manipulator back to the desired trajectory.
   * **How?** The error $e = q_d - q$ is multiplied by $K_p$, producing a torque proportional to the deviation — like a virtual spring between the current and desired joints.

3. **Layer 3 — Derivative Feedback (Damping Layer)**
   * **Why?** A spring-only system (pure $K_p$) would oscillate forever. $K_d$ introduces *velocity-proportional damping*.
   * **How?** $\dot{e}$ is multiplied by $K_d$, dissipating energy and ensuring smooth, non-oscillatory convergence.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Description / Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\tau = K_p e + K_d \dot{e} + G(q)$ | **PD + Gravity Control Law** (core equation) | Nm |
| 2 | $e = q_d - q$ | Position tracking error vector | rad or m |
| 3 | $\dot{e} = \dot{q}_d - \dot{q}$ | Velocity error vector | rad/s or m/s |
| 4 | $M(q)\ddot{q} + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q) = \tau$ | **Lagrangian robot dynamics** (frictionless) | Nm |
| 5 | $M(q)\ddot{e} + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{e} + K_d \dot{e} + K_p e = M(q)\ddot{q}_d + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q}_d$ | Closed-loop **error dynamics** in tracking mode | Nm |
| 6 | $V(e,\dot{e}) = \tfrac{1}{2}\dot{e}^{T}M(q)\dot{e} + \tfrac{1}{2}e^{T}K_p e$ | **Lyapunov function candidate** (regulation case) | J (Joules) |
| 7 | $\dot{V} = -\dot{e}^{T}K_d \dot{e}$ | Lyapunov derivative (regulation case) | W (Watts) |
| 8 | $K_p = K_p^{T} \succ 0$ | Positive-definite gain condition | — |
| 9 | $K_d = K_d^{T} \succ 0$ | Positive-definite damping condition | — |
| 10 | $\dot{M}(q) - 2C(q,\dot{q}) = S$ (skew-symmetric) | **Skew-symmetry property** used in stability proof | — |
| 11 | $\sigma(M(q)) \in [\mu_1, \mu_2]$ with $0 < \mu_1 \le \mu_2$ | Boundedness of inertia matrix eigenvalues | kg·m² |
| 12 | $\Vert G(q) \Vert \le \bar{g}$ (bounded gravity norm) | Bound needed for tracking-error analysis | Nm |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall Reminder:** All gain matrices in formula sheet are **symmetric positive-definite**, written mathematically as $A \succ 0$. Do NOT confuse with *positive semi-definite* ($A \succeq 0$), which is **not** sufficient for asymptotic stability in PD Gravity Control.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

PD Gravity Control is the workhorse of **industrial robotic arms** in pick-and-place, welding, and assembly lines. Its specific engineering applications include:

* **SCARA and 6-DOF articulated arms** in semiconductor fabrication, where the controllers must counter gravity while moving at high speeds.
* **Space robotic arms** (e.g., Canadarm on the ISS), where gravity is replaced by other slowly-varying disturbances — the same controller architecture is reused.
* **Surgical robots** (e.g., Da Vinci), where smooth trajectory tracking and elimination of sag due to gravity are critical for sub-millimetre accuracy.
* **Mobile manipulators on uneven terrain**, where the *apparent* gravity vector changes with slope; here $G(q)$ is recomputed online.

> [!NOTE]
> **Connection to Computed-Torque Control:** PD Gravity Control is essentially a *partial* computed-torque controller. The full computed-torque control inverts the *entire* model $M(q)$, $C(q,\dot{q})$, and $G(q)$, whereas PD Gravity Control inverts **only $G(q)$** — making it computationally cheaper but slightly less performant in high-speed tracking.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Closed-Loop Error Dynamics (Tracking Case)

We start from the **Lagrangian manipulator dynamics** (neglecting friction $F(\dot{q})$ for analytical clarity, as is standard in KTU derivations):

$$
M(q)\,\ddot{q} \;+\; C(q,\dot{q})\,\dot{q} \;+\; G(q) \;=\; \tau
$$

**Step 1:** Substitute the **PD + Gravity control law** $\tau = K_p e + K_d \dot{e} + G(q)$ into the right-hand side of the dynamics equation.

$$
M(q)\,\ddot{q} \;+\; C(q,\dot{q})\,\dot{q} \;+\; G(q) \;=\; K_p\, e \;+\; K_d\, \dot{e} \;+\; G(q)
$$

**Step 2:** The $G(q)$ terms appear on both sides and cancel identically (this is the *whole point* of gravity compensation).

$$
M(q)\,\ddot{q} \;+\; C(q,\dot{q})\,\dot{q} \;=\; K_p\, e \;+\; K_d\, \dot{e}
$$

**Step 3:** Introduce the error variable $e = q_d - q$. Differentiate twice with respect to time:

$$
q = q_d - e \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \dot{q} = \dot{q}_d - \dot{e} \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \ddot{q} = \ddot{q}_d - \ddot{e}
$$

**Step 4:** Substitute $\ddot{q} = \ddot{q}_d - \ddot{e}$ and $\dot{q} = \dot{q}_d - \dot{e}$ into the gravity-cancelled dynamics.

$$
M(q)(\ddot{q}_d - \ddot{e}) \;+\; C(q,\dot{q})(\dot{q}_d - \dot{e}) \;=\; K_p\, e \;+\; K_d\, \dot{e}
$$

**Step 5:** Expand and bring all error-related terms to the left-hand side.

$$
M(q)\ddot{q}_d - M(q)\ddot{e} \;+\; C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q}_d - C(q,\dot{q})\dot{e} \;=\; K_p\, e \;+\; K_d\, \dot{e}
$$

**Step 6:** Move the terms $M(q)\ddot{q}_d + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q}_d$ to the right-hand side (they represent the *reference* dynamics that act as a forcing term in the error equation).

$$
- M(q)\ddot{e} \;-\; C(q,\dot{q})\dot{e} \;-\; K_d\, \dot{e} \;-\; K_p\, e \;=\; - M(q)\ddot{q}_d \;-\; C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q}_d
$$

**Step 7:** Multiply both sides by $-1$ to obtain the canonical error-dynamics form.

$$
M(q)\ddot{e} \;+\; C(q,\dot{q})\dot{e} \;+\; K_d\, \dot{e} \;+\; K_p\, e \;=\; M(q)\ddot{q}_d \;+\; C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q}_d
$$

This is the **standard closed-loop error dynamics equation** for PD Gravity Control in the tracking case. For the **regulation case** (i.e., $q_d(t) = q_d^{c}$ is a constant setpoint), $\dot{q}_d \equiv 0$ and $\ddot{q}_d \equiv 0$, simplifying to:

$$
M(q)\ddot{e} \;+\; C(q,\dot{q})\dot{e} \;+\; K_d\, \dot{e} \;+\; K_p\, e \;=\; 0
$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Lyapunov Stability Proof (Regulation Case)

We prove that the origin $(e, \dot{e}) = (0, 0)$ is **asymptotically stable** using **Lyapunov's Direct Method**.

**Step 1:** Choose a Lyapunov function candidate $V : \mathbb{R}^{2n} \to \mathbb{R}_{\ge 0}$.

$$
V(e, \dot{e}) \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\,\dot{e}^{T} M(q)\, \dot{e} \;+\; \tfrac{1}{2}\, e^{T} K_p\, e
$$

**Step 2:** Verify that $V$ is *positive definite* (this is the *Lyapunov candidate validity* check).

* The kinetic-like term $\tfrac{1}{2}\dot{e}^T M(q) \dot{e} \ge 0$ because $M(q) \succ 0$ (a fundamental property of the inertia matrix of any rigid-link manipulator).
* The potential-like term $\tfrac{1}{2} e^T K_p e \ge 0$ because $K_p \succ 0$ (designer choice).
* $V = 0 \iff e = 0$ and $\dot{e} = 0$. Therefore $V$ is **positive definite**.

**Step 3:** Compute the time derivative of $V$ along the trajectories of the system.

$$
\dot{V} \;=\; \dot{e}^{T} M(q)\, \ddot{e} \;+\; \tfrac{1}{2}\,\dot{e}^{T} \dot{M}(q)\, \dot{e} \;+\; e^{T} K_p\, \dot{e}
$$

**Step 4:** Apply the **skew-symmetry property** of robot manipulators: $\dot{M}(q) = C(q,\dot{q}) + C^T(q,\dot{q})$. This implies that the matrix $[\dot{M}(q) - 2C(q,\dot{q})]$ is skew-symmetric, so for any vector $x$, we have $x^T [\dot{M}(q) - 2C(q,\dot{q})] x = 0$. Hence:

$$
\dot{e}^{T}\,[\dot{M}(q) - 2C(q,\dot{q})]\, \dot{e} \;=\; 0 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \dot{e}^{T} \dot{M}(q)\, \dot{e} \;=\; 2\,\dot{e}^{T} C(q,\dot{q})\, \dot{e}
$$

Substituting this into $\dot{V}$:

$$
\dot{V} \;=\; \dot{e}^{T} M(q)\, \ddot{e} \;+\; \dot{e}^{T} C(q,\dot{q})\, \dot{e} \;+\; e^{T} K_p\, \dot{e}
$$

**Step 5:** Factor out $\dot{e}^T$ from the first two terms.

$$
\dot{V} \;=\; \dot{e}^{T} \bigl[\,M(q)\, \ddot{e} \;+\; C(q,\dot{q})\, \dot{e}\,\bigr] \;+\; e^{T} K_p\, \dot{e}
$$

**Step 6:** Use the closed-loop error dynamics (regulation case): $M(q)\ddot{e} + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{e} = -K_d \dot{e} - K_p e$.

$$
\dot{V} \;=\; \dot{e}^{T}\, \bigl[\, -K_d\, \dot{e} \;-\; K_p\, e\,\bigr] \;+\; e^{T} K_p\, \dot{e}
$$

**Step 7:** Expand the products.

$$
\dot{V} \;=\; -\dot{e}^{T} K_d\, \dot{e} \;-\; \dot{e}^{T} K_p\, e \;+\; e^{T} K_p\, \dot{e}
$$

**Step 8:** Use the symmetry of $K_p$ (i.e., $K_p^T = K_p$), so $\dot{e}^T K_p e = e^T K_p^T \dot{e} = e^T K_p \dot{e}$. The last two terms therefore cancel.

$$
\dot{V} \;=\; -\dot{e}^{T} K_d\, \dot{e}
$$

**Step 9:** Verify that $\dot{V}$ is *negative semi-definite* (Lyapunov stability, not yet asymptotic). Since $K_d \succ 0$ and $\dot{e}^T K_d \dot{e} \ge 0$, we have $\dot{V} \le 0$. Furthermore, $\dot{V} = 0 \iff \dot{e} = 0$.

**Step 10 (Asymptotic Stability via LaSalle):** Apply **LaSalle's Invariance Principle**. The largest invariant set within $\{\dot{e} = 0\}$ is determined as follows. If $\dot{e} \equiv 0$, then $\ddot{e} \equiv 0$ as well. Substituting into the error dynamics:

$$
M(q)\cdot 0 \;+\; C(q,\dot{q})\cdot 0 \;+\; K_d\cdot 0 \;+\; K_p\, e \;=\; 0 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; K_p\, e \;=\; 0
$$

Since $K_p \succ 0$, this implies $e = 0$. Therefore, the **only invariant set** is $\{(e,\dot{e}) = (0,0)\}$, proving **asymptotic stability** of the equilibrium.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Tracking Case Note:** In the tracking case, the right-hand side $M(q)\ddot{q}_d + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q}_d$ is generally non-zero, so the error dynamics become *non-autonomous*. The Lyapunov derivative becomes $\dot{V} = -\dot{e}^T K_d \dot{e} + \dot{e}^T [M(q)\ddot{q}_d + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q}_d]$, and we can only prove **practical stability** (bounded tracking error) — not asymptotic tracking — for pure PD + Gravity in the tracking case.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Steady-State Error in Pure PD (Why Gravity Compensation is Needed)

To see why a *plain* PD controller (without $G(q)$) is insufficient, consider a 1-DOF arm holding a mass $m$ at distance $\ell$. The gravity torque is $G = m g \ell \cos(q)$. The plain PD law gives $\tau = K_p e + K_d \dot{e}$. At steady state ($\dot{e} = 0$, $\ddot{e} = 0$), the dynamics reduce to:

$$
G(q_{ss}) \;=\; K_p\, e_{ss} \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; e_{ss} \;=\; \dfrac{m g \ell \cos(q_{ss})}{K_p} \;\ne\; 0
$$

With PD + Gravity compensation, the same steady state yields $G(q_{ss})$ cancelled on both sides, leaving $0 = K_p e_{ss}$, so $e_{ss} = 0$ — **zero steady-state error**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Control Loop Block Architecture (PD + Gravity)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    refQ["qd reference trajectory"]
    sumNode["Summing Junction"]
    kpBlock["Kp gain matrix"]
    kdBlock["Kd gain matrix"]
    gBlock["G(q) gravity model"]
    plant["Robot Plant M,C,G"]
    outQ["q measured joints"]
    errPath["Error computation"]
    sumTwo["Torque summation"]

    refQ --> errPath
    outQ --> errPath
    errPath -- "e = qd - q" --> sumNode
    sumNode -- "e" --> kpBlock
    kpBlock --> sumTwo
    gBlock --> sumTwo
    outQ --> gBlock
    errPath -- "e dot" --> kdBlock
    kdBlock --> sumTwo
    sumTwo -- "tau" --> plant
    plant --> outQ
```

*Caption:* Closed-loop architecture of PD + Gravity Control. The reference $q_d$ is compared to the measured $q$ to produce error $e$, fed through $K_p$ and $K_d$ blocks. The gravity vector $G(q)$ is computed in parallel from the measured configuration and summed with the PD output to form the joint torque $\tau$, which drives the robot.

---

### 4.2 Error Dynamics and Stability Flow (Lyapunov Pipeline)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    eDef["Define e = qd - q and e dot"]
    dynEq["Closed-loop error dynamics M qddot + C qdot + Kd qdot + Kp e = M qddot_d + C qdot_d"]
    regCase["Assume regulation: qd = constant, qd dot = 0, qddot_d = 0"]
    lyapCand["Choose Lyapunov function V = 1/2 e dot Transpose M e dot + 1/2 e Transpose Kp e"]
    posDef["Verify V is positive definite since M and Kp are positive definite"]
    lyapDer["Differentiate V dot along trajectories"]
    skew["Apply skew-symmetry property dot M minus 2C is skew"]
    cancel["Kp symmetric cancellation yields V dot = minus e dot Transpose Kd e dot"]
    negSemi["V dot is negative semi-definite proves Lyapunov stability"]
    lasalle["Apply LaSalle invariance principle on set e dot = 0"]
    asymp["Result Kp e = 0 gives e = 0 asymptotic stability proven"]
    track["Tracking case yields practical stability only bounded error"]

    eDef --> dynEq
    dynEq --> regCase
    regCase --> lyapCand
    lyapCand --> posDef
    posDef --> lyapDer
    lyapDer --> skew
    skew --> cancel
    cancel --> negSemi
    negSemi --> lasalle
    lasalle --> asymp
    asymp --> track
```

*Caption:* The full Lyapunov stability proof pipeline. Each node represents a logical step in the analysis, culminating in asymptotic stability for the regulation case and practical stability for the tracking case.

---

### 4.3 Comparison Block: Plain PD vs PD + Gravity

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    plainBlock["Plain PD Control tau = Kp e + Kd e dot"]
    plainProp["Properties: Steady state error nonzero, Cheap, No model needed"]
    pdgravBlock["PD + Gravity Control tau = Kp e + Kd e dot + G q"]
    pdgravProp["Properties: Zero steady state error, Needs model, Better tracking"]

    plainBlock --> plainProp
    pdgravBlock --> pdgravProp
```

*Caption:* Side-by-side property comparison of plain PD control versus PD + Gravity compensation. The latter is strictly superior in the presence of gravity loading, at the cost of requiring accurate knowledge of the gravity model $G(q)$.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** *[KTU University Exam — May 2024]* Define **PD Gravity Control** for a robot manipulator. State the control law and explain the role of each term in eliminating steady-state error due to gravity.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
PD Gravity Control is a model-based motion control strategy where a Proportional-Derivative (PD) feedback law is augmented with a feed-forward compensation term equal to the gravitational torque vector $G(q)$ of the manipulator. The control law is:

$$
\tau \;=\; K_p\, e \;+\; K_d\, \dot{e} \;+\; G(q)
$$

* **$K_p e$ term (1 mark):** Acts as a virtual spring that generates a restoring torque proportional to position error $e = q_d - q$, pulling the manipulator back to the desired configuration.
* **$K_d \dot{e}$ term (1 mark):** Provides velocity-proportional damping, dissipating energy and preventing oscillations.
* **$G(q)$ term (1 mark):** Cancels the gravity-induced torque at the current configuration. At steady state ($\dot{e}=0$, $\ddot{e}=0$), the gravity terms on both sides of the dynamics cancel, leaving $K_p e = 0$, which forces $e = 0$ and hence **zero steady-state error**.

---

**Q2.** *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]* Why is the **skew-symmetry property** of robot manipulators essential in the Lyapunov stability proof of PD Gravity Control?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The skew-symmetry property states that the matrix $[\dot{M}(q) - 2C(q,\dot{q})]$ is skew-symmetric, meaning for any vector $x$:

$$
x^{T}\,[\dot{M}(q) - 2C(q,\dot{q})]\, x \;=\; 0
$$

* **Role 1 (1 mark):** During the differentiation of the Lyapunov function $V = \tfrac{1}{2}\dot{e}^T M(q) \dot{e} + \tfrac{1}{2} e^T K_p e$, a term $\tfrac{1}{2}\dot{e}^T \dot{M}(q) \dot{e}$ appears.
* **Role 2 (1 mark):** The skew-symmetry property converts this term into $\dot{e}^T C(q,\dot{q}) \dot{e}$, which then combines with the $\dot{e}^T M(q) \ddot{e}$ term to form $\dot{e}^T [M(q) \ddot{e} + C(q,\dot{q}) \dot{e}]$, allowing direct substitution of the closed-loop error dynamics.
* **Role 3 (1 mark):** Without this property, the resulting $\dot{V}$ would contain an un-cancellable cross-term and we could not conclude $\dot{V} = -\dot{e}^T K_d \dot{e} \le 0$, which is the crux of the stability proof.

---

### Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks, typically split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Students must answer ONE full question (both sub-parts).

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — May 2024]*

**(a)** Derive the **closed-loop error dynamics equation** for an $n$-DOF robot manipulator under PD + Gravity Control in the tracking case. Clearly state all assumptions. (7 marks)

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

We begin with the Lagrangian manipulator dynamics (neglecting friction, as is conventional in KTU derivations):

$$
M(q)\,\ddot{q} \;+\; C(q,\dot{q})\,\dot{q} \;+\; G(q) \;=\; \tau \quad \text{...(1)}
$$

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Substitute the PD + Gravity control law $\tau = K_p e + K_d \dot{e} + G(q)$ into equation (1).

$$
M(q)\ddot{q} + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q) = K_p e + K_d \dot{e} + G(q)
$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** The $G(q)$ terms cancel from both sides, giving:

$$
M(q)\ddot{q} + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} = K_p e + K_d \dot{e} \quad \text{...(2)}
$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Define the error $e = q_d - q$, with derivatives $\dot{e} = \dot{q}_d - \dot{q}$ and $\ddot{e} = \ddot{q}_d - \ddot{q}$. Substituting $q = q_d - e$ into (2):

$$
M(q)(\ddot{q}_d - \ddot{e}) + C(q,\dot{q})(\dot{q}_d - \dot{e}) = K_p e + K_d \dot{e}
$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Expanding and collecting error terms on the left:

$$
M(q)\ddot{q}_d - M(q)\ddot{e} + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q}_d - C(q,\dot{q})\dot{e} = K_p e + K_d \dot{e}
$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Move the reference dynamics to the right:

$$
-M(q)\ddot{e} - C(q,\dot{q})\dot{e} - K_d \dot{e} - K_p e = -M(q)\ddot{q}_d - C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q}_d
$$

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Multiply by $-1$:

$$
M(q)\ddot{e} + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{e} + K_d \dot{e} + K_p e = M(q)\ddot{q}_d + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q}_d
$$

**Step 7 (1 mark):** State the result clearly:
* For **regulation** ($q_d = \text{const.}$): right-hand side becomes zero, yielding the autonomous error equation.
* For **tracking** (general $q_d(t)$): the right-hand side acts as a bounded forcing term (provided $\dot{q}_d$, $\ddot{q}_d$ are bounded), leading to bounded tracking error under suitable gain conditions.

**(b)** Using **Lyapunov's Direct Method**, prove that the equilibrium $(e, \dot{e}) = (0, 0)$ of the regulation case is **asymptotically stable**. State clearly any property used. (7 marks)

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Consider the regulation case $\dot{q}_d = 0$, $\ddot{q}_d = 0$, so the error dynamics simplify to:

$$
M(q)\ddot{e} + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{e} + K_d \dot{e} + K_p e = 0
$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Choose the Lyapunov function candidate:

$$
V(e,\dot{e}) = \tfrac{1}{2}\dot{e}^{T}M(q)\dot{e} + \tfrac{1}{2}e^{T}K_p e
$$

Since $M(q) \succ 0$ and $K_p \succ 0$, the function $V$ is **positive definite** and radially unbounded, with $V(0,0) = 0$.

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Differentiate $V$ along system trajectories:

$$
\dot{V} = \dot{e}^{T}M(q)\ddot{e} + \tfrac{1}{2}\dot{e}^{T}\dot{M}(q)\dot{e} + e^{T}K_p\dot{e}
$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Use the **skew-symmetry property** of robot manipulators, $\dot{e}^{T}[\dot{M}(q) - 2C(q,\dot{q})]\dot{e} = 0$, so $\dot{e}^{T}\dot{M}(q)\dot{e} = 2\dot{e}^{T}C(q,\dot{q})\dot{e}$. Substituting:

$$
\dot{V} = \dot{e}^{T}M(q)\ddot{e} + \dot{e}^{T}C(q,\dot{q})\dot{e} + e^{T}K_p\dot{e}
$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Factor and substitute the error dynamics $M(q)\ddot{e} + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{e} = -K_d \dot{e} - K_p e$:

$$
\dot{V} = \dot{e}^{T}[-K_d\dot{e} - K_p e] + e^{T}K_p\dot{e} = -\dot{e}^{T}K_d\dot{e} - \dot{e}^{T}K_p e + e^{T}K_p\dot{e}
$$

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Since $K_p$ is symmetric, $\dot{e}^T K_p e = e^T K_p \dot{e}$, so the last two terms cancel, yielding:

$$
\dot{V} = -\dot{e}^{T}K_d\dot{e} \le 0
$$

This is **negative semi-definite**, proving Lyapunov stability.

**Step 7 (1 mark):** Apply **LaSalle's Invariance Principle**. The set $\{(e,\dot{e}) : \dot{V} = 0\} = \{(e,\dot{e}) : \dot{e} = 0\}$. Within this set, if $\dot{e} \equiv 0$, then $\ddot{e} \equiv 0$, and the error dynamics reduce to $K_p e = 0$. Since $K_p \succ 0$, we have $e = 0$. Hence, the origin is the **only invariant point**, and asymptotic stability follows.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]*

**(a)** For a **1-DOF rotary manipulator** with link mass $m$ and length $\ell$ rotating in the vertical plane, derive the **gravity torque** $G(q)$ and show that a **plain PD controller** (without gravity compensation) produces a **non-zero steady-state error** for a constant setpoint. (7 marks)

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Compute the potential energy of the link due to gravity. Taking the pivot as origin and $q$ measured from horizontal, the centre of mass is at $(\tfrac{\ell}{2}\cos q, \tfrac{\ell}{2}\sin q)$. The potential energy is:

$$
P(q) = m g \cdot \tfrac{\ell}{2} \sin q = \tfrac{1}{2} m g \ell \sin q
$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** The gravity torque is the partial derivative:

$$
G(q) = \frac{\partial P}{\partial q} = \tfrac{1}{2} m g \ell \cos q
$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** The full dynamics for the 1-DOF arm (assuming negligible friction) is:

$$
I\,\ddot{q} + G(q) = \tau, \quad I = \tfrac{1}{3} m \ell^{2}
$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Apply a plain PD controller (no $G(q)$ term): $\tau = K_p e + K_d \dot{e}$ where $e = q_d - q$.

$$
I\ddot{q} + \tfrac{1}{2}m g \ell \cos q = K_p(q_d - q) + K_d(\dot{q}_d - \dot{q})
$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** For a *constant* setpoint $q_d$ (so $\dot{q}_d = \ddot{q}_d = 0$), at steady state $\dot{q} = 0$ and $\ddot{q} = 0$:

$$
\tfrac{1}{2}m g \ell \cos q_{ss} = K_p(q_d - q_{ss}) = K_p\, e_{ss}
$$

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Solve for the steady-state error:

$$
e_{ss} = q_d - q_{ss} = \frac{m g \ell \cos q_{ss}}{2 K_p} \;\ne\; 0
$$

**Step 7 (1 mark):** Interpretation: The steady-state error is **non-zero** and inversely proportional to $K_p$. Increasing $K_p$ reduces the error but causes higher oscillations and risks actuator saturation. Hence, plain PD is **inadequate** under gravity loading — motivating the addition of the $G(q)$ compensation term.

**(b)** Now show that the **PD + Gravity controller** $\tau = K_p e + K_d \dot{e} + G(q)$ achieves **zero steady-state error** for the same system. Briefly comment on practical limitations. (7 marks)

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** With $\tau = K_p e + K_d \dot{e} + G(q)$, the closed-loop dynamics become:

$$
I\ddot{q} + \tfrac{1}{2}m g \ell \cos q = K_p(q_d - q) + K_d(\dot{q}_d - \dot{q}) + \tfrac{1}{2}m g \ell \cos q
$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** The gravity terms cancel exactly:

$$
I\ddot{q} = K_p(q_d - q) + K_d(\dot{q}_d - \dot{q})
$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** At steady state ($\dot{q} = 0$, $\ddot{q} = 0$, $\dot{q}_d = 0$):

$$
0 = K_p(q_d - q_{ss}) = K_p\, e_{ss}
$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Since $K_p \succ 0$, it follows that:

$$
e_{ss} = 0 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; q_{ss} = q_d
$$

**Zero steady-state error is achieved** — this is the central advantage of PD + Gravity compensation.

**Step 5 (1 mark):** **Practical Limitation 1 — Model Uncertainty:** The compensation term $G(q)$ relies on exact knowledge of $m$, $\ell$, and $g$. If the actual mass differs (e.g., the robot picks up an unknown payload), the cancellation is imperfect and a residual steady-state error reappears.

**Step 6 (1 mark):** **Practical Limitation 2 — Computational Cost:** Computing $G(q)$ at every control cycle requires evaluating trigonometric functions for every joint, which can be expensive in high-DOF manipulators (e.g., 7-DOF Franka Panda).

**Step 7 (1 mark):** **Practical Limitation 3 — Asymptotic Tracking:** For *time-varying* trajectories ($q_d(t)$ with $\dot{q}_d \ne 0$), zero error is not guaranteed; only **bounded tracking error** is ensured — a fundamental limitation of PD + Gravity (full computed-torque control is needed for asymptotic tracking).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the $\boldsymbol{G(q)}$ term in the steady-state-error derivation** — many students drop $G(q)$ from the dynamics and obtain $e_{ss} = 0$ trivially. This loses **2 marks** immediately. Always include $G(q)$ and then *cancel* it explicitly to show the contrast.
> 2. **Confusing positive-definite ($\succ 0$) with positive-semi-definite ($\succeq 0$)** for $K_p$ and $K_d$. Semi-definite matrices yield *only stability*, not *asymptotic stability*. The KTU marking scheme deducts **1 mark** for each incorrect definiteness statement.
> 3. **Skipping the skew-symmetry step** in the Lyapunov proof — examiners specifically look for the line $\dot{e}^T[\dot{M} - 2C]\dot{e} = 0$. Omitting it loses **2 marks**.
> 4. **Not stating LaSalle's Invariance Principle** when claiming asymptotic stability — $\dot{V}$ is only negative *semi-definite*, so LaSalle must be explicitly invoked. Missing this loses **1 mark**.
> 5. **Using $\boldsymbol{q}$ instead of $\boldsymbol{e}$ in the Lyapunov function** — the standard form $V = \tfrac{1}{2}\dot{e}^T M(q) \dot{e} + \tfrac{1}{2}e^T K_p e$ uses the **error** coordinates, not the joint coordinates. Conflating them is a frequent error.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

* **Control law:** $\tau = K_p e + K_d \dot{e} + G(q)$ — PD gains with feed-forward gravity compensation.
* **Error definition:** $e = q_d - q$ and $\dot{e} = \dot{q}_d - \dot{q}$.
* **Closed-loop error dynamics (tracking):** $M(q)\ddot{e} + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{e} + K_d \dot{e} + K_p e = M(q)\ddot{q}_d + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q}_d$.
* **Regulation case:** Right-hand side becomes zero, yielding an autonomous error equation.
* **Lyapunov candidate:** $V = \tfrac{1}{2}\dot{e}^T M(q) \dot{e} + \tfrac{1}{2}e^T K_p e$ — positive definite.
* **Lyapunov derivative:** $\dot{V} = -\dot{e}^T K_d \dot{e} \le 0$ — negative semi-definite.
* **Key property used:** Skew-symmetry $\dot{M} - 2C$ being skew-symmetric, allowing $\dot{M}$-term to collapse into $2C$.
* **Asymptotic stability** is proved via **LaSalle's Invariance Principle** (mandatory since $\dot{V}$ is only semi-definite).
* **Zero steady-state error** under constant setpoint is the *central practical benefit* of including $G(q)$.
* **Tracking limitation:** Pure PD + Gravity achieves only **bounded** (not zero) tracking error for time-varying references; full **computed-torque control** is required for asymptotic tracking.
* **Practical limitations:** Requires accurate model of $G(q)$; sensitive to payload changes; computationally non-trivial for high-DOF arms.
* **Gain-tuning rule of thumb:** Choose $K_p$ diagonal-dominant, then increase $K_d$ until oscillations dampen within 2–3 cycles. $K_d$ should be roughly $\sqrt{K_p}$ times the link inertia.
* **Relation to other controllers:** PD + Gravity is a *subset* of computed-torque control (only $G$ is inverted, not $M$ or $C$). It is *more powerful* than plain PD but *less powerful* than full computed-torque.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Computed Torque Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEMET722 - ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Computed Torque Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Computed Torque Control (CTC)**, also known as **Inverse Dynamics Control** or **Feedback Linearization Control**, is a model-based, nonlinear control strategy for robotic manipulators in which the controller explicitly uses the **complete nonlinear dynamic model** of the robot to compute the joint actuator torques required to follow a desired trajectory.

The control law *inverts* the manipulator dynamics so that the resulting closed-loop behavior becomes a **set of decoupled, linear second-order systems** — one for each joint — that can be regulated by simple PD (or PID) gains.

Mathematically, the computed torque law is expressed as:

$$\tau(t) = M(q)\left[\ddot{q}_d + K_v \dot{e} + K_p e\right] + h(q,\dot{q})$$

where:
- $q, \dot{q}, \ddot{q} \in \mathbb{R}^n$ are the actual joint position, velocity, and acceleration vectors.
- $q_d, \dot{q}_d, \ddot{q}_d$ are the desired (reference) joint trajectories.
- $e = q_d - q$ is the position tracking error.
- $M(q) \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$ is the symmetric, positive-definite **inertia matrix**.
- $h(q, \dot{q}) = C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q) + F(\dot{q})$ lumps the Coriolis/centripetal, gravity, and friction terms.
- $K_p, K_v \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$ are diagonal positive-definite gain matrices.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — Driving a car up a steep, curvy hill with cruise control:**
> Imagine you set a desired speed (trajectory) for your car, but the hill has changing slopes, curves, and wind. A *naïve PD controller* would just push the accelerator harder if the car slows down. A *Computed Torque Controller*, however, behaves like an expert driver who **knows the exact mass of the car, the slope of the hill, the curvature, and the wind speed**. Before pressing the pedal, this driver *pre-calculates* how much gravitational pull, centrifugal force, and drag must be cancelled — and then applies just the right extra force to track the speed profile perfectly.

In a robot:
- The **"hill"** is the nonlinear coupling between joints (Coriolis + gravity).
- The **"expert driver"** is the inverse-dynamics solver running in real time.
- The **"pedal pressure"** is the torque vector $\tau$ sent to the motors.

The result: complex nonlinear dynamics are *exactly cancelled*, leaving behind an easy-to-tune linear servo loop at every joint.

### 1.3 Why CTC Matters in KTU Robotics

- It is the **canonical example of model-based control** in Module 4.
- It bridges Module 3 (Lagrangian dynamics) and Module 5 (advanced adaptive/robust control).
- It is the **theoretical foundation** of feed-forward plus feedback control used in industrial robots (ABB, KUKA, FANUC all use variants of it).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Robot Dynamic Model — Starting Point

Every serial-chain rigid manipulator obeys the **Euler–Lagrange equation** of motion (covered in Module 3):

$$M(q)\ddot{q} + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q) + F(\dot{q}) = \tau$$

| Symbol | Meaning | Property |
|---|---|---|
| $M(q)$ | Inertia / mass matrix | Symmetric, positive-definite, $M(q) = M(q)^T \succ 0$ |
| $C(q,\dot{q})$ | Coriolis & centripetal matrix | $\dot{M} - 2C$ is skew-symmetric (Passivity property) |
| $G(q)$ | Gravitational torque vector | Conservative, $\partial G/\partial q$ is the stiffness matrix |
| $F(\dot{q})$ | Friction vector (viscous + Coulomb) | Often modelled as $F_v \dot{q} + F_c \operatorname{sgn}(\dot{q})$ |
| $\tau$ | Generalized joint torque vector | Control input produced by actuators |

### 2.2 Core Idea of CTC — Three Logical Steps

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **STEP 1 — Define a servo error dynamics.**
> Pick a desired linear second-order behaviour for the tracking error $e = q_d - q$:
> $$\ddot{e} + K_v \dot{e} + K_p e = 0 \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad \ddot{q} = \ddot{q}_d + K_v \dot{e} + K_p e$$
> This is a *reference* acceleration we want the robot to have.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **STEP 2 — Invert the dynamics.**
> Solve the manipulator equation for the torque required to produce exactly that reference acceleration. Because $M(q)$ is invertible (positive-definite ⇒ non-singular), the inverse exists and is well-conditioned.
> $$\tau^{\text{ref}} = M(q)\bigl[\ddot{q}_d + K_v \dot{e} + K_p e\bigr] + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q) + F(\dot{q})$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **STEP 3 — Substitute and observe cancellation.**
> Plug $\tau^{\text{ref}}$ into the original dynamics. The nonlinear terms $C\dot{q}$, $G$, $F$ **cancel exactly**, and $M(q)$ on both sides **divides out**, leaving the desired linear error dynamics.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Exam Cheat Sheet)

| # | Equation | Meaning / Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\tau = M(q)a + h(q,\dot{q})$ | Compact manipulator model |
| 2 | $a = \ddot{q}_d + K_v \dot{e} + K_p e$ | Auxiliary reference acceleration |
| 3 | $\tau = M(q)[\ddot{q}_d + K_v \dot{e} + K_p e] + h(q,\dot{q})$ | **Computed Torque Law (full)** |
| 4 | $K_p, K_v \succ 0$ diagonal | Gain selection (use pole-placement per joint) |
| 5 | $\ddot{e} + K_v \dot{e} + K_p e = 0$ | Closed-loop linear error dynamics |
| 6 | $s^2 + K_{v,i} s + K_{p,i} = 0$ | Per-joint characteristic polynomial |
| 7 | $K_{p,i} = \omega_n^2,\ K_{v,i} = 2\zeta\omega_n$ | Choose natural frequency $\omega_n$ and damping $\zeta \in (0,1)$ |
| 8 | $V = \tfrac{1}{2}\dot{e}^T M \dot{e} + \tfrac{1}{2}e^T K_p e$ | Lyapunov candidate (stability proof) |
| 9 | $\dot{V} = -\dot{e}^T K_v \dot{e} \le 0$ | Negative semi-definite ⇒ stable |
| 10 | $\ddot{V} = -2\dot{e}^T K_v \ddot{e} \neq 0$ | Barbalat ⇒ $\dot{e} \to 0$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Anti-Pipe Note:** All absolute-value / norm symbols below are written as `\vert` (e.g. $\vert \dot{e} \vert$) so the markdown table does not break.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Industrial robotics:** Every high-speed pick-and-place robot (delta, SCARA) uses inverse-dynamics feed-forward — a discrete-time cousin of CTC.
- **Space manipulators:** Used in free-flying robots (Canadarm, Dextre) where gravity-free, model-accurate control is mandatory.
- **Humanoid robotics:** Locomotion controllers for Atlas, ASIMO use **task-space CTC** with floating-base dynamics.
- **CNC machine tools:** The same idea controls drive torques to follow complex 3-axis tool paths.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** A *full* derivation of CTC earns the complete 7 marks. Skipping the "substitution & cancellation" step costs 2 marks. Always end the derivation by writing the final error-dynamics equation.

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Full Closed-Loop Error Dynamics

**Goal:** Show that applying the computed torque law makes the closed-loop tracking error follow $\ddot{e} + K_v \dot{e} + K_p e = 0$.

**Step 1 — Write the actual robot dynamics.**

$$M(q)\ddot{q} + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q) + F(\dot{q}) = \tau$$

**Step 2 — Define the tracking error and its derivatives.**

$$e = q_d - q \quad\Rightarrow\quad \dot{e} = \dot{q}_d - \dot{q} \quad\Rightarrow\quad \ddot{e} = \ddot{q}_d - \ddot{q}$$

**Step 3 — Define the auxiliary reference acceleration (the inner-loop command).**

$$a = \ddot{q}_d + K_v \dot{e} + K_p e$$

**Step 4 — Write the computed torque control law.**

$$\tau = M(q)\,a + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q) + F(\dot{q})$$

**Step 5 — Substitute the control law into the actual robot dynamics.**

$$M(q)\ddot{q} + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q) + F(\dot{q}) = M(q)\,a + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q) + F(\dot{q})$$

**Step 6 — Cancel identical nonlinear terms on both sides.**

$$M(q)\ddot{q} = M(q)\,a$$

**Step 7 — Left-multiply by $M(q)^{-1}$ (justified because $M(q) \succ 0$).**

$$\ddot{q} = a = \ddot{q}_d + K_v \dot{e} + K_p e$$

**Step 8 — Rearrange to expose the error dynamics.**

$$\ddot{q}_d - \ddot{q} + K_v \dot{e} + K_p e = 0$$

$$\ddot{e} + K_v \dot{e} + K_p e = 0$$

**Step 9 — Per-joint scalar form (since $K_p, K_v$ are chosen diagonal).**

$$\ddot{e}_i + K_{v,i}\,\dot{e}_i + K_{p,i}\,e_i = 0,\quad i = 1,\ldots,n$$

**Conclusion:** The closed-loop system is **decoupled** (no cross-coupling between joints) and **linear**. The dynamics of each joint reduces to a stable mass-spring-damper if $K_{p,i} > 0$ and $K_{v,i} > 0$.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Lyapunov Stability Proof (Examiner Favourite)

**Step 1 — Propose a scalar energy-like Lyapunov function.**

$$V(e,\dot{e}) = \frac{1}{2}\,\dot{e}^T M(q)\,\dot{e} + \frac{1}{2}\,e^T K_p\,e \ge 0$$

$V$ is positive-definite because $M(q) \succ 0$ and $K_p \succ 0$.

**Step 2 — Differentiate with respect to time.**

$$\dot{V} = \dot{e}^T M(q)\ddot{e} + \frac{1}{2}\dot{e}^T \dot{M}(q)\dot{e} + e^T K_p \dot{e}$$

**Step 3 — Use the error dynamics to substitute $\ddot{e} = -K_v \dot{e} - K_p e$.**

$$\dot{V} = \dot{e}^T M(q)\bigl[-K_v \dot{e} - K_p e\bigr] + \frac{1}{2}\dot{e}^T \dot{M}(q)\dot{e} + e^T K_p \dot{e}$$

**Step 4 — The $\dot{e}^T M K_p e$ and $e^T K_p \dot{e}$ terms cancel (they are scalar transposes of each other).**

$$\dot{V} = -\dot{e}^T M(q) K_v \dot{e} + \frac{1}{2}\dot{e}^T \dot{M}(q)\dot{e}$$

**Step 5 — Use the skew-symmetry property $\dot{M}(q) - 2C(q,\dot{q}) = $ skew-symmetric, and the fact that in the closed-loop $M\ddot{e} = M(-K_v \dot{e} - K_p e)$ contains the residual $C\dot{e}$ via the original dynamics, so the cross-term vanishes and we get:**

$$\dot{V} = -\dot{e}^T K_v \dot{e} \le 0$$

since $K_v \succ 0$.

**Step 6 — Apply Barbalat's Lemma.**

$\dot{V} \le 0$ and $V$ is bounded below (by 0), so $V(t)$ converges. Hence $\dot{e}(t) \to 0$ as $t \to \infty$. Combined with the error dynamics, $e(t) \to 0$ as well.

**Conclusion:** The equilibrium $e = 0,\ \dot{e} = 0$ is **uniformly asymptotically stable**.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Gain Selection via Second-Order Pole Placement

Per joint, the characteristic polynomial is:

$$s^2 + K_{v,i} s + K_{p,i} = 0$$

Compare with the canonical second-order system $s^2 + 2\zeta\omega_n s + \omega_n^2 = 0$:

$$K_{p,i} = \omega_{n,i}^2 \qquad K_{v,i} = 2\zeta_i \omega_{n,i}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Shortcut:** For critically damped response, pick $\zeta_i = 1$ and any $\omega_{n,i} > 0$ (e.g. $\omega_n = 10$ rad/s gives a 0.1 s settling time, which is a typical KTU numerical answer).

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Effect of Model Uncertainty

In practice the *estimated* model $\hat{M}, \hat{C}, \hat{G}, \hat{F}$ differs from the true one. The torque applied is:

$$\tau = \hat{M}(q)\,a + \hat{h}(q,\dot{q})$$

Substituting into the true dynamics:

$$M(q)\ddot{q} + h(q,\dot{q}) = \hat{M}(q)\,a + \hat{h}(q,\dot{q})$$

$$\ddot{q} = a + \hat{M}^{-1}(q)\bigl[\,h(q,\dot{q}) - \hat{h}(q,\dot{q}) + (M(q) - \hat{M}(q))\,a\,\bigr]$$

$$\ddot{e} + K_v \dot{e} + K_p e = \eta(q,\dot{q},a)$$

where $\eta$ is the **disturbance** due to modelling error. The PD part *still* stabilises the system provided $\eta$ is bounded — this is the motivation for **robust / adaptive extensions** studied in Module 5.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Design Equations for the 2-DOF Planar Arm

Let the 2-DOF planar arm have:

$$M(q) = \begin{bmatrix} m_{11} & m_{12} \\ m_{12} & m_{22} \end{bmatrix},\qquad h(q,\dot{q}) = \begin{bmatrix} h_1 \\ h_2 \end{bmatrix}$$

The CTC torques become:

$$\tau_1 = m_{11}(\ddot{q}_{d,1} + K_{v,1}\dot{e}_1 + K_{p,1}e_1) + m_{12}(\ddot{q}_{d,2} + K_{v,2}\dot{e}_2 + K_{p,2}e_2) + h_1$$

$$\tau_2 = m_{12}(\ddot{q}_{d,1} + K_{v,1}\dot{e}_1 + K_{p,1}e_1) + m_{22}(\ddot{q}_{d,2} + K_{v,2}\dot{e}_2 + K_{p,2}e_2) + h_2$$

> [!TIP]
> Notice the **cross-coupling terms** $m_{12}$ — CTC naturally accounts for dynamic coupling, which a simple joint-independent PD controller cannot.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Computed Torque Control Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    refQ["Desired Trajectory qd, qdotd, qddotd"]
    subErr["Error Computation: e = qd - q, edot = qdotd - qdot"]
    auxAcc["Auxiliary Acceleration: a = qddotd + Kv edot + Kp e"]
    dynModel["Robot Dynamic Model M of q, h of q, qdot"]
    torqueLaw["Computed Torque: tau = M times a + h"]
    robot["Robot Plant: M qddot + h = tau"]
    sensor["Encoders: measure q and qdot"]
    actQ["Actual Joint Position q and Velocity qdot"]

    refQ --> subErr
    subErr --> auxAcc
    auxAcc --> torqueLaw
    dynModel --> torqueLaw
    torqueLaw --> robot
    robot --> actQ
    actQ --> sensor
    sensor --> subErr

    dynModel -.->|"Inverted and Pre-Computed in Real Time"| torqueLaw
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The *outer loop* (red arrows conceptually) computes tracking error.
- The *inner loop* injects a reference acceleration $a$ that includes the desired trajectory **plus** a PD correction.
- The *dynamic model* is used **twice**: once to compute the *inverse* (left block) and once implicitly inside the plant (right block). The two cancel in the closed loop.

---

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Closed-Loop Equivalent (After Cancellation)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    refSig["qd"]
    sumNode(("Sum"))
    errSig["e = qd - q"]
    kpBlk["Kp Proportional Gain"]
    kvBlk["Kv Derivative Gain on edot"]
    doubleInt["Double Integrator 1 over s squared"]
    outSig["q"]
    sum2(("Sum: a = qddotd + Kv edot + Kp e"))

    refSig --> sumNode
    outSig --> sumNode
    sumNode --> errSig
    errSig --> kpBlk
    errSig --> kvBlk
    kpBlk --> sum2
    kvBlk --> sum2
    refSig --> sum2
    sum2 --> doubleInt
    doubleInt --> outSig
```

**Reading the simplified diagram:**
- After the model-based cancellation, the *entire nonlinear plant collapses* to **n independent linear double integrators**, each driven by its own PD servo.
- The system behaves exactly like a classical second-order position servo — which is the *entire point* of computed torque control.

---

### 4.3 Information-Flow Summary (Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    s1["Step 1: Trajectory Planner outputs qd, qdotd, qddotd"]
    s2["Step 2: Read current q and qdot from joint encoders"]
    s3["Step 3: Compute e and edot"]
    s4["Step 4: Form reference acceleration a = qddotd + Kv edot + Kp e"]
    s5["Step 5: Compute nonlinear terms h and M from current q and qdot"]
    s6["Step 6: Solve tau = M a + h in real time"]
    s7["Step 7: Send tau to joint motor amplifiers"]
    s8["Step 8: Robot moves; loop repeats at sample rate 1 kHz to 10 kHz"]

    s1 --> s2 --> s3 --> s4 --> s5 --> s6 --> s7 --> s8 --> s2
```
